text:
FLUOROSCOPIC STEROID INJECTION INTO THE LEFT KNEE

CLINICAL INDICATION:  ___ female with left knee osteoarthritis.

COMPARISON:  Left knee radiography dated ___.

TECHNIQUE:

The risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure were explained to the
patient, and written informed consent was obtained.  A preprocedural timeout
confirmed the patient identity and site and side of the procedure to be
performed.  The patient was placed supine on the fluoroscopic table and an
appropriate spot marked.  The area was prepped and draped in standard sterile
fashion.  1% lidocaine was used to anesthetize the skin and subcutaneous
tissues.  Under intermittent fluoroscopic guidance, a 20-gauge spinal needle
was advanced into the left knee joint and appropriate position confirmed by
injection of Optiray 320 contrast material.  Then, solution containing 80 mg
of Kenalog and 2 mL of 0.25% bupivacaine was injected into the left knee
joint.  The needle was removed, hemostasis achieved, and a sterile dry
dressing applied.

The patient tolerated the procedure well, and there were no immediate
complications.  The patient left the department in good condition.  The
patient experienced mild relief of pain after the procedure.

IMAGING FINDINGS:  Radiopaque contrast within the left knee joint as well as
within the subcutaneous tissues and tracking along the extensor mechanism. 
Tricompartmental degenerative change is present with spurring and joint space
loss, most severe within the medial compartment.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Status post fluoroscopically-guided therapeutic injection of Kenalog and
bupivacaine into the left knee joint.
2.  Tricompartmental degenerative change as detailed above.

The procedure was supervised by Dr. ___ attending radiologist.

EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PORTABLE AP)

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with acute dyspnea.  // acute change in
respiratory status      acute change in respiratory status

IMPRESSION: 

In comparison with the study of ___, the endotracheal tube and left
subclavian catheter are in satisfactory position.  Cardiac silhouette is again
mildly enlarged with some indistinctness of pulmonary vessels that could
reflect some elevation in pulmonary venous pressure.  Opacification at the
bases silhouetting the left hemidiaphragm could reflect small pleural effusion
and underlying atelectatic changes.  However, in the appropriate clinical
setting, superimposed pneumonia would have to be considered.

CLINICAL HISTORY  ___ year old woman with known aneurysm.  // Pipeline
embolization of brain aneurysm.**Dr. ___ Anes booked 2:30p, ___

EXAMINATION:  Patient has multiple aneurysms along with a left parietal
temporal AVM. She presented here for a pipeline embolization treatment of her
right para ophthalmic aneurysms.

The following vessels were selectively catheterize injected: Left common
carotid artery, left internal carotid artery, right common carotid artery,
right internal carotid artery including Three dimensional rotational
angiography and postprocessing on separate work station with concurrent
physician supervision with images being used for final interpretation, right
femoral artery.
ANESTHESIA:  ANESTHESIA:  General endotracheal anesthesia was provided by
separate anesthesia provider throughout the entirety of the case. They monitor
the patient's hemodynamic and respiratory parameters.

TECHNIQUE:  OPERATORS: Dr. ___, Dr. ___ Dr. ___
physician performed the procedure. Dr. ___ supervised the
trainee during the key components of the procedure and has reviewed and agrees
with the trainee's findings.

PROCEDURE:  The patient was brought into the Angiography suite, ID was
confirmed via wrist band. The patient previously had been maintained on
full-dose aspirin and full dose Plavix in the week leading up to this
procedure. General endotracheal anesthesia was induced by a separate
anesthesia provider. An arterial line was then placed. Patient's bilateral
groins were prepped and draped in usual sterile manner. The mid femoral head
on the right was located using anatomic and radiographic landmarks.
Micropuncture kit was used to gain access to the right femoral artery, and
serial dilation was undertaken until a 8 ___ groin sheath to be inserted
under direct fluoroscopic visualization.  ___ catheter was
advanced over the 0.038 glidewire brought up the aorta used to select the left
common carotid artery.  Cervical biplane road mapping was undertaken.  Next,
the left internal carotid artery was selected under road mapping technique in
multiple planes in the intracranial biplane and magnified biplane oblique
views were undertaken from this vessel. Then pulled back into the aorta in
used to select the right innominate followed by the right common carotid
artery. Cervical biplane road map imaging was undertaken. Under road mapping
technique, the right internal carotid artery was then selected and multiple
views of the intracranial biplane, 3 dimensional rotational angiography was
separate processing a separate 3D workstation for attending physician
supervision of the images for final interpretation an use of best working
projection angles was undertaken.

5000 units of heparin were then administered via IV, intermittent ACT levels
were checked to maintain a therapeutic range. Next new road map was undertaken
exchanged for a ___ shuttle catheter was undertaken. This was
brought into the proximal portion of the right internal carotid artery. New
road map imaging was undertaken are best working projection angles and
magnified biplane oblique. A ___ ___ catheter was connected to a continuous
heparinized saline flush into this, a Marksman catheter along with a Synchro 2
micro wire were inserted. The entirety of this assembly was brought up the
guide catheter bone and under direct fluoroscopic visualization, and was used
to select the distal M1 on the right. Following this, the guidewire was
removed and a Pipeline flex 4 mm x 18 mm device was brought through the micro
catheter, the optimal distal zone was selected, and it was deployed just
distal to the aneurysms and brought through the cavernous portion of the right
ICA. Afterwards followup angiography in the magnified biplane oblique views
demonstrated concern for an area of non wall apposition without evidence of
thrombus. Next exchange length Synchro 2 scanner wire was used to exchange out
the marks the micro catheter and a Scepter 4 mm x 20 mm balloon was prepped in
the usual manner and brought up in exchanged in inflated with in the biplane
embolization device. This resulted in improved wall apposition of the mid
distal section of the pipe line.

The Scepter ___ balloon was removed, and followup angiography in the
intracranial biplane and magnified biplane oblique views were undertaken. The
guide catheter was then pulled back into the aorta under direct fluoroscopic
visualization gentle puffs of contrast. Then finally removed from the body.
___ projection the right femoral artery was undertaken, patient had very
diminutive size femoral artery and this decision was made to leave the groin
sheath in place temporarily until a to be removed under manual pressure in a
delayed fashion given the patients recent heparinization and dual antiplatelet
status.

At the conclusion the procedure, the patient was open, an extubated was noted
to be at her neurologic baseline. She has transfer turn ICU bed and brought to
the PACU for further convalescence.

FINDINGS: 

Left common carotid artery:  The cervical bifurcation is best seen on the AP
projection, as previously noted, the patient has extremely low bifurcation
around the T1 level. There is no evidence of vessel spasm or dissection. There
is no significant vessel tortuosity.

Left internal carotid artery: The distal left ICA, proximal and distal MCA
branches are well-visualized. The patient does not have significant filling of
the left ACA distribution secondary to a hypoplastic A1.  Again in the distal
parietal temporal region there is blush of a AVM with early venous drainage
into the right transverse sigmoid sinus.  This appears to be supply from a
branch of the right MCA. Also identified, is infundibulum from which the left
ophthalmic artery arises. This is not changed significantly from the prior
angiogram.

Right common carotid artery: The cervical bifurcation is well visualized,
there is no significant vessel tortuosity or stenosis noted.

Right internal carotid artery:  The distal right ICA, proximal distal MCA and
ACA branches are well-visualized. Vessel caliber within the distal cervical
Peters region does have some mild irregularity, this is stable compared to the
prior angiograms and likely represents pulse jetting from the power injector
itself.  The contralateral ACA circulation is also seen to fill from this
right-sided injection secondary to a patent A-comm.  Within the para
ophthalmic region, 2 ophthalmic region aneurysms are seen, along with a
cavernous portion aneurysm from the left ICA.  No other aneurysms are
identified.

The next series of images, best working projections have been obtained in the
magnified biplane oblique views. The ___ intermediate catheter can clearly
be seen.  After deployment of the Pipeline flex post embolization injection
demonstrates patency and filling of the distal ICA, ACA, MCA vasculature.
There is no evidence of thrombus formation within the stent itself. There is a
region within the carotid siphon where there is not appear to be full vessel
wall apposition. After deployment of the septa balloon within this region of
the Pipeline flexed, followup injection demonstrates improved wall apposition.
For final review of the 3 dimensional rotational angiography with processing
on a separate 3D workstation the dual volume motor demonstrate that this
likely actually represents the vessel wall and takeoff of the cavernous
aneurysm. There appears to be good vessel wall apposition at both the proximal
and distal portions of the pipe line flex device. There is no evidence of
vessel cut off or other thromboembolic complication.

Right femoral artery: Very diminutive in size, there is no significant
tortuosity. Given that the 8 ___ sheath essentially is the diameter of this
vessel, decision was made to apply manual pressure in a delayed fashion for
hemostasis.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Successful deployment of a pipe plane flex device in the right internal
carotid artery to cover 2 para ophthalmic and 1 cavernous segment aneurysm. 
This was dictated with slight angioplasty to improve vessel wall apposition
within the carotid siphon.

2.  Left parietal temporal AVM, either ___ grade 2 versus 3. This
is unchanged in size compared to the prior angiogram.

3. Left para ophthalmic infundibulum previously seen on angiography.

Dr. ___ was personally present, supervised common participated
in all key portions of the case. ___ has reviewed the above images and agrees
with the findings as stated above.

text:
EXAMINATION:  PELVIS U.S., TRANSVAGINAL

INDICATION:  History: ___ with hx of chronic abdominal pain ___ years, chief
complaint of acute worsening of abdominal pain, heavy vaginal bleeding,
vaginal pain.  // Pelvic pathology?  Uterine pathology?  Ovarian pathology?

TECHNIQUE:  Grayscale ultrasound images of the pelvis were obtained with
transabdominal approach followed by transvaginal approach for further
delineation of uterine and ovarian anatomy.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

The uterus is anteverted and measures 8.9 x 4.1 x 6.6 cm.  The myometrium is
somewhat heterogeneous though without discrete lesion.  The endometrium is
homogenous and measures 8 mm.

The ovaries are normal.  There is no free fluid.

IMPRESSION: 

Heterogeneous appearance of the myometrium which raises the possibility of
adenomyosis.

text:
INDICATION:  PEG pulled at rehab, replacement with Foley catheter.

TECHNIQUE:  2 supine abdominal films of the abdomen were obtained after the
instillation of Gastrografin contrast through the Foley catheter.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

Intraluminal contrast is seen within the stomach.  There is also contrast
outlining the catheter external to the patient in the region of the umbilicus.
Additional densities projecting over the right abdomen including
irregular-shaped densities over the mid abdomen and significant contrast
lateral to the iliac wing are external given their configuration. 
Calcifications project over the right side of the abdomen and pelvis.

IMPRESSION: 

Endoluminal contrast seen within the stomach.  No visualized intra-abdominal,
extraluminal contrast.

text:
MRI OF THE LUMBAR SPINE:

CLINICAL INDICATION:  ___ female patient with DM, lumbar back pain,
compression deformity at L4, rule out pathological fracture.

COMPARISON:  No prior MRIs of the lumbar spine are available for comparison. 

TECHNIQUE:  Sagittal T1 and T2 images were performed, multi-axial T1 and T2
images were obtained.

FINDINGS:  The signal intensity in the vertebral bodies is grossly normal, the
bone marrow at S1 demonstrates a focal area of hyperintensity on T1 and T2,
likely consistent with fat.  

The intervertebral disc space at T12/L1 demonstrates disc desiccation, with
posterior disc bulge producing anterior thecal sac deformity, no significant
neuroforaminal narrowing is identified at this level. 

The intervertebral disc space at L1/L2 demonstrates disc desiccation, no
significant spinal canal stenosis or neuroforaminal narrowing was noted.

L2/L3 demonstrates bilateral joint facet hypertrophy without evidence of
significant neuroforaminal narrowing or spinal canal stenosis.

At L3/L4, there is evidence of disc desiccation, posterior broad-based disc
bulge producing bilateral neuroforaminal narrowing, more evident on the left
side with possible nerve compression.  In addition, there is also bilateral
articular joint facet hypertrophy as well as ligamentum flavum thickening
producing moderate-to-severe spinal canal stenosis.  The vertebral body at L4
demonstrates compression deformity with loss of the vertebral height
anteriorly involving the inferior endplate, there is no evidence of
significant ___- or retro-listhesis.  

Intervertebral disc space at L4/L5 demonstrates disc desiccation, posterior
broad-based disc protrusion, bilateral vertebral joint facet hypertrophy as
well as ligamentum flavum thickening producing significant spinal canal
stenosis with possible nerve root compression bilaterally, the thecal sac in
the anterior posterior diameter measures approximately 5 mm in size,
subchondral cystic formations are noted in the articular facets and the
endplate changes in the anterior aspect of the L4 and superior endplate of L5
may represent a Schmorl's node.  

The intervertebral disc space at L5/S1 demonstrates disc desiccation, minimal
posterior disc bulge producing mild bilateral neuroforaminal narrowing.  In
addition, there is bilateral articular joint facet hypertrophy as well as
mild-to-moderate ligamentum flavum thickening bilaterally, moderate spinal
canal stenosis is identified at this level.  

The sacroiliac joints appear normal.  The visualized aspect of the
retroperitoneum and prevertebral sutures are unremarkable.  

IMPRESSION:  Multilevel degenerative changes of the lumbar spine as described
above.  

At L3/L4, there is significant spinal canal stenosis associated with
degenerative changes consistent with articular facet hypertrophy, ligamentum
flavum thickening, subchondral cystic formations on the right.  

At L4/L5, there is disc desiccation, degenerative changes consistent with
articular joint facet hypertrophy, ligamentum flavum thickening producing
severe spinal canal stenosis as described above.

The vertebral body at L4 demonstrates anterior compression deformity and
endplate changes likely consistent with a Schmorl's node. 

L5/S1 demonstrates disc desiccation, mild posterior diffuse disc bulge as well
as moderate articular joint facet hypertrophy and ligamentum flavum producing
mild-to-moderate spinal canal stenosis as described above.

INDICATION:  Right intraparenchymal hemorrhage, transfer from outside
hospital.

COMPARISON:  CTA from ___, and MRI/MRA from same day.

TECHNIQUE:  Non-contrast head CT.

FINDINGS:  There is a 3.3 x 2.8 cm lobulated area of hyperdensity in the right
parietal lobe (3, 19), consistent with intraparenchymal hemorrhage.  There is
mild surrounding hypodensity consistent with edema, and mild mass effect on
adjacent sulci and occipital horn of the right lateral ventricle. There are
scattered foci of subarachnoid hemorrhage in the right parietal sulci, and
similar sulcal hyperdensity is also seen in the left parietal lobe, also
consistent with subarachnoid hemorrhage.  There is a small amount of
intraventricular blood layering in the bilateral occipital horns.

There is no shift of normally midline structures.  Basal cisterns are not
effaced.  Calcifications in the bilateral basal ganglia are stable.  There is
no sign of acute vascular territorial infarction.  Visualized osseous
structures are unremarkable.  Partial opacification of the left maxillary
sinus is not significantly changed.  Visualized paranasal sinuses are
otherwise normally aerated.

IMPRESSION:

3.3 cm area of right parietal intraparenchymal hemorrhage, with mild edema and
mass effect, small foci of associated subarachnoid hemorrhage, and
intraventricular blood.  While this appearance could result from hypertensive
hemorrhage with intraventricular extension, the recurrent bleeding raises
concern for a possible underlying etiology for hemorrhage, and despite prior
negative workup, reinvestigation should be considered.

mGy-cm.

11.8 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 555 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: The partially imaged lower lungs are clear.  No evidence of a
pleural effusion.  Trace pericardial fluid is likely physiologic.

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver is unremarkable.  No biliary ductal dilatation
demonstrated.  Mild prominence of the CBD is unchanged.  The gallbladder is
within normal limits.

PANCREAS: The pancreas is unremarkable.  An 1.7-cm elongated hyperdense mass
embedded in the jejunal loops in the left upper abdomen with similar
attenuation to the pancreas and could be an ectopic pancreas (series 2, image
29).

SPLEEN: Unremarkable.

ADRENALS: Unremarkable.

URINARY: The kidneys are unremarkable except for a minute hypodensity in the
right renal cortex is too small to accurately characterize on CT (series 2,

collection.

PELVIS: No free fluid in the pelvis.  The uterus and adnexa are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  No pelvic or
inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: A few periuterine varices are seen bilaterally, more so on the left
side contiguous with a prominent left gonadal vein.

BONES: No evidence of worrisome osseous lesions.  There is a prominent
anterior osteophyte at T12.  The hepatic vasculature is patent.

SOFT TISSUES: The patient appears to have a belly button ring which creates
streak artifact in the abdomen at this level.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  No CT evidence to explain the etiology of the patient's pain.

2.  1.7-cm elongated hyperdense structure in the left upper abdomen most
likely representing ectopic pancreas.  Consider non-emergent outpatient MRI
for confirmation.
3.  Prominent periuterine varices, greater on the left which could be
correlated for symptoms of pelvic congestion syndrome.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Consider non-emergent outpatient MRI without and with
contrast for confirmation of the ectopic pancreas.

text:
INDICATION:  ___ year old man with segment VIII HCC and tumor thrombus in the
right lobe.  Evaluation for Y90 treatment.

COMPARISON:  MRI liver dated ___

TECHNIQUE:  OPERATORS: Dr. ___ and Dr.
___ radiologist performed the procedure. Dr. ___
___ supervised the trainee during the key components of the procedure
and has reviewed and agrees with the trainee's findings.
ANESTHESIA: Moderate sedation was provided by administrating divided doses of
0 mcg of fentanyl, 1 mg of midazolam and 0.25 mg of Dilaudid throughout the
total intra-service time of 50 minutes during which the patient's hemodynamic
parameters were continuously monitored by an independent trained radiology
nurse. 1% lidocaine was injected in the skin and subcutaneous tissues
overlying the access site
MEDICATIONS: As above.
CONTRAST: 21 ml of Optiray contrast.
FLUOROSCOPY TIME AND DOSE:  11.8 min, 405 mGy

PROCEDURE:
1. Right common femoral artery access
2. Common hepatic arteriogram
3. Right hepatic arteriogram
4. Right hepatic arteriogram with cone beam CT
5. Right hepatic arteriogram from treatment position
6. Injection of 4 mCi of Technetium ___
PROCEDURE DETAILS:
Following the discussion of the risks, benefits and alternatives to the
procedure, written informed consent was obtained from the patient. The patient
was then brought to the angiography suite and placed supine on the exam table.
A pre-procedure time-out was performed per ___ protocol.  The right groin
was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.

Using ultrasound guidance, the right common femoral artery was accessed using
a micropuncture needle after injection of 1% subcutaneous lidocaine.  Images
were not saved to PACS.  A .018 Nitinol wire was easily advanced into the
right common iliac artery.  After skin ___ the micropuncture needle was
exchanged for a micropuncture sheath.  The wire and inner dilator were
removed.  A ___ wire was carefully advanced through the micropuncture
sheath into the abdominal aorta.  The micropuncture sheath was exchanged for a
___ x 10 cm sheath.  The sheath was aspirated and flushed and connected to a
pressurized bag of non heparinized saline.

A C2 Cobra catheter was advanced over the ___ wire into the abdominal
aorta.  The wire was removed and the catheter was used to selectively
catheterize the superior mesenteric artery.  A very small hand contrast
injection confirmed placement within the superior mesenteric artery.  An ___
microcatheter pre loaded with a double angled Glidewire was advanced through
the catheter and used to selectively catheterize the completely replaced
common hepatic artery originating from the superior mesenteric artery.  The
wire was removed and a common hepatic arteriogram was then performed.

The wire was readvanced through the microcatheter and used to selectively
catheterize the right hepatic artery.  The wire was removed and a small hand
contrast injection confirmed placement.  A right hepatic arteriogram was then
performed to confirm positioned.

Next, a cone beam CT right hepatic arteriogram was performed. Rotational
cone-beam CT angiography was performed to help delineate the anatomy. 
Multiplanar CT images were reconstructed and 3D volume-rendered images of the
arterial anatomy required post-processing on an independent workstation under
direct physician ___ (Dr. ___.  These images were used in the
interpretation, decision making for intervention and reporting of this
procedure.  Following the cone beam CT, the microcatheter was slightly
advanced.  A small hand contrast injection confirmed placement.  A repeat
right hepatic arteriogram to confirm treatment position was performed.

At this point nuclear medicine brought 4 mCi of technetium ___ M to the ___
suite.  The dose and patient identifying information was confirmed using 2
distinct patient identifiers.  The dose was then received an injected into the
right hepatic artery at the treatment position.

The catheter and microcatheter were then removed from the sheath using
radiation safety precautions.  A preliminary radiation safety check was
performed via the representative of the radiation safety office.

A right common femoral arteriogram using the sheath was then performed. 
Angio-Seal closure of the right common femoral arteriotomy was then performed.
Manual pressure for 10 minutes over the right groin was maintained to ensure
hemostasis.  Sterile dressings were applied. The patient tolerated the
procedure well and was transported on a stretcher to nuclear medicine for
scintigraphy study to determine the lung shunt fraction.

FINDINGS:


1. Variant anatomy with a completely replaced common hepatic artery
originating from the superior mesenteric artery.  The proper hepatic artery
trifurcates into the left, middle and right hepatic arteries.
2. No definitive enhancing tumor was identified.
3. Technically successful arterial mapping of the right hepatic lobe

IMPRESSION: 

Planning arteriography for Yttrium 90 radioembolization as above. Results of
pulmonary scintigraphy will be reported separately.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  The patient is scheduled for a yttrium 90
radioembolization treatment arteriography in the near future.

INDICATION:  ___ woman with history of renal stones presents with
right lower quadrant pain, assess for renal stone size.

COMPARISONS:  CT from ___ and renal ultrasound from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT-acquired axial images were obtained from the lung bases to
the pubic symphysis in the prone position without intravenous or oral
contrast.  Coronal and sagittal reformations were prepared.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH CONTRAST:  Imaged lung bases are clear without pleural
or pericardial effusion.  Epicardial lymph node is not enlarged and unchanged.

Please note, assessment of solid visceral organs in the abdomen is limited in
the absence of intravenous contrast.  The liver is normal in attenuation
without focal lesion.  The gallbladder, pancreas, spleen, and bilateral
adrenal glands are normal.  The kidneys are normal without stones. The right
sided hydronephrosis has essentially resolved.  Stomach, small and large
bowel, and appendix are unremarkable.  There is no free air or free fluid in
the abdomen.  There is no pathologic mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymph node
enlargement, with unchanged nonenlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes measuring
up to 8mm.  No aortic calcifications are seen.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:  Uterus, adnexa, and rectum are normal. A 4 x
4 x 3 mm distal right ureteral stone is seen. There is no free pelvic fluid or
lymphadenopathy.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  There is no lytic or sclerotic bony lesion to suggest
osseous malignancy.

IMPRESSION:  4 mm stone at the right UVJ.

text:
INDICATION:  Neck pain and dysphagia with question of retropharyngeal abscess.

COMPARISON:  CT of the torso from the same date as well as an MR of the
cervical spine provided for reference from ___ dated
___.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial CT images were acquired through the neck following
administration of 70 cc of intravenous Optiray contrast.  Coronal and sagittal
reformatted images were also reviewed.

FINDINGS:  As was seen to better effect on the comparison MRI from one day
prior, there is a large amount of soft tissue thickening in the prevertebral
space, which is primarily iso- and slightly hypo-dense on CT.  There is no
evidence of frank fluid or of an organized collection.  Epidural involvement
of disease was seen to better effect on the comparison MRI.  Regional vascular
structures opacify normally.  There are multiple cervical lymph nodes seen
bilaterally, none of which appear enlarged by CT size criteria.  Imaged
intracranial contents are normal.  Imaged portions of the lung apices were
seen to better effect on the comparison CT.  There is no fracture or
dislocation and there are no suspicious sclerotic or lytic osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:  Large amount of primarily isodense prevertebral soft tissue
thickening, likely phlegmon, seen to better effect on the MR from one day
prior.  There is no evidence of organized fluid collection or abscess.

NOTES ON ATTENDING REVIEW:

1. The ___ MRI demonstrates bilateral longus ___ abscesses, which are
not well visualized on this CT scan due to differences in technique.  In the
right longus ___, there is an abscess at the level of C5-C6, measuring 0.6
cm in diameter in maximal axial cross-section, and 1.9 cm craniocaudad.  In
the left longus ___, there are small discontinuous abscesses at the levels
of C4 through C7, measuring up to 1 cm in diameter in maximal axial
cross-section.  This was discussed with Dr. ___ at 8:30 am on
___.

2. There is disc space narrowing and endplate osteophytes at C6-7, consistent
with chronic spondylosis.  The ___ MRI demonstrates an epidural abscess
centered at C6-7, but no evidence for discitis or osteomyelitis.  No evidence
of endplate erosion is seen on the current study.  However, a follow-up MRI
may be helpful to reassess for discitis/osteomyelitis, since early discitis
may be occult on MRI.

3. A 1.9 cm right level 2A lymph node (2:52) is enlarged but maintains a
normal fatty hilus.  There are numerous other cervical lymph nodes
bilaterally, not pathologically enlarged by CT criteria.  These are likely
reactive.

text:
EXAMINATION:  CT ABD AND PELVIS W AND W/O CONTRAST, ADDL SECTIONS

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with cirrhosis and portal hypertension with
reccurent GI bleeds, thrombus  // Please evaluate for the presence of portal
hypertensive varices in the wall of the stomach or in the wall, approximating
the small bowel.

TECHNIQUE:  Multiphasic Liver: Multidetector CT of the abdomen was done
without and with IV contrast. Initially, the abdomen was scanned without IV
contrast. Subsequently, a single bolus of IV contrast was injected and the
abdomen was scanned in the early arterial phase, followed by a scan of the
abdomen and pelvis in the portal venous phase, followed by a scan of the
abdomen in equilibrium phase (3-min delay).
Oral contrast was administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 4.4 s, 28.4 cm; CTDIvol = 5.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 154.3
mGy-cm.
   2) Sequenced Acquisition 0.5 s, 0.2 cm; CTDIvol = 18.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 3.6
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 6.9 s, 0.2 cm; CTDIvol = 112.7 mGy (Body) DLP =
22.5 mGy-cm.
   4) Spiral Acquisition 3.1 s, 19.9 cm; CTDIvol = 10.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 199.2
mGy-cm.
   5) Spiral Acquisition 7.1 s, 46.3 cm; CTDIvol = 11.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 531.5
mGy-cm.
   6) Spiral Acquisition 3.1 s, 19.9 cm; CTDIvol = 10.3 mGy (Body) DLP = 199.2
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,110 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen and pelvis ___ and ___, and
MR abdomen ___

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Visualized lung fields are within normal limits.  There is no
evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: Cirrhotic liver morphology is redemonstrated.  Allowing for
differences in technique, a heterogeneous area of enhancement along the
anterior right hepatic lobe (8:22) in proximity to the falciform ligament
appears similar to the prior study.  Few arterially hyperenhancing foci about
the periphery of the right hepatic lobe are without correlate on portal venous
or delayed phases and are likely perfusional.  Specifically, the largest
arterially hyperenhancing focus near the right hepatic dome (6:15) appears
similar in size and location to the arterially hyperenhancing focus seen on
the MRI in ___.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic
biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is within normal limits.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, pancreatic ductal
dilatation.  An ovoid lesion at the tip of the pancreatic tail measuring 2.6 x
1.5 cm (8:36) is unchanged from prior, previously 2.5 x 1.4 cm, and likely
represents a folded, redundant portion of the tail the pancreas versus less
likely splenosis. An additional smaller ovoid lesion medially (8:35) is also
unchanged and may represent a splenule.  There is no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN: Status post splenectomy.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no evidence of solid renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout. The colon and
rectum are unremarkable.  The appendix is normal.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: Fibroid uterus.  No adnexal abnormality.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: Chronic partially occlusive thrombus extending from portal vein/SMV
confluence to the right intrahepatic portal veins appears similar compared to
prior but markedly improved since ___.  The splenic vein is
diminutive status post splenectomy.

There are mild paraesophageal varices (8:15) and possible mild esophageal
varices (8:17).  Additionally, there are mild gastric varices.  Evaluation for
GI tract varices is limited in the presence of positive oral contrast.

There is new thrombus within the right gonadal vein (8:73).

There is a normal variant replaced right hepatic artery.  There is no
abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Mild atherosclerotic disease is noted.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture. 
There is lumbarization of the S1 vertebra.  Limbus vertebra is again seen at
the L4 level.

SOFT TISSUES: There is a small bowel containing umbilical hernia with neck
measuring approximately 1.4 cm (8:71).

IMPRESSION:


1. New nearly occlusive right gonadal vein thrombus.
2. Chronic partially occlusive thrombus extending from the portal vein/SMV
confluence to the right intrahepatic portal vein branches appears similar to
prior.
3. Mild paraesophageal and gastric varices with possible mild esophageal
varices.  It is noted that evaluation for gastrointestinal tract varices is
moderately limited in the presence of positive oral contrast which may obscure
subtle wall enhancement abnormalities, but no additional large varices are
seen.
4. Small bowel containing umbilical hernia.

NOTIFICATION:  The impression and recommendation above was entered by Dr.
___ on ___ at 11:57 into the Department of Radiology critical
communications system for direct communication to the referring provider.

HISTORY:  ___ female with right lower extremity numbness and weakness.

TECHNIQUE:  Multiplanar CTA of the head and neck was performed following
administration of intravenous contrast.  Additional 3D reconstructed images of
the intra- and extra-cranial arterial vasculature were performed.

COMPARISON:  Non-contrast CT head, ___ and ___.

FINDINGS:

CT HEAD:  There is no intracranial hemorrhage, mass effect, or gray-white
matter differentiation abnormality.  The ventricles and extra-axial spaces are
appropriate for age.  There is a tiny mucus retention cyst in a posterior left
ethmoid air cell and minimal mucosal thickening within the left maxillary
sinus.  The remaining paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are well
aerated.

CTA HEAD: There is punctate calcification within the V4 segment of the left
vertebral artery.  The left vertebral artery is dominant.  There is a
fetal-type right PCA with a robust right PCOM and a diminutive right P1
segment.  There is a tiny infundibulum at the origin of the right PCOM. The
left PCOM is diminutive.  There is atherosclerotic calcification of the
cavernous internal carotid arteries without flow-limiting stenosis.  The
remaining intracranial vertebrobasilar system, internal carotid, anterior,
middle, and posterior cerebral arteries are normal in course and caliber
without evidence of occlusion, flow-limiting stenosis, aneurysm greater than 2
mm, or arteriovenous malformation.

CTA NECK:  Incidental note is made of a bovine arch, a normal variant.  There
is dense atherosclerotic calcification of the aortic arch and origins of the
great vessels without hemodynamically significant narrowing.

There is atherosclerotic calcification at the origin of the left vertebral
artery without hemodynamically significant stenosis.  The right vertebral
artery origin is patent.  The left vertebral artery is dominant.  The
vertebral arteries are normal in course and caliber without evidence of
flow-limiting stenosis, occlusion, or dissection.

The right common carotid artery is markedly tortuous and demonstrates
circumferential atherosclerotic calcification at its bulb without
hemodynamically significant stenosis.  There is a punctate calcification
involving the distal left common carotid artery and the left carotid bulb
without hemodynamically significant stenosis.  The remaining extracranial
common carotid, internal, and external carotid arteries are normal in course
and caliber without evidence of occlusion, flow-limiting stenosis, or
dissection.

Cross-sectional analysis of the internal carotid arteries is as follows:
RIGHT:  Proximal Dmin. 4.5 mm; distal Dmin. 4.4 mm.
LEFT:  Proximal Dmin. 4.5 mm; distal Dmin. 3.9 mm.  

There is a 1.5 cm left thyroid nodule.  The airway is patent.  There is
biapical pleural scarring and emphysematous changes.  There are multilevel
spondylotic degenerative changes of the cervical spine, most pronounced at
C6-7.  There is no evidence of osseous, lytic or blastic lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1.  No acute hemorrhage.  No evidence of an acute major vascular territorial
infarction.  MRI would be more sensitive for an acute infarction.
2.  No evidence of occlusion, flow-limiting stenosis, aneurysm greater than 2
mm, or arteriovenous malformation involving the intra or extracranial arterial
vasculature.
3.  1.5 cm left thyroid nodule.  Recommend thyroid ultrasound for further
evaluation, if not already performed previously.

hematoma.  Mild central intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation is noted with
dilation of the common bile duct. This appears stable compared to the prior
MRCP.  The distal CBD tapers as expected without evidence of focal lesions.

or ureters. There is no perinephric abnormality.

duodenum and the pancreatic head.  The remaining small bowel loops demonstrate

with mild pancreatitis after ERCP.

3. Stable CBD and intrahepatic ductal dilation.
4. Stable hepatic and pancreatic fluid collections, previously characterized
as chronic hematomas.

text:
INDICATION:  ___ year old man with indwelling left tunneled dialysis line and
facial swelling, evaluate for SVC occlusion.

COMPARISON:  None.

TECHNIQUE:  OPERATORS: Dr. ___, Interventional Radiology Fellow and Dr.
___, attending radiologist performed the procedure. Dr. ___
___ personally supervised the trainee during the key components of the
procedure and has reviewed and agrees with the trainee's findings.
ANESTHESIA: Moderate sedation was provided by administrating divided doses of
0 mcg of fentanyl and 1 mg of midazolam throughout the total intra-service
time of 29 minutes during which the patient's hemodynamic parameters were
continuously monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse. 1% lidocaine
was injected in the skin and subcutaneous tissues overlying the access site
MEDICATIONS: Versed
CONTRAST: 20 ml of Optiray contrast.
FLUOROSCOPY TIME AND DOSE:  1.3 min, 60 see mGy

PROCEDURE:
1. Ultrasound-guided left internal jugular vein access with a micropuncture
set
2. Left brachiocephalic and SVC venogram
PROCEDURE DETAILS:
Following the discussion of the risks, benefits and alternatives to the
procedure, written informed consent was obtained from the patient. The patient
was then brought to the angiography suite and placed supine on the exam table.
A pre-procedure time-out was performed per ___ protocol.

The bilateral necks were prepped and draped in standard fashion.  Ultrasound
was performed of the right internal jugular and left internal jugular veins. 
Images were stored to PACS.  Under ultrasound guidance, a micropuncture needle
was advanced into the left internal jugular vein.  A Nitinol wire was passed
into the left brachiocephalic vein.  A micropuncture sheath was placed with
tip into the left internal jugular vein over the wire.  A small amount of
contrast was injected to confirm positioning.  A venogram was performed from
this catheter location.  Details are below.

The micropuncture sheath was removed and manual pressure was held.  A sterile
bandage was applied.  The patient tolerated the procedure without
complications.

FINDINGS: 

1.  Initial ultrasound of the right neck demonstrated occlusion of the right
internal jugular vein.
2.  Left brachiocephalic and SVC venogram demonstrates patent central veins
without evidence of stenosis or occlusion.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Patent left brachiocephalic and superior vena cava without evidence of
stenosis or occlusion.
2.  Occlusion of right internal jugular vein, likely chronic.

distention, it is difficult to assess for recurrent malignancy on CT.

The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with mild atherosclerotic
calcifications, more dense at the arch.  Contrast bolus time is not optimized
to evaluate for pulmonary embolism, but there is no large central PE. The

This study is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation.  Contrast from
prior barium swallow is seen in the transverse colon. Atherosclerotic
calcifications are noted at the origins of the celiac and SMA arteries.  No
adrenal mass is visualized.

BONE WINDOWS:  No bone finding suspicious for infection or malignancy is seen.

distention, it is difficult to evaluate for recurrent malignancy on this exam.
Consider endoscopy if clinical concern for recurrence is high.

tracheal wall displacement.
3.  Peribronchial lung parenchymal and centrilobular opacities, almost
exclusively in the dependent bilateral lower lobes, are concerning for
aspiration. A more consolidated region in the right lower lobe may be evolving
aspiration pneumonia.
4.  Small bilateral pleural effusions, slightly increased from ___.
5.  No nodule or mass worrisome for metastatic disease to the lungs.

complications of HIV.

marrow edema, which would indicate acute compression fractures.  If clinically
indicated, the patient may be brought back for STIR images.

3.  Mild narrowing of several neural foramina in the cervical spine, as
described above.

text:
HISTORY:  ___ female with Crohn disease.  Evaluation for improvements
in the terminal ileum on treatment.

TECHNIQUE:  Multiplanar T1 and T2 weighted images were acquired on 1.5 tesla
magnet, including 3D imaging obtained prior to, during and after the
uneventful administration of Gadavist.  Additionally, 900 mL of barium and 450
mL of water were administered orally and 0.25 mg of Levsin was administered
via sublingual tabs.

COMPARISON:  Comparison is made to prior MRI of the abdomen from ___ as well as ___.

FINDINGS:

Since the prior study, there has been interval improvement in the extent of
disease affecting the terminal ileum, with improvement in the degree of wall
thickening and post-contrast enhancement of the distal most terminal ileum to
the ileocecal valve (5:18, 6:51).  There is less wall thickening and edema
within the wall of the terminal ileum in this area.  As seen previously, a
short segment of approximately 1.5 cm of distal ileum demonstrates wall
thickening and luminal narrowing of approximately 5 mm (6:68), which appears
to represent the proximal-most portion of affected ileum.  Overall, this
affected segment measures approximately 25 cm.  There has also been interval
improvement in the degree of adjacent wall thickening and enhancement of the
cecum. The ileocecal valve itself is relatively normal in appearance. 

There is no evidence of phlegmon, abscess or obstruction.  There is no
abnormal dilatation of the more proximal loops of small bowel.  The remainder
of the small bowel is normal in appearance with no evidence of wall thickening
or obstruction and no abnormal enhancement.  Other than the improved wall
thickening in the adjacent cecum, the intra-abdominal loops of large bowel are
essentially unremarkable, with re-demonstration of diverticula within the
sigmoid colon (6:68) with no evidence of diverticulitis.  Some scattered right
lower quadrant mesenteric lymph nodes are again identified.

The liver demonstrates homogeneous signal intensity with redemonstration of a
T2 hyperintense cystic lesion within hepatic segment VI (07:28), consistent
with a biliary hamartoma.  There is no intra- or extra-hepatic biliary ductal
dilatation.  Within the gallbladder, there is re- demonstration of small
gallstones (6:14).  There is no evidence of cholecystitis.  The portal vein is
patent.  The common bile duct is not dilated and the pancreas again
demonstrates a 3 mm cystic lesion within the head of the pancreas (5:22),
unchanged since ___.  The bilateral kidneys, adrenal glands, and
spleen are unremarkable.  The intra-abdominal vasculature is unremarkable with
a normal caliber aorta throughout and a widely patent celiac axis, superior
mesenteric and inferior mesenteric artery.

The uterus and bilateral adnexa are unremarkable. The bladder is normal in
appearance. There is no pelvic lymphadenopathy.  No abnormal bone marrow
signal is identified and no destructive osseous lesions are present.

IMPRESSION:

1. Improvement in the degree of wall thickening, edema and inflammation within
a continuous portion of the wall of the distal-most 25 cm of the terminal
ileum, including persistent luminal narrowing of a 1.5 cm segment at the
proximal end.  There is no evidence of obstruction, fistulization, phlegmon,
or abscess.

2. Unchanged 3 mm cystic lesion within the head of the pancreas, statistically
most likely representing a small side branch IPMN. 

3. Sigmoid diverticulosis with no evidence of diverticulitis.

4. Cholelithiasis with no evidence of cholecystitis.

5. Stable appearance of subcentimeter biliary hamartoma in hepatic segment VI.

INDICATION:  A ___ woman with end-stage renal disease status post
transplant, now on hemodialysis.  Atrial fibrillation, on Coumadin.  Now
presents with abdominal pain.  Mildly elevated amylase/lipase.  Question
pancreatitis.

COMPARISON:  CT of the abdomen and pelvis of ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial acquired images were obtained through the abdomen prior to
and after the administration of 100 mL of IV contrast.  Coronal and sagittal
reformatted images were also displayed.

FINDINGS:  The lung bases demonstrate small bilateral pleural effusions. While
the right-sided pleural effusion has remained stable in size since ___,
the left-sided pleural effusion has slightly increased in size. There is
associated atelectasis at the bases.  The previously noted pulmonary nodule at
the left lung base is not evaluated on this study.  Mild patchy opacities at
the left lung base are incompletely evaluated.  The heart size remains
enlarged.

The spleen, adrenal glands, stomach, and visualized intra-abdominal loops of
bowel are within normal limits.  The patient is status post cholecystectomy.
Again noted adjacent to the aorta at the level of the diaphragmatic crura
(2:15) is a hypodense structure previously described as a prominent cisterna
chyli.  The native kidneys are atrophic bilaterally and do not enhance well.
Again seen is a 12-mm hypodensity in the right kidney with a partial calcified
rim.  The periphery of this lesion does enhance.  Multiple hypodensities
within the left kidney are too small to characterize, but remain unchanged in
appearance.  The liver is within normal limits.

The pancreas does not demonstrate any peripancreatic fat stranding to suggest
pancreatitis.  There is a 9-mm hypodensity in the uncinate process (3:29).  On
the coronal views (400B:30), this hypodensity appears like it could be
connecting with the pancreatic duct and could represent a side branch IPMN.
The remainder of the pancreas is unremarkable.  Small retroperitoneal and
mesenteric lymph nodes do not meet CT criteria for pathologic enlargement.
There is no free air or free fluid.  Vascular calcifications are noted
throughout the abdominal vasculature.

The visualized superior aspect of the transplanted kidney demonstrates normal
enhancement throughout.  There appears to be fatty replacement of the right
psoas muscle, although this is unchanged in appearance.

BONE WINDOWS:  No suspicious osseous lesions are identified.

IMPRESSION:
1.  No radiologic evidence of pancreatitis.  A 9-mm hypodensity within the
uncinate process of the pancreas could represent a side branch IPMN. Attention
to this area should be paid on followup examination. Comparison to prior
study is difficult as no IV contrast was used previously.
2.  Bilateral small pleural effusions with associated atelectasis.  The left-
sided pleural effusion has increased in size since ___.  Mild patchy
opacities at the left lung base are incompletely evaluated and could be
further evaluated with chest x-ray. 
3.  Peripherally enhancing lesion within the right native kidney for which
further evaluation is warranted with a renal MR.

INDICATION:  Mr. ___ is a ___ year-old male with history of IVDU, transferred
from ___ for concern for C5-6 discitis/osteomyelitis  // Osteomyelitis in
C5/C6? Septic discitis?

COMPARISON:  CT neck ___

PROCEDURE:  CT-guided C5-6 biopsy.

OPERATORS:  Dr. ___, radiology fellow and Dr. ___, attending
radiologist. Dr. ___ supervised the trainee during the key
components of the procedure and reviewed and agrees with the trainee's
findings.

TECHNIQUE:  The risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure were
explained to the patient.  After a detailed discussion, informed written
consent was obtained.  A pre-procedure timeout using three patient identifiers
was performed per ___ protocol.

The patient was placed in a supine position on the CT scan table.  Limited
preprocedure CTscan of the intended biopsy area was performed.  Based on the
CT findings an appropriate position for the biopsy was chosen.  The site was
marked.

The site was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.  1% lidocaine
were administered to the subcutaneous and deep tissues for local anesthetic
effect.  Under CT guidance, a 17 gauge coaxial needle was introduced into the
lesion.  An 18 gauge core biopsy device with a 22 mm throw was used to obtain 
core biopsy specimens, which were sent for pathology.

The specimen was evaluated by onsite cytologist, deemed adequate.

The procedure was tolerated well and there were no immediate post-procedural
complications.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 6.4 s, 19.6 cm; CTDIvol = 8.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 158.3
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.4 s, 1.0 cm; CTDIvol = 2.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 2.9
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 5.1 s, 1.4 cm; CTDIvol = 52.7 mGy (Body) DLP =
75.9 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 244 mGy-cm.

SEDATION:  Moderate sedation was provided by administering divided doses of 4
mg Versed and 2 1 mcg fentanyl throughout the total intra-service time of 40
minutes during which patient's hemodynamic parameters were continuously
monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse.

FINDINGS:


1. Findings consistent with osteomyelitis discitis of C5-6.

IMPRESSION: 

Technically successful CT-guided biopsy of bone at the C5-6 disc space.

text:
EXAMINATION:  BILATERAL BREAST STEREOTACTIC-GUIDED CORE BIOPSY WITH CLIP
PLACEMENT; SPECIMEN RADIOGRAPH; AND POST-PROCEDURE MAMMOGRAM

INDICATION:  ___ woman initially presenting with left intermittent
nipple discharge and found to have indeterminate microcalcifications in the
left lower outer breast and right upper inner posterior breast.  Request for
stereotactic core biopsy.

COMPARISON:  The relevant imaging was available for this procedure including:
Diagnostic bilateral mammogram dated ___.
Bilateral targeted breast ultrasound dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

LEFT:  Coarse heterogeneous indeterminate calcifications in the left lower
slightly outer breast at mid depth are again demonstrated.  These are
posterior to the coarse linearly arranged calcifications which appear benign.

RIGHT:  Faint, linear microcalcifications with suggestion of branching in the
right upper inner breast at posterior depth are again demonstrated.

PROCEDURE:  Consent: The procedure, risks, benefits and alternatives were
discussed with the patient and written informed consent was obtained.
Time-out certification:  Performed using three patient identifiers, with
confirmation of side and site.
Allergies / Medications:  The patient's medication list and history of
allergies were reviewed prior to beginning the procedure.
Clinicians:  ___, M.D., ___, M.D., ___, M.D. The
procedure was supervised by ___, M.D.(Attending).
Description:

LEFT BREAST:
First, we targeted the indeterminate calcifications in the left breast. The
lesion was localized stereotactically using an inferior approach. Using
standard aseptic technique and local anesthesia, a small skin incision was
made and a 9-g needle was advanced to the calcifications in the left lower
outer breast. Pre- and post-fire images confirmed the needle was at the
target.  Several core biopsies via a vacuum-assisted biopsy device while
giving additional anesthesia were obtained.  The initial attempt yielded
limited samples and a decision was made to exchange the biopsy device from
which we were then able to obtain several adequate samples. Next, a TriMark
dumbbell clip was deployed at the biopsy site. The needle was removed and
hemostasis was achieved.

RIGHT BREAST:
Next, we turned our attention to the right breast. The  lesion was localized
stereotactically via the superior approach. Using standard aseptic technique
and local anesthesia, a small skin incision was made and a 9-g needle was
advanced to the calcifications in the right upper medial breast. Pre- and
post-fire images confirmed the needle was at the target, and 10 core biopsies
were obtained using a vacuum-assisted biopsy device while additional
anesthesia was given. Next, a TriMark dumbbell clip was deployed at the biopsy
site.  The needle was removed and hemostasis was achieved.


Estimated blood loss:  < 5 cc.
Specimens: Sent to pathology, labeled with and without calcifications for each
breast (Left breast labeled A, B; Right breast labeled C, D).
Anesthesia:  ___ cc 1% lidocaine and 8 cc 1% lidocaine with epinephrine.
Complications: The patient tolerated the procedure well and had a small 2 cm
hematoma in the left breast.
Post-procedure diagnosis: Same.

SPECIMEN RADIOGRAPH:  Specimen radiograph confirmed the presence of
microcalcifications in the specimens obtained from both breasts.

POST-PROCEDURE MAMMOGRAM:
LEFT: CC and ML views confirm appropriate clip placementwith residual
calcifications anteriomedial and slightly superior to the dumbell clip.  There
is post-biopsy change including a small 2-cm hematoma at the biopsy site just
medial and inferior to the clip.

RIGHT:  CC and ML views confirm appropriate clip placement with a few residual
calcifications anterior to the biopsy clip. There are expected post-biopsy
changes.  Again seen are two additional linear calcifications in the lower
inner breast which were not targeted today and for which further management
will depend on the results from today's biopsy.  Again seen is a small
circumscribed, 1-cm oval mass in the right upper outer breast which could be a
fibroadenoma, for which further management will also depend on the results
from today's biopsy.

IMPRESSION: 

Technically successful bilateral stereotactic core biopsy of indeterminate
calcifications in the left lower outer and right upper inner breast with
residual calcifications and post-biopsy change as above.  Pathology is
pending.

The patient expects to hear the pathology results from Dr. ___,
the referring provider, in ___ business days.  Standard post care instructions
were provided to the patient and her daughter, ___, who accompanied her for
the pre-procedure, consent, and post-procedure period.

As the Attending radiologist, I personally supervised the Resident and Fellow
during the key components of the above procedure and I reviewed and agree with
the Resident's and Fellow's findings and dictation.

COMPARISON:
GJ placement dated ___

TECHNIQUE:
OPERATORS: Dr. ___ Interventional ___ and Dr. ___,
Interventional Radiology fellow performed the procedure. Dr. ___
supervised the trainee during any key components of the procedure where
applicable and reviewed and agrees with the findings as reported below.
ANESTHESIA: Moderate sedation was provided by administrating divided doses of
100mcg of fentanyl and 2 mg of midazolam throughout the total intra-service
time of 63 min during which the patient's hemodynamic parameters were
continuously monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse. 1% lidocaine
was injected in the skin and subcutaneous tissues overlying the access site.
MEDICATIONS:
CONTRAST: 10 ml of Optiray contrast
FLUOROSCOPY TIME AND DOSE: 36.1 min,  mGy

PROCEDURE:  18 ___ MIC gastrojejunostomy exchange.

PROCEDURE DETAILS: Following the discussion of the risks, benefits and
alternatives to the procedure, written informed consent was obtained from the
patient. The patient was then brought to the angiography suite and placed
supine on the exam table. A pre-procedure time-out was performed per ___
protocol. The tube site was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.

The existing tube was injected with aired and showed opacification of the
gastric rugae and duodenum. The stay sutures were cut and a stiff glide wire
was introduced into the stomach. The existing feeding tube was then removed. 
When attempting to advance the new GJ tube over the wire, it buckled at the
pylorus and curled in the stomach.  The tube was removed over the wire.  A 7
___ sheath was placed. A Kumpe catheter was then introduced through the
sheath and was advanced through the pylorus and into the proximal jejunum.  An
Amplatz wire was advanced through the catheter.  The catheter and sheath were
removed.  Repeated attempts to advance the new GJ over the Wire was attempted,
but it again buckled at the pylorus uncurled stomach.  Tube was again removed
over the wire.  Kumpe catheter was advanced over the wire.  Amplatz wire was
exchanged for a Glidewire.  The Glidewire and catheter were used to advanced
through the pylorus into the jejunum.  Once in place, the Glidewire was
exchanged for an Amplatz wire.  Catheter was removed.  The new GJ was again
advanced over the wire, this time despite difficulty passing the catheter
through the pylorus, it was successfully advanced into the duodenum.  The wire
was removed.
The catheters balloon was inflated with 7 ml of contrast contrast diluted in
sterile water in the stomach after confirming the position of the catheter
with air injection. The catheter was then flushed, capped and secured to the
skin with 0-silk sutures. Sterile dressings were applied.
The patient tolerated the procedure well and there were no immediate
complications.

FINDINGS: 

1.  Appropriately positioned new 18 ___ MIC gastrojejunostomy tube.

IMPRESSION:


Successful placement of a 18 ___ MIC gastrojejunostomy tube with its tip in
the proximal jejunum.  Tube can be used immediately

RECOMMENDATION(S):

Catheter does not pass easily through the pylorus.  Recommend caution when
advancing.

EXAMINATION:  MRI CERVICAL SPINE WITHOUT CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with Parkinsonism, now with brisk reflexes
throughout and mild hypertonia in legs.  Please evaluate for spinal canal
stenosis, spondylosis.

TECHNIQUE:   Sagittal T1 weighted, T2 weighted, and fat suppressed T2 weighted
images of the cervical spine with axial gradient echo and T2 weighted images.

COMPARISON:  Cervical spine radiographs ___.

FINDINGS: 

Vertebral body heights are within normal limits.  No concerning bone marrow
signal abnormalities are seen.  Minimal retrolisthesis of C2 on C3 , C3 on C4,
of C4 on C5, and of C6 on C7 is similar to the ___ radiographs.  Mild
anterolisthesis of C7 on T1 is also noted, not well assessed on the prior
radiographs.

The cerebellar tonsils are normally positioned, and the visualized portion of
the posterior fossa appears unremarkable.

The craniocervical junction and the C1-C2 level appear unremarkable.

C2-C3:  Minimal retrolisthesis indents the ventral thecal sac but does not
contact the spinal cord.  Mild facet arthropathy without significant neural
foraminal narrowing.

C3-C4:  Mild retrolisthesis and a broad-based central disc protrusion with
endplate osteophyte indent the ventral thecal sac and contact the ventral
spinal cord.  Cord signal is within normal limits.  The spinal canal is
moderately narrowed.  Bilateral neural foramina are severely narrowed by
uncovertebral and facet osteophytes.

C4-C5:  Mild retrolisthesis and a central/left paracentral broad-based disc
protrusion with endplate osteophytes cause severe spinal canal stenosis with
spinal cord compression.  Apparent foci of high signal are seen within the
spinal cord bilaterally, images 6 and 9 of series 2 and 3, and image 6:21,
compatible with myelomalacia.  There is severe right and moderate to severe
left neural foraminal narrowing by uncovertebral and facet osteophytes.

C5-C6:  Left paracentral disc herniation with overlying endplate osteophytes
flatten the left ventral spinal cord and moderately narrow the spinal canal on
the left.  Cord signal remains within normal limits.  There is severe
bilateral neural foraminal narrowing by uncovertebral and facet osteophytes.

C6-C7:  Broad-based central/right paracentral disc protrusion with endplate
osteophytes, and minimal retrolisthesis, flatten the right and central ventral
of the spinal cord with mild to moderate spinal canal narrowing.  Cord signal
remains within normal limits.  There is severe right and moderate to severe
left neural foraminal narrowing by uncovertebral and facet osteophytes.

C7-T1:  Mild anterolisthesis is present.  No significant spinal canal or
neural foraminal narrowing is seen.

T1-T2:  No spinal canal or neural foraminal narrowing.

3 mm focus of T2 hyperintensity in the posterior aspect of the interpolar left
thyroid lobe, image 6:32, is of doubtful clinical significance.

IMPRESSION: 

Extensive multilevel degenerative disease.  Severe spinal canal stenosis at
C4-C5 with spinal cord compression and evidence of myelomalacia.  Moderate
spinal canal stenosis with ventral spinal cord deformity is present at several
other levels, without evidence for cord signal abnormalities.  Severe neural
foraminal narrowing at multiple levels.

EXAMINATION:  MR HEAD W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with ongoing R face weakness and aphasia,
corresponding to location of SAH  // eval for vasospasm leading to infarct

TECHNIQUE:  Sagittal T1 weighted imaging was performed. Axial imaging was
performed with gradient echo, FLAIR, diffusion, and T2 technique.

COMPARISON:  CTA head of ___, outside hospital MRI head of ___.

FINDINGS: 

FLAIR hyperintense and gradient echo negative susceptibility related
hypointense foci within the left prefrontal gyral sulci extending inferiorly
to the superior temporal lobe are identified compatible with subarachnoid
hemorrhage, similar in distribution from prior CT examinations allowing for
technical differences. There is gyriform gradient echo negative susceptibility
in of the left parietal sulci near the vertex (series 11, image 16) unchanged
from prior exam of ___. This may represent hemosiderin staining from prior
hemorrhage. Additional foci of gradient echo negative susceptibility in the
inferior right occipital lobe (series 11, image 10) which may represent
sequela of microhemorrhage versus vessel en face.  In particular, the
posterior focus is contiguous with a linear line extending into the torcula,
suggesting vessel.

Although evaluation is confounded by the subarachnoid hemorrhage, there does
appear to be very subtle punctate foci of slow diffusion of the left
precentral gyrus (series 4, image 24 through 26), suspicious for associated
ischemia/infarct; however, this can be difficult to differentiate from the
adjacent negative susceptibility related to the hemorrhage, given the slight
technical differences between the diffusion and the gradient echo sequences.

There are superimposed periventricular and subcortical T2/FLAIR white matter
hyperintense foci, similar in appearance to prior exam of ___, which are
nonspecific, but commonly seen in setting of chronic microangiopathy. Sulci,
ventricles and cisterns are within expected limits for the patient's age.
The major intracranial flow voids are preserved, with incidental note of a 2
mm outpouching at the left MCA bifurcation compatible with known aneurysm.
The paranasal sinuses are clear. The orbits are unremarkable. The mastoid air
cells are clear.

IMPRESSION:


1. Re- identified is left prefrontal gyral subarachnoid hemorrhage extending
to the superior left temporal lobe.
2. Evaluation is complicated by this hemorrhage;  small cortically based left
prefrontal gyral tiny foci of slow diffusion suspicious for ischemia/infarct
versus related to hemorrhage.  Attention on followup for better
characterization.
3. Stable since ___, superimposed gyriform gradient echo negative
susceptibility within the sulci of the left occipital lobe, potentially
representing hemosiderin staining/ superficial siderosis.
4. Additional gradient echo negative susceptibility foci in the inferior right
occipital lobe, potentially microhemorrhage although one focus appears
contiguous with a linear line extending into the torcula and thus likely
represent vessels en face.
5. Stable superimposed T2/FLAIR subcortical and periventricular white matter
hyper intensities, which are nonspecific, but compatible with chronic
microangiopathy.

NOTIFICATION:  Prelim. findings were discussed by Dr. ___ with Dr.

text:
HISTORY:  ___ male with dysphagia.

COMPARISON:  None available.

TECHNIQUE:  An oral and pharyngeal swallowing evaluation was performed today
in collaboration with speech and swallow pathology.  Multiple consistencies of
barium were administered orally during video fluoroscopic observation.  

FINDINGS: During the oral phase, bolus formation was mildly prolonged with
ground solid, however, no significant oral residue remained after the swallow.
Bolus control was moderately reduced with premature spillover of contents into
the piriform sinuses before all liquid swallows.  AP tongue movement was
grossly within functional limits, with timely oral transit. During the
pharyngeal phase, swallow initiation was timely. Palatal elevation, epiglottic
deflection, pharyngeal transit, and bolus propulsion were within functional
limits.  Laryngeal elevation and laryngeal valve closure was mildly reduced.
No residue remained within the valleculae or piriform sinuses after the
swallow.  The patient experienced deep penetration with thin and nectar thick
liquids before and during the swallow secondary to premature spillover of
contents. While no aspiration was seen with nectar thick liquids, a small
amount of thin liquid was aspirated before the height of the swallow without a
spontaneous cough.  Cued coughs were ineffective at clearing the aspirate.

IMPRESSION:  Patient with moderate oral and pharyngeal dysphagia characterized
by reduced oral control, reduced high laryngeal excursion, and silent
aspiration of thin liquids.  Aspiration of penetrated nectar thick liquids was
improved when taking small single sips from the straw.

For further dietary recommendations, please refer to speech and swallow
pathology note on CareWeb.

EXAM:  MRI of the cervical spine.

CLINICAL INFORMATION:  Patient with new-onset numbness radiating to the left
arm.  Patient is due to receive chemotherapy for lymphoma for further
evaluation.

TECHNIQUE:  T1, T2 and inversion recovery sagittal and gradient echo and T2
axial images of cervical spine were obtained before gadolinium.  T1 sagittal
and axial images were obtained following gadolinium.  The examination is
limited by motion.

FINDINGS:  At the craniocervical junction and at C2-3 mild degenerative change
is noted.  At C3-4 there is disc bulging identified without spinal stenosis or
foraminal narrowing.

At C4-5 disc bulging and posterior ridging identified with mild spinal
stenosis and mild-to-moderate right foraminal narrowing.

At C5-6 there is posterior ridging and disc bulging with mild spinal stenosis
with mild bilateral foraminal narrowing.

At C6-7 disc bulging and mild spinal stenosis seen with posterior ridging with
moderate left foraminal narrowing.

At C7-T1 moderate left foraminal narrowing identified without spinal stenosis.

From T1-2 and T2-3 mild degenerative change is noted.

Spinal cord shows normal signal on limited evaluation on this motion limited
study.

Following gadolinium no abnormal enhancement is identified.

IMPRESSION:  Multilevel degenerative changes with mild spinal stenosis from
C4-5 to C6-7.  No evidence of intrinsic spinal cord signal abnormalities.
Minimal extrinsic indentation on the spinal cord at C4-5, C5-6 and C6-7
secondary to posterior ridging.  Limited study secondary to motion.  No
enhancing  intraspinal mass or signs of metastatic disease seen.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of 100 cc of Omnipaque intravenous
contrast.
Reformatted coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal
intensity projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  DLP: 463.48 mGy-cm

COMPARISON:  Same date chest x-ray.

FINDINGS: 

The aorta is normal in course andcaliber. .

The patient is post partial left lobectomy with a superiorly coursing left
pulmonary artery, likely post surgical change.  Despite that, the pulmonary
arteries are well opacified to the subsegmental level, with no evidence of
filling defect within the main, right, left, lobar, segmental or subsegmental
pulmonary arteries. The main and right pulmonary arteries are normal in
caliber.

Small calcifications are noted in the mediastinum, in this patient with
history of sarcoidosis. There is no supraclavicular, axillary, or hilar
lymphadenopathy. The thyroid gland appears unremarkable.

There is no evidence of pericardial effusion. There is no pleural effusion.

The patient is post likely left upper lobectomy.  There is bilateral apical
scarring and fibrotic changes is well as upward traction of the hila. There is
consolidation in the periphery of the left mid to lower lung measuring
maximally 11 mm, also appearing to involve the pleura.

Limited images of the upper abdomen are unremarkable.

No lytic or blastic osseous lesion suspicious for malignancy is identified.

IMPRESSION: 

1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic dissection.
2.  Status post left upper lobectomy.  Peripheral hypodense consolidation
along the lateral left mid to lower lung, involving the pleura, that could
represent a resolving pneumonia, however underlying malignancy is not
excluded. Would recommend short-term 6 week followup for assessment.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  See impression point 2.

TECHNIQUE:  Sagittal imaging was performed with T2, T1, and STIR technique,

COMPARISON:  MRI of the lumbar spine from ___.

FINDINGS: 

There is minimal retrolisthesis of T12 on L1, similar to prior.  Grade 1
anterolisthesis of L4 on L5, L5 on S1 is unchanged compared to the prior exam.
Mild levoscoliosis centered at L3-L4 is also unchanged compared to the prior
exam.  Vertebral body height is well preserved.  ___ type 2 endplate changes
are seen at L3-L4.  A rounded focus of fat is seen within the L1 vertebral
body measuring up to 1.5 cm and L4 vertebral body measuring up to 0.5 cm. 
Diffuse degenerative disc desiccation is seen throughout the lumbar spine. 
The cord terminates at T12-L1.  No terminal cord signal abnormalities are
seen.  There multilevel diffuse disc bulges.  Advanced lower lumbar facet
arthritis.

T11-T12: There is no spinal canal or neural foraminal narrowing.

T12-L1: No significant spinal canal narrowing.  Facet joint osteophytes
contribute to mild bilateral neural foraminal narrowing, right greater than
left.

L1-L2: No significant spinal canal narrowing.  Facet joint osteophytes are
seen resulting in mild right neural foraminal narrowing.  The left neural
foramina is patent.

L2-L3: Mild disc bulge is seen, which in conjunction with facet joint
osteophytes and ligamentum flavum thickening results in mild spinal canal
narrowing, similar.  Facet joint osteophytes contribute to mild to moderate
right and mild left neural foraminal narrowing, similar.

L3-L4: Disc bulge with a focal central disc protrusion is seen, which is less
prominent compared to prior.  In conjunction with facet joint arthropathy and
ligamentum flavum thickening findings result in moderate severe spinal canal
narrowing, mildly improved.  Facet joint osteophytes results in severe right
and mild-to-moderate left neural foraminal narrowing, overall similar to the
prior exam.

L4-L5: Disc bulge, facet joint arthropathy and ligamentum flavum thickening
results in moderate to severe spinal canal narrowing, minimally progressed
compared to the prior exam.  Facet joint osteophytes contribute to moderate
right and mild left neural foraminal narrowing, mildly worsened on the right.

L5-S1: Uncovering of the intervertebral disc, disc bulge, facet joint
arthropathy and ligamentum flavum thickening results in moderate spinal canal
narrowing, similar.  Both lateral recesses are narrowed, mildly worsened since
prior.  There is severe bilateral foraminal narrowing, similar to prior.

Extensive right paraspinal soft tissue edema is seen spanning from T12 through
L2.  If there is clinical concern for right L1, L2 transverse process
fracture, CT would be more sensitive in further evaluation.  No paravertebral
soft tissue abnormalities are identified.

IMPRESSION:


1. Interval mild progression of moderate to severe lumbar spondylosis.
2. There is multilevel central canal narrowing, most prominent and moderate to
severe at L3-L4, L4-5 level.
3. There is multilevel significant foraminal narrowing, as above.
4. Moderate right paraspinal soft tissue edema spanning from T12 through L2
could be sequelae of prior trauma.  No definite fractures identified, however
please note that CT is more sensitive for subtle fractures, in particular
transverse process fractures at these levels.

EXAMINATION:  CT ABD AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION:  NO_PO contrast; History: ___ with lower abdominal pain and
diarrhea, history of diverticulitisNO_PO contrast// Eval diverticulitis or
other acute intra-abdominal process.

TECHNIQUE:  Single phase contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired through the
abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 4.2 s, 55.7 cm; CTDIvol = 24.5 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,362.1 mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 3.4 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.7
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 4.8 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 26.8 mGy (Body) DLP =
13.4 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,377 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen pelvis ___.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Visualized lung fields are within normal limits.  There is no
evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions. There is no evidence of intrahepatic or
extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is surgically absent.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no hydronephrosis.  Redemonstration of tiny hypodensities in the
upper pole of the left kidney, too small to fully characterize, but likely
cysts.  There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout. Colonic
diverticula are seen within the distal transverse colon, descending colon, and
sigmoid colon, without evidence for acute diverticulitis.  The appendix is not
visualized, and no secondary signs of inflammation to suggest appendicitis are
demonstrated.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: An intrauterine device appears appropriately position
within the uterus.  No concerning adnexal abnormality.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  No atherosclerotic disease
is noted.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES: Small umbilical and infraumbilical fat containing hernias are
present, unchanged from the prior exam, with each fascial defect measuring up
to 19 mm.

IMPRESSION:
