text:
INDICATION:  Evaluation of patient with history of metastatic renal cell
carcinoma status post two cycles of Avastin therapy.

COMPARISON:  CT torso from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT-acquired axial images were obtained from the thoracic inlet
to the pubic symphysis after administration of 100 mL of Visipaque contrast. 
Three-minute delayed images were obtained through the abdomen.  Multiplanar
reformatted images were prepared.

CT OF THE CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST:  Again visualized is a rim-enhancing nodule
along the musculature of the left chest wall (2:23), which remains stable and
now measures 14 x 7 mm.  Previously visualized necrotic-appearing lymph node
abutting the right pulmonary vein (2:37) appears mildly enlarged in comparison
to prior study and now measures 20 x 15 mm compared to 16 x 11 mm previously. 
Otherwise, there is no evidence of new axillary, hilar, or mediastinal
lymphadenopathy.  The pulmonary artery appears normal in caliber and course. 
Coronary artery calcifications are again visualized.

Multiple hypodense lesions involving the lateral wall of the left ventricle,
apex, intraventricular septum, and right ventricle are again visualized. 
Previously visualized hypodense lesion in the free wall of the left ventricle
(2:43) has now enlarged and measures 34 x 32 mm compared to 34 x 24 mm
previously.  Furthermore, previously visualized right ventricular lesion is
again visualized and has remained relatively stable at 35 x 32 mm compared to
42 x 30 mm previously.  A moderate-sized pericardial effusion is again seen.

Previously visualized lingular pulmonary lesion (2:37) has also minimally
increased in size and now measures 43 x 26 mm compared to 38 x 30 mm
previously.  Numerous other pulmonary lesions are visualized throughout
bilateral lungs and these have increased in both size and number.  

The airways are patent to the subsegmental levels.  There is no evidence of
consolidations.  Thoracic aorta is normal in caliber and contour.  The
remainder of the great vessels are within normal limits. A new soft tissue
lesion is visualized in the anterior chest wall (2:30) measuring 12 x 9 mm.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:  Numerous liver lesions are again
identified throughout the liver.  Previously identified lesion in segment V of
the liver has now increased in size (2:64) and measures 21 x 13 mm compared to
12 x 9 mm previously.  Furthermore, the heterogeneous liver area anterior to
this region has also increased in size (2:67).  

Cholelithiasis without evidence of cholecystitis are visualized; otherwise,
the gallbladder is within normal limits.  The pancreas and spleen appear
normal.  Subcentimeter hypodensities are again visualized throughout the right
kidney, but are too small to characterize.  There is, however, a 12 x 9 mm new
nodule in the right pararenal space (2:83), which was not previously seen. 
Furthermore, previously seen hypodensity in the right adrenal gland with
attenuation values that are indeterminable is again seen (2:62).  The left
renal resection bed does not demonstrate any soft tissues.

Previously visualized retrocrural node again seen (2:16) appears stable at 30
x 12 mm.  Otherwise, there is no other pathologic retroperitoneal or
mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

A new soft tissue density suggestive of likely metastasis is visualized in the
anterior abdominal wall (2:71) and measures 20 x 20 mm.  Furthermore,
previously visualized posterior soft tissue density has enlarged (2:76) and
now measures 19 x 12 mm compared to 8 x 8 mm previously.  

Atherosclerotic calcifications are visualized throughout the aorta and there
is an area of hypodensity (2:73) in the lumen suggestive of thrombus.  There
is a lack of opacification of the left common iliac arteries at the aortic
bifurcation suggestive of occlusion of the left common iliac artery.  
Atherosclerotic calcifications are noted throughout the external iliac artery
at the bifurcation of the right common iliac artery into the internal and
external iliac arteries and limit the assessment for thrombosi.  However,
there is a lack of opacification of the left internal iliac arteries
suggestive of continued thrombosis.

There is no free air or free fluid in the abdomen.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:  The bladder and distal ureters are within
normal limits.  The prostate remains enlarged but stable.  There is no pelvic
free air or free fluid.  There is no pathologic pelvic or inguinal
lymphadenopathy.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  Partly sclerotic lesion in the lateral aspect of the
right sixth rib remains unchanged.  Lytic sclerotic lesion is again visualized
in the vertebral body of L1.  Multiple new soft tissue lesions are visualized
as described above.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Progression of metastatic disease as shown by increased size and number of
pulmonary lesions along with increase in size of liver lesions, and the
presence multiple new soft tissue nodules as described above.
2.  Continued visualization of mural thrombus in the aorta along with
continued occlusion of the left common iliac artery.

EXAMINATION:  Thoracolumbar spine without contrast.

HISTORY:  ___ male with acute compression fractures of L1 and T12, to
evaluate for kyphoplasty planning.

TECHNIQUE:  Multiplanar CT of the thoracolumbar spine was performed without
intravenous gadolinium administration.

MR lumbar spine ___.

FINDINGS:

There is dense atherosclerotic calcification of the abdominal aorta and its
branches.  There are multiple right renal hypodensities and bilateral
perinephric fat stranding.  There is biapical pleural scarring.

There is approximately 40% compression of the L1 vertebral body and
approximately 20% compression of the T12 vertebral body with approximately 3
mm retropulsion of fracture fragments and no significant kyphotic angulation
deformity.

There is preservation of the remaining vertebral body heights and alignment. 
There are multilevel endplate degenerative changes and vacuum disc phenomenon
at T11-12.

At L5-S1, there is a diffuse disc bulge and facet osteophytes resulting in
mild bilateral neural foraminal narrowing.  The spinal canal is not narrowed.

At L4-5, there is a diffuse disc bulge, facet osteophytes, and ligamentum
flavum thickening resulting in mild narrowing of the spinal canal.  There is
mild right neural foraminal narrowing secondary to bulging disc material and
facet osteophytes.

At L3-4, there is a diffuse disc bulge, facet osteophytes,ligamentum flavum
thickening and posterior disc osteophyte resulting in moderate  narrowing of
the spinal canal and right subarticular recess.  There is moderate right and
mild left neural foraminal narrowing secondary to bulging disc material and
facet osteophytes.

At L2-3, there is a diffuse disc bulge and facet osteophytes without
significant narrowing of the spinal canal.  There is mild bilateral neural
foraminal narrowing secondary to bulging disc material and facet osteophytes.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Compression fractures of the L1 and T12 vertebral bodies of indeterminate
age with mild retropulsion of fracture fragments and no significant angulation
deformity.  MRI may be obtained for further evaluation of acuity, if
clinically indicated.

2.  Additional multilevel degenerative changes of the lumbar spine as
described above.

3.  Advanced atherosclerotic calcification of the abdominal aorta and its
branches.

text:
INDICATIONS:  ___ woman with metastatic colon cancer.  Question
disease progression.

COMPARISONS:  ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial CT images of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis were obtained
with oral and intravenous contrast.  Sagittal and coronal reformations were
also performed.  Three-minute delayed phase imaging through the abdomen was
also obtained.

CT OF THE CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST:  A Port-A-Cath again terminates at the
cavoatrial junction.  Multiple small subcentimeter mediastinal lymph nodes,
not meeting CT size criteria for pathological enlargement, are not
significantly changed, although a left hilar lymph node may be minimally
increased in size, measuring 9 x 13 mm on this examination, compared to 9 x 12
mm previously.  There is no pleural or pericardial effusion.  In the superior
segment of the left lower lobe, there is a soft tissue nodule measuring 3 mm
in diameter (3:30) which is not significantly changed. Three additional small
nodules in the left upper lobe are also not significantly changed
(3:17,26,20).  

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:  Multiple hypodense metastatic lesions are
again visualized throughout the liver.  These show a mixed response to
therapy.  For example, a large mass in the mid right lobe measures 43 x 32 mm
(5:24), compared to 45 x 33 mm before, not significantly changed.  However, a
conglomerate of nodules in segment IV measures 44 x 33 mm (5:28) compared to
29 x 28 mm before, clearly increased in extent, with new nearby satellite
nodules as well.  On the other hand, many nodules are somewhat smaller.  A
representive one in segment VII, for example, measures 13 x 13 mm (5:17),
compared to 20 x 16 mm previously.  These measurements are performed using the
delayed phase images in order to minimize the influence of bolus timing on the
measurements.  

Soft tissue infiltration of the gallbladder, porta hepatis and celiac fat has
progressed somewhat, consistent with additional metastatic disease.  A small
new cuff of soft tissue thickening is also noted along the upper aspect of the
left gonadal vein, and fat stranding about the aorta and nearby
retroperitoneal fat is more prominent.  On the other hand, there is no
definite evidence for peritoneal implants, although a small amount of new
ascites is present.

The spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable.  The
stomach, small and large bowel are within normal limits.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:  The uterus, adnexal regions, and bladder
are within normal limits.  A considerable quantity of stool is present within
the rectum, which is moderately distended.  There is a very small amount of
free fluid, but no definite evidence for peritoneal carcinomatosis on this
study.  The ascites is new, however, since the prior study.  

There are no suspicious lytic or blastic lesions.  The bones appear
demineralized.

IMPRESSION:  

1.  Mixed response of hepatic metastases to therapy with some areas showing an
increased extent of metastatic disease.  

2.  Evidence for retroperitoneal metastatic disease as well as a small new
quantity of ascites, raising concern for early peritoneal disease in this
setting.  Short-term follow-up surveillance is recommended.

HISTORY:  ___ woman with progressive leg weakness and a possible
paraneoplastic process.  Please evaluate for breast malignancy.

COMPARISON:  CT chest from ___.

DIGITAL DIAGNOSTIC BILATERAL MAMMOGRAM WITH CAD:
Heterogeneously dense parenchyma is identified which limits mammographic
sensitivity.  A focal asymmetry is noted in the upper outer right breast which
is pliable on spot compression views.  It is not seen on the 90-degree lateral
view of the right breast.  However, further evaluation with ultrasound was
performed.  In addition, slightly prominent right axillary lymph nodes are
noted.  No suspicious microcalcifications or unexplained architectural
distortion is identified.

RIGHT BREAST ULTRASOUND:
Evaluation of the right breast was limited as the patient was sitting in a
wheelchair during the scan.  At 12 o'clock, 6 cm from the nipple, there is a
1.5 x 1 x 1.8 cm ill-defined hypoechoic mass with no internal vascularity that
is suspicious.  Approximately 1 cm medial to the dominant mass, there is a 0.3
x 0.4 x 0.6 cm ill-defined hypoechoic nodule with no internal vascularity that
is also suspicious.  In total, they measure 2.4 cm in transverse dimension.  

At 12 o'clock, 8 cm from the nipple, there is a third hypoechoic ill-defined
area that measures 0.9 x 0.9 cm and is approximately 1.3 cm superior to the
dominant lesion.  

Additional scanning shows irregular serpentine densities projecting towards
the nipple that likely represent debris within ducts.

The right axilla was scanned and a borderline abnormal lymph node was noted
with cortical thickness measuring up to 0.3 cm with macrolobulations.  

LEFT BREAST ULTRASOUND: Given the slight prominent lymph nodes on CT scan, a
left axillary ultrasound was performed. Evaluation of the left breast was
limited as the patient was sitting in a wheelchair during the scan.

The left axilla was scanned and borderline prominent lymph nodes were noted
with cortical thickness measuring up to 0.4 cm.

IMPRESSION:
Suspicious mass in the right breast at 12 o'clock, 6 cm from the nipple, for
which ultrasound-guided core biopsy is recommended.  In addition, slightly
prominent lymph nodes are noted bilaterally and fine-needle aspiration is
recommended.  This was discussed with Dr. ___ telephone by Dr.
___ at the time of the exam.

On review of the mammogram and CT scan, there is a questionable asymmetry in
the superior slightly medial left breast.  Given that the patient will be on a
stretcher at the time of the ultrasound-guided core biopsy, in a more optimal
position for ultrasound evaluation, a repeat left breast ultrasound involving
the upper inner left breast is recommended to evaluate for an underlying
malignancy.

BI-RADS 4B -- suspicious, intermediate suspicion of malignancy, histologic
sampling is recommended in the absence of a clinical contraindication.

HISTORY:  Altered mental status and fever, evaluate for the presence of
pneumonia and acute intra-abdominal source.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT-images were acquired from the thoracic inlet to the pubic
symphysis after the uneventful administration of 100 mL of Visipaque.  Coronal
and sagittal reformations were provided and reviewed.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen and pelvis dated ___.

FINDINGS:

Chest:  The imaged thyroid is unremarkable.  Scattered axillary and
mediastinal lymph nodes do not meet CT size criteria for enlargement.  The
heart is normal in size and there is no pericardial effusion.  Status post
CABG with severe native coronary artery and moderate aortic valvular
calcifications present.

The airways are patent to the subsegmental level.  There is no pleural
effusion or pneumothorax.  3 mm ground-glass nodules are seen in the right
upper lobe (2:14) and (2:25).

Abdomen:  The liver enhances homogeneously without focal lesions.  The
gallbladder is distended with gallbladder wall edema and small layering
gallstones.  There are impacted stones within the common bile duct at the
ampulla measuring up to 9-mm (2:66).  There is mild intrahepatic biliary
ductal dilation.  The common bile duct is dilated to 1.4 cm.  

The spleen, pancreas and adrenal glands are unremarkable.  The kidneys enhance
symmetrically and excrete contrast without hydronephrosis.  A 1.5 cm hypodense
cyst within the posterior cortex of the left kidney is unchanged.  A 1.3 cm
heterogenously dense lesion within the lower pole of the right kidney and is
stable but not further characterized on this study.  The stomach and small
bowel are normal.  There is slight thickening of the rectal wall with haziness
of the fat and fecal loading.  There is no obstruction.  There is a small
hiatal hernia.  

There is no free air or free fluid.  Moderate atherosclerosis is seen within
the aorta.  Mild infrarenal ectasia measuring 2.5 x 2.2 cm is noted.  The
portal vein, splenic vein and superior mesenteric vein are patent.

Pelvis:  A Foley catheter is seen within the collapsed bladder.  The sigmoid
colon is unremarkable.  The appendix is normal.  There is no free pelvic
fluid.

Bones:  There are no suspicious osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Impacted stones within the common bile duct at the ampulla resulting in
gallbladder distention/wall edema and mild intrahepatic ductal dilation.  GI
consultation and ERCP are recommended. 

2.  Ground-glass nodules in the lungs for which followup Chest CT in 12 months
can be performed if clinically indicated.

3.  Indeterminate lesion within the right kidney could be further evaluated
with ultrasound.

4.  Fecal loading within the rectum with mild inflammation.

These findings were discussed with Dr. ___ by Dr. ___ @ 9:52 am by
telephone.

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with with CAD s/p DES to LAD ___, CTA ___
w incidental penetrating descending ulcers in thoracic aorta and infrarenal
ectasia up to 2.5 cm. No interval study. * CTA chest abdomen pelvis ___
(___): There is slight irregularity of the descending thoracic aorta with
small focal outpouchings likely representing penetrating ulcer. No evidence of
aortic dissection. Moderate atherosclerosis of the abdominal aorta with
infrarenal ectasia measuring up to 2.5 cm. Moderate centrilobular emphysema. 1
cm left thyroid gland nodule. Bilateral adrenal gland thickening.
Diverticulosis.

TECHNIQUE:  Abdomen and pelvis CTA: Non-contrast and multiphasic post-contrast

mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  No images available for direct comparison.  Outside report from

FINDINGS: 

CHEST

VASCULAR: Mild atherosclerotic disease is seen within the thoracic aorta and
carotid arteries.  Mild ectasia of the ascending thoracic aorta measuring up
to 3.2 cm.  Cardiac stent is present within the LAD.

LUNGS: 5 mm pleural based lung nodule in the right middle lobe (series 5,
image 69).  Moderate centrilobular emphysematous changes.  Minimal atelectasis
is noted in the lung bases.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA: Small hiatal hernia.  No mediastinal or hilar
lymphadenopathy.

PLEURA AND PERICARDIUM: There is no pleural or pericardial effusion.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES: 1.3 cm hypoenhancing nodule in the lower pole of the
left thyroid lobe.  No supraclavicular or axillary lymphadenopathy.

ABDOMEN

VASCULAR: Patent celiac artery, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior
mesenteric artery. There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.   There is severe
calcium burden in the abdominal aorta and great abdominal arteries.  There is
severe atherosclerotic disease of the infra abdominal aorta extending into the
bilateral common iliac arteries.  Redemonstration of known infrarenal ectasia
and multiple small focal outpouchings likely representing penetrating ulcers. 
For example best seen on series 5, image 110).

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is a 1 cm lesion in the hepatic segment 6 incompletely characterized on
this examination (series 6, image 75).  There is no evidence of intrahepatic
or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is within normal limits,
without stones or gallbladder wall thickening.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation. There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands demonstrate mild hyperplasia.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
Within the midpole of the left kidney, there is a 7 mm hypoenhancing rounded
lesion, too small to further characterize (series 5, image 110).  There is no
evidence of stones or hydronephrosis. There are no urothelial lesions in the
kidneys or ureters. There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber, wall thickness
and enhancement throughout.  Colonic diverticulosis without evidence of acute
diverticulitis.  Moderate colonic stool burden.  There is no evidence of
mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

RETROPERITONEUM: There is no evidence of retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
evidence of pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.  There is no free fluid in the
pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The visualized reproductive organs are unremarkable.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture. 
Mild degenerative changes are seen in the lumbar spine.

SOFT TISSUES: Tiny fat containing supraumbilical hernia.

IMPRESSION: 

Patient experienced a contrast reaction during this exam.  It is recommended
that this patient receive premedication prior to future studies containing
this contrast material. For details please see note in the electronic medical
record/OMR. The patient has received instruction on how to manage this event
at home.

Severe atherosclerotic disease of the infrarenal abdominal aorta extending
into the bilateral common iliac arteries.  Redemonstration of known infrarenal
ectasia multiple small focal outpouchings likely representing penetrating
ulcers as detailed above.

There is a 5 mm pleural based lung nodule in the right middle lobe.  For
incidentally detected single solid pulmonary nodule smaller than 6 mm, no CT
follow-up is recommended in a low-risk patient, and an optional CT in 12
months is recommended in a high-risk patient.See the ___ ___
Guidelines for the Management of Pulmonary Nodules Incidentally Detected on
CT" for comments and reference: ___

1 cm liver lesion in hepatic segment 6 is incompletely characterized on this
single-phase CT examination. This report should be correlated with the outside
CTA chest abdomen and pelvis performed at ___ on ___.

___ on ___ at 11:43 into the Department of Radiology critical
communications system for direct communication to the referring provider.

rupture or hemorrhage.

text:
PROCEDURE:
1.  Antegrade right-sided nephrostogram.
2.  Percutaneous repositioning of upper pigtail of right double-J stent.
3.  Replacement for new 8 ___ percutaneous nephrostomy tube catheter.

HISTORY:  ___ woman with history of retroperitoneal fibrosis, status
post bilateral double-J stenting (retrograde), has had successful trial of
capping of the covering right nephrostomy tube.  Request is to evaluate for
flow and possible catheter removal.

COMPARISON:  Reference is made to a prior study of ___.

PHYSICIANS: Dr ___ the procedure.

ANALGESIA:  Patient received 100 mcg of fentanyl during the procedure.  In
addition, patient received 5 cc of 1% buffered lidocaine along the
percutaneous access tract.

PROCEDURE NOTE IN DETAIL:  Informed consent was obtained outlining the risks
and benefits of the proposed procedure.  The patient was then brought to the
angiography department and placed prone on the imaging table.  A scout image
demonstrated expected position of the bilateral double-J stents and the
right-sided percutaneous nephrostomy tube. 
Injection of contrast demonstrated mild right-sided hydronephrosis and
demonstrated the pigtail of the double-J stent to be positioned within a mid
pole calix.  Approximately 15 cc of dilute contrast was injected which
resulted in opacification of the upper two-thirds of the stent; however, no
contrast was seen to pass into of bladder.  Findings were thought to be in
part related to the position of the upper pigtail of the double-J stent within
the mid pole calix.  
As a result, the existing nephrostomy catheter was removed and exchanged for a
5 ___ sheath.  Through the 5 ___ sheath, and following placement of a
safety wire, a radial jaw biopsy forceps was used to push the upper loop of
the double-J stent into the renal pelvis.  This resulted in improved position
of the upper loop of the double-J stent within the renal pelvis; however,
still no contrast was seen to pass through the stent into the bladder. 

The findings were discussed with Dr. ___ recommended replacement of the
right percutaneous nephrostomy tube for a continued trial of internal drainage
and possible repeat antegrade nephrostogram in ___ weeks.  Findings were
conveyed to the patient.  Via the existing safety wire, a new 8 ___ All
Purpose drain was advanced and formed within the renal pelvis.  Injection of
contrast demonstrated satisfactory position of the catheter, but persistent
lack of flow of contrast into the bladder.  
The catheter was secured to the skin using a 0 silk anchor suture, and sterile
dressing was applied.  The catheter was capped to allow continued trial of
internal drainage as per Dr. ___.  
The patient was discharged well in stable condition to the radiology care
unit. 
There were no early complications.

IMPRESSION:  Persistent mild-to-moderate hydronephrosis with vitualy no flow
of contrast through the existing double-J stent into the bladder despite
repositioning of the upper pigtail of the double-J stent from a mid pole calix
to a more optimal position within the renal pelvis.  
Findings were discussed with Dr. ___ a decision was made to maintain our
percutaneous renal access with a capped right-sided 8 ___ percutaneous
nephrostomy tube.

pancreatic ductal dilatation.  A subcentimeter hypodensity at the junction of
the pancreatic body and tail is not significantly changed compared with
___, and may represent volume averaging of fat (5:111).  There is no
peripancreatic stranding.

subcentimeter splenic hypodensity is too small to characterize, however is
stable since ___ (9:26).

metastatic disease, better characterized on MRI pelvis ___.  No
pathologic fracture.
3. A subcentimeter enhancing lesion arising from the lower pole of the left
kidney is not significantly changed, and may represent a small RCC.
4. Please refer to separate report of CT chest performed on the same day for
description of the thoracic findings.

NOTIFICATION:  The impression and recommendation above was entered by Dr.
___ on ___ at 15:55 into the Department of Radiology

phthisis bulbi, as demonstrated on the prior CT.  The right orbit is

lobes.  No evidence of hemorrhage.

ventricle, but no midline shift.

text:
ULTRASOUND-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY AND CLIP DEPLOYMENT RIGHT BREAST

INDICATION:  Irregular mass right breast.

Preprocedure timeout was performed and written informed consent was obtained.
The hypoechoic irregular mass in the right breast at 2 o'clock 4 cm from the
nipple was visualized.  The skin was prepped and draped in usual fashion.  4
cc of 1% lidocaine were used for anesthesia.  Under continuous ultrasound
guidance a 13-gauge coaxial guide was advanced to the margin of the lesion.
The stylet was exchanged for a 14-gauge spring-loaded Bard Monopty needle.
Multiple core biopsy specimens were obtained, placed in formalin, and
subsequently sent to Pathology.  An INRAD clip was then deployed at the biopsy
site.

The needle was removed.  There were no complications, and the patient left the
Department in satisfactory condition.

EXAMINATION:  RIGHT BREAST MRI-GUIDED VACUUM-ASSISTED CORE BIOPSY WITH CLIP
PLACEMENT AND POST PROCEDURE MAMMOGRAM

INDICATION:  ___ woman with a 0.5 cm focus of enhancement in the upper
slightly outer right breast at posterior depth.  Request for MRI-guided core
biopsy.

COMPARISON:  The relevant imaging was available for this procedure, including
breast MRI dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

Again seen is a focus of enhancement in the right upper slightly outer breast
at posterior depth.  This was targeted for biopsy.

PROCEDURE:  Consent: The procedure, risks, benefits and alternatives were
discussed with the patient and written informed consent was obtained.
Time-out certification:  Performed using three patient identifiers, with
confirmation of side and site.
Allergies / Medications:  The patient's medication list and history of
allergies were reviewed prior to beginning the procedure.

Clinicians: ___, MD ___, M.D. the procedure was supervised
by ___, M.D..
Description:
The patient was placed prone in the MRI breast coil.  The right breast was
placed in the grid device.  Multiplanar T1-weighted images were acquired on a
3.0 Tesla magnet including dynamic contrast-enhanced images obtained prior to,
during and after the uneventful intravenous administration of 0.1 mmol/kg of a
gadolinium agent.

Preprocedure imaging redemonstrated the lesion within the right breast, which
was targeted for MRI guided biopsy, using the lateral approach.  Using
standard aseptic technique and local anesthesia, under MRI guidance a 9-g
needle was advanced to the lesion, and obturator placed and appropriate
position confirmed.  Next, multiple core biopsies were obtained using a
vacuum-assisted biopsy device, while additional anesthesia was given. 
Post-biopsy images demonstrate accurate sampling and expected post-biopsy
changes at the target site.

Next, a titanium dumbbell-shaped clip was deployed at the biopsy site. 
Post-deployment images demonstrate expected post-biopsy changes.

Estimated blood loss:  < 5 cc.
Specimens: Sent to pathology.
Anesthesia:  ___ cc 1% lidocaine.
Complications: No immediate complications.
Post procedure diagnosis: Same.

Standard post care instructions were provided to the patient.

POST-PROCEDURE MAMMOGRAM:   CC and ML views confirm appropriate clip
placement.

IMPRESSION: 

Technically successful MRI-guided biopsy.

Once the pathology report is available and concordance is established an
addendum will be generated to this report, and the patient will be contacted
by the BreastCare Center provider with the results and the appropriate
recommendations.

As the Attending radiologist, I personally supervised the Fellow during the
key components of the above procedure and I reviewed and agree with the
Fellow's findings and dictation.

text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA HEAD WANDW/O C AND RECONS Q1213 CT HEAD

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with 2 wks of pain behind the left eye with
proptosis s/p fall who is still experiencing headache and pain behind the
right eye with blurred vision, double vision.  // THIS SHOULD BE CTV to look
for cavernous sinus venous thrombosis.

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous MDCT axial images were obtained through the brain
without contrast material.  Next, rapid axial imaging was performed through
the brain during the uneventful infusion of 70 mL of Omnipaque intravenous
contrast material in the venous phase. Three-dimensional angiographic volume
rendered and segmented images were then generated on a dedicated workstation.
This report is based on interpretation of all of these images.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Sequenced Acquisition 18.0 s, 18.0 cm; CTDIvol = 50.2 mGy (Head) DLP =
903.1 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 3.0 s, 23.9 cm; CTDIvol = 31.3 mGy (Head) DLP = 748.6
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Head) = 1,652 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  MRI and MRA brain performed ___ at 08:20

FINDINGS: 

CT HEAD WITHOUT CONTRAST:
There is a small amount of hyperdense hemorrhage dependently within the left
lateral ventricle occipital horn (02:17).  There is a small amount of
hyperdense hemorrhage within the fourth ventricle (02:10) and in the pre
pontine space (2:8).  The subsequent MRI was available for review at time of
final interpretation of this CTV.  In conjunction with the MRI there appears
to be isodense blood products in the left suprasellar cistern compatible with
chronic subarachnoid hemorrhage.  Additionally a small amount of isodense
fluid is seen in the right subdural space which was better appreciated on the
MRI compatible with a chronic subdural hematoma.  There is no significant mass
effect.  There is no evidence of infarction, acute hemorrhage, edema, or mass.
The ventricles and sulci are normal in size and configuration.

There is partial opacification of the left mastoid air cells and fluid within
the left petrous apex air cells.  The remaining visualized portion of the
paranasal sinuses,mastoid air cells, and middle ear cavities are clear. The
visualized portion of the orbits are unremarkable.

CTV HEAD:
The cavernous sinuses appear patent.  Evaluation of the arteries is limited by
venous phase of contrast.  The left PCOM aneurysm which was appreciated on the
MRI is not well visualized on the CTV due to obscuration by venous structures.
The dural venous sinuses are patent.

IMPRESSION:


1. Acute intraventricular hemorrhage with a small amount of layering blood in
the occipital horn of the left lateral ventricle, the fourth ventricle and the
pre pontine space.
2. Chronic subarachnoid hemorrhage in the left suprasellar cistern adjacent to
the left PCOM aneurysm which was better appreciated on the subsequent MRA.
3. Chronic tiny right subdural hematoma without significant mass effect.
4. Patent cavernous sinuses.

NOTIFICATION:  The findings were discussed by Dr. ___ with Dr. ___
on the telephone on ___ at 10:45 AM, 5 minutes after discovery of the
findings.

HISTORY:  Right lower quadrant pain.  

TECHNIQUE:  CT of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast.  Coronal and
sagittal reformations were reviewed.  Oral contrast was not administered at
the request of the ordering physician.

DLP:  508 mGy-cm.  

COMPARISON:  None 

FINDINGS:

LOWER CHEST:  The lungs are clear.  Cardiac apex is unremarkable.

ABDOMEN:  The liver enhances heterogenously, but there are no focal liver
lesions. The main portal vein is patent.  

The gallbladder contains no evidence of stones and there is no pericholecystic
fluid or gallbladder wall edema.  The spleen, pancreas and bilateral adrenal
glands are normal.  

Bilateral kidneys enhance and excrete contrast symmetrically without evidence
of hydronephrosis or suspicious renal masses.

The abdominal aorta is normal in course and caliber.  

There is no abdominal free fluid, free air, or lymphadenopathy.

The stomach, small, and large bowel are normal in course and caliber.  The
terminal ileum contains fecalized contents.  The appendix measures up to 12 mm
and has a hyperemic wall as well as surrounding stranding.  

PELVIS:  The bladder, uterus, and rectum are unremarkable.  There no adnexal
abnormalities.  There is no pelvic lymphadenopathy.  There is a trace amount
of simple pelvic free fluid. 

BONES:  There are no suspicious bony lesions.

IMPRESSION:

1. Uncomplicated acute appendicitis.  
2. Heterogenous liver enhancement. In the presence of normal LFTs, this is

INDICATION:  Right hip pain for 3 weeks not resolving, concern for infection
versus AVN, please aspirate right hip.

PHYSICIANS:  Dr. ___ (radiology fellow) and Dr. ___
___ (radiology attending) who was present throughout the procedure.

PROCEDURE DETAILS:

Following discussion of the risks, benefits and alternatives to the procedure,
informed written patient consent was obtained.  The patient was brought to the
fluoroscopy suite and placed supine on the table with the right hip in
internal rotation.  A preprocedure timeout was performed using three patient
identifiers.  The skin of the right hip was prepped and draped in the usual
sterile fashion.  Approximately 3 cc of 1% lidocaine was infiltrated into the
skin and subcutaneous tissues for local anesthesia.  Using an 18-gauge spinal
needle, under intermittent fluoroscopic guidance, we accessed the hip joint. 
We were able to aspirate approximately 0.5 cc of clear yellow fluid.  The
needle was repositioned on two occasions without further aspiration of fluid. 
Therefore, we injected a small amount of Optiray 320 to confirm
intra-articular positioning.  This was followed by injecting approximately 8
cc of Optiray and aspiration of about 1.5 cc of fluid.  The needle was
removed.  There were no immediate post-procedure complications.

RIGHT HIP ARTHROGRAM:

The initial scout film demonstrates mild degenerative changes in the right hip
joint.  Injection of iodinated contrast opacified the normal-appearing
appearing joint space.  No extra-articular contrast was seen.  Incidental note
is made of a pelvic phlebolith.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Technically successful aspiration of the right hip joint, only a small
amount of fluid could be aspirated, this was sent for culture.  The
re-aspirate was sent for cell count.
2.  Normal-appearing right hip arthrogram.

Lung bases appear clear.  Trace atelectasis or possibly a tiny bit of pleural
fluid is seen dependently.  The visualized portions of the heart and
pericardium are unremarkable.

Corresponding to the two drains which were present after the completion of the
CT-guided drainage ___, are regions of hypodensity in the region
of the pigtails and along the course of the drains, consistent with minimal
residual fluid about the catheters.  However, just to the left of the superior
aspect of the caudate (series 2, image 14) is a 3.2 x 2.5-cm air and fluid
collection which has grown since the prior examination where it was
approximately 1.2 x 1.5 cm.  The multiple locules at the porta hepatis which
contain both air and fluid appear fairly similar to the prior examination,
although the distribution has changed somewhat in the interval.  Within the
medial aspect of the posterior right lobe of the liver at the junctions of
segments VI and VII (dominantly segment VI; series 2, image 24) is a 13 x
15-mm loculation of fluid with a thick rind, but which has decreased in size
since the examination of the ___, where it measured 18 x 22 mm.  A trace of
subcapsular fluid is seen along the inferior hepatic tip, and appears
essentially new since the prior examination (series 2, image 38), but only
measures approximately 1 cm in its most prominent location posteriorly.

Subsegmental thrombus of the right posterior hepatic vein is again present,
unchanged since the prior examination.  Diffuse heterogeneous enhancement of
the liver is consistent with the diffuse infectious and inflammatory processes
which remains ongoing.

The gallbladder is nondistended but is significantly edematous.  The spleen
appears unremarkable, as does the adrenal glands, kidneys, and pancreas.  It
is notable that the edematous gallbladder wall and some of the locules of air
and fluid in the porta hepatis are immediately superior to the pancreatic

Post-surgical anatomy is present, status post Billroth II procedure.

Bowel loops appear otherwise grossly unremarkable.

approximately 12 cm in diameter, with an estimated volume at 900-1000 cc.  A

concerning for malignancy.

existing percutaneous hepatic drains.

aorta/diaphragmatic crus.  This location would be technically difficult for a
percutaneous drainage/aspiration.

3.  A new sliver of fluid is seen along the inferior/posterior tip of the
liver.  Its morphology suggests that this may be a small subcapsular
collection possibly due to inflammatory response of the liver.  Given its tiny
size at this time, it is unlikely that this may be currently be percutaneously
aspirated.

4.  Residual air and fluid locules at the porta hepatis.  While the appearance
of this is different than on the prior examinations, the overall degree of
collections is likely similar, allowing for differences in technique.

5.  Significantly distended bladder with a small locule of non-dependent air.

These findings were discussed with Dr. ___ at the time of interpretation, 3:30
p.m., ___.

EXAMINATION:  BILATERAL DIGITAL DIAGNOSTIC MAMMOGRAM INTERPRETED WITH CAD AND
BILATERAL BREAST ULTRASOUND

INDICATION:  ___ woman for follow-up of probably benign left breast
calcifications first seen on prior mammogram dated ___.  Also
complains of pain over outer central left breast.  Family history of breast
cancer in her mother at age ___.

COMPARISON:  Prior mammograms dated ___. 
Ultrasound ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Bilateral CC and MLO views, left MCCS, MMLS, ML, and right MCCS
and MMLS were obtained. Computer aided detection was utilized and assisted
with interpretation.  Bilateral targeted ultrasound was performed.

FINDINGS: 

BILATERAL MAMMOGRAM:

Tissue density: C- The breast tissue is heterogeneously dense which may
obscure detection of small masses.

LEFT BREAST: The previously noted group of microcalcifications in the lower
inner left breast have nearly completely resolved compared to the prior
studies.  There are no new suspicious grouped microcalcifications, dominant
masses, or architectural distortion.  Vascular calcifications are seen.

RIGHT BREAST: Loosely grouped benign-appearing microcalcifications are seen in
the upper outer right breast.  Additionally, a circumscribed round asymmetry
is seen in the outer breast posterior depth on the CC view that persists on
the spot magnification CC views.  This was not definitively visualized on the
other views.

LEFT BREAST ULTRASOUND:  Targeted ultrasound of the left breast was performed
from 2 o'clock to 4 o'clock over the patient's area of concern.  Small simple
cysts were noted without evidence of suspicious solid or cystic masses.

RIGHT BREAST ULTRASOUND: Targeted ultrasound of the right breast was performed
from 9 o'clock to 12 o'clock.  A 1.6 cm simple cyst was noted at 9 o'clock, 5
cm from the nipple, which corresponds to the mammographic finding.  There are
no suspicious solid or cystic masses.

IMPRESSION:


1. The previously noted group of microcalcifications in the lower inner left
breast have nearly completely resolved.  No additional followup is necessary
of these calcifications.
2. No imaging correlate for the area of clinical concern in the left breast.
3. No specific evidence for malignancy in either breast.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Age and risk appropriate screening.  Clinical followup for
left breast pain.

NOTIFICATION:  Findings and recommendation were reviewed with the patient who
agrees with the plan.

BI-RADS:  2 Benign.

appearance.  The cervical vessels are patent.  The visualized brain parenchyma
is unremarkable.  No lesions are noted within the ___-, or oropharynx.  The
airway is patent.  Evaluation of the lung apices again demonstrates multifocal
lung opacities in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe as seen on
prior chest CT.  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are identified
within the visualized osseous structures.  The visualized paranasal sinuses
are normally aerated.  There is a right-sided central venous catheter, which
is identified within the SVC.  The tip is excluded from the images.

IMPRESSION:

1.  No acute process noted within the neck.  Specifically, no soft tissue mass
identified as clinically questioned.

2.  Redemonstration of multifocal airspace opacities within the posterior
segment of the right upper lobe.

INDICATION:  ___ male with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
History of past small-bowel obstruction and multiple abdominal surgeries.

COMPARISON:  ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Contrast-enhanced MDCT-acquired axial images of the abdomen and
pelvis from the lung bases to the pubic symphysis.  Multiplanar reformatted
images were obtained.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:  Atelectasis in the dependent
portion of the lung bases is mild.  A small hiatal hernia is present.  The
liver is again noted to be shrunken and nodular in contour consistent with
cirrhosis.  Diffuse low attenuation of the liver is compatible with fatty
infiltration.  No focal hepatic lesion is identified. The gallbladder has been
removed. The intra- and extra- hepatic bile ducts are not dilated.

The spleen and right adrenal gland are normal.  The left adrenal gland shows
some mild thickening, unchanged from ___.  The kidneys enhance
and excrete contrast symmetrically. Stable hypodensity in the interpolar
aspect of the left kidney is too small to characterize.  The pancreas is
mildly atrophic, however, without focal lesion or ductal dilatation.

The small bowel is fluid filled and dilated measuring up to 4.7cm. The distal
ileum and colon are relatively collapsed. These findings are compatible with
small bowel obstruction. Note is made of a transition point in the lower
central abdomen (300B:34) in the region of post- surgical change, which
suggests the etiology is likely related to adhesion. There is perihepatic
ascites and fluid in the right paracolic gutter.

The anterior abdominal wall shows postoperative change. The abdominal aorta is
normal in caliber.  The celiac artery, SMA, ___ are patent.  Aortic
atherosclerotic calcification is moderately severe.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:  The rectum, sigmoid colon,
bladder, prostate, and seminal vesicles are unremarkable.  Multiple bowel
anastomoses are identified in the abdomen and pelvis.

BONE WINDOWS:  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion is identified. High-
grade compression fracture of L1 is stable.  Grade 1 anterolisthesis of L4 on
L5 is unchanged.  Note is made of small L5-S1 posterior disc bulge.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Findings compatible with small-bowel obstruction. Transition point
identifed in the lower central abdomen in the region of post surgical change,
suggesting the likley etiology to be adhesion. No free air. Small amount of
perihepatic and right paracolic free fluid.
2.  Cirrhosis.  Fatty infiltration of the liver.
3.  Stable high-grade compression fracture of L1 vertebral body.
4.  Hiatal hernia.  
5.  Scattered colonic diverticulosis without evidence of diverticulitis.

EXAMINATION:  Portable AP chest x-ray.

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with hypoxia and concern for PNA vs. pulmonary
edema secondary to acute diastolic heart failure  // Rule out PNA vs.
pulmonary edema

TECHNIQUE:  AP projection.

COMPARISON:  Portable AP chest x-ray obtained ___.

FINDINGS: 

There is stable position of left chest pacer device with associated dual leads
projecting over the right atrium and right ventricle. There is sepsis stable
position of right-sided PICC line with distal tip again not well visualized,
projecting over the approximate mid to lower SVC.

The cardiomediastinal silhouette is unchanged in appearance. The bilateral
hila are not well visualized.

There are worsening right greater than left alveolar consolidations, likely
signifying worsening asymmetric pulmonary edema ; in particular, the left lung
field appears improved with evidence of decreasing pulmonary edema, while the
right airspace opacities are worsened in comparison multiple prior
radiographs. Given this appearance and asymmetric nature of progression with
continued improvement of the left lung and continued worsening of the right,
there is also concern for superimposed multifocal pneumonia.

There are probably unchanged small bilateral pleural effusions. There is no
pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION: 

1. Continued worsening of right alveolar consolidations despite resolving
pulmonary edema on left, concerning for right multifocal pneumonia
superimposed on resolving pulmonary edema.
2. Probably unchanged small bilateral pleural effusions.

text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA TORSO

INDICATION:  A ___ man with a traumatic abdominal pain and chest pain,
evaluate for aortic dissection or aneurysm.

TECHNIQUE:  MDCT images were obtained through the abdomen and pelvis,
initially without contrast, and subsequently in the arterial phase after
administration of IV Omnipaque contrast.  Axial images were interpreted in
conjunction with coronal, sagittal, and MIP reformats.

DLP:  ___ MGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast upper twenty-___.

FINDINGS: 

CTA THORAX:
The main thoracic vessels are well opacified. The thoracic aorta is minimally
calcified, and normal in caliber without evidence of intramural hematoma or
dissection. The proximal aortic arch branch vessels are normal. The main,
right and left, and central pulmonary arterial branches are well opacified
without filling defect. There is no arteriovenous malformation.

CT THORAX:
There is mild cardiomegaly. Coronary artery calcifications are noted. There is
no pericardial effusion. Multiple mediastinal lymph nodes not meet CT size
criteria for lymphadenopathy. There is no axillary lymphadenopathy. There is
diffuse esophageal wall thickening, possibly secondary to recent vomiting,
clinically correlate.

Lung windows demonstrate a 5 mm right lower lobe nodule, not seen on prior CT
scans (series 2, image 60). Additionally, within the more superior right lower
lobe, there is a 4 mm subpleural nodule (series 2, image 47). Opacity at the
right greater than left lung base is consistent with dependent atelectasis.
There is a trace right layering simple pleural effusion.

CTA ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber and without evidence of aneurysmal
dilation or dissection. The celiac axis, SMA, bilateral renal arteries, and
___ are grossly patent. Atherosclerotic mural calcifications are seen
throughout the aorta and its major branches. Assessment of the venous
vasculature is limited by the timing of contrast.

ABDOMEN:

The liver is normal in appearance and without focal abnormality. The portal
venous system is patent. There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic
biliary dilatation.  Multiple layering gallstones are seen within the lumen of
a nondistended gallbladder, without evidence of wall thickening. Bilateral
adrenal hyperplasia is unchanged since ___. Multiple hypodense,
mildly complex cystic renal foci bilaterally measure up to 2.5 cm, generally
unchanged since ___. A 9 mm exophytic hyperdense posterior left renal
cyst is increased in apparent density in comparison to prior CT scans.  The
pancreas is normal.

There is free intraperitoneal air. Additionally, there are multiple foci of
retroperitoneal air, predominantly in the anterior pararenal space. There is
free intraperitoneal and retroperitoneal fluid which is mildly complex,
layering along the liver capsule and in the right pericolic gutter, as well as
within the anterior pararenal space and the right lateroconal fascia and
tracking into the pelvis. There is discontinuity of the medial wall of  the
D1/D2 segment of the duodenum with small focus of air adjacent which appears
extraluminal (2:110). In concert, these findings are concerning for duodenal
perforation involving the D1-D2 junction, with subsequent retroperitoneal and
intraperitoneal leakage of enteric air and fluid.

The stomach is grossly unremarkable in appearance.  Patient is status post
right colectomy and a surgical ileocolonic anastomosis is seen along the
anterior mid-abdomen. The involved loops of large and small bowel are closely
adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, which appears to have a small defect,
compatible with site of known enterocutaneous fistula. There is no evidence of
bowel wall thickening or obstruction. There is no retroperitoneal
lymphadenopathy by CT size criteria.

PELVIS:
The bladder, sigmoid colon, and rectum are grossly unremarkable.  Prominent
bilateral appearing external iliac chain lymph nodes measure up to 2 cm
(series 2, image 16).

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
There is mild degenerative joint change of the imaged thoracolumbar spine.
Alignment is normal. There is no concerning osteolytic or osteosclerotic
lesion identified.

IMPRESSION:


1. Findings are concerning for a perforated ulcer of the D1/D2 segment of the
duodenum with moderate amount of intraperitoneal and retroperitoneal mildly
complex free fluid, free air, and stranding.
2. No evidence of aortic dissection or aneurysm.
3. New right lower lobe lung nodules measuring up to 5 mm, as above. If
patient is low risk by ___ criteria guidelines, recommend followup
chest CT at 12 months. If high risk, recommend ___ month chest CT followup.
4. An exophytic 9 mm hyperdense posterior left renal cyst, increased in
apparent density in comparison to prior CT scans.  This may reflect a
hemorrhagic or proteinaceous cyst, but recommend MRI for further assessment.
5. Surgical ileocolonic anastomosis is noted in the anterior mid abdomen, with
close apposition of the involved bowel to a small defect in the anterior
abdominal wall, consistent with known enterocutaneous fistula.
6. Trace right pleural effusion.
7. Apparent diffuse esophageal thickening may be secondary to ongoing emesis
with esophagitis, but correlate clinically.
8. Cholelithiasis.
9. Stable bilateral adrenal hyperplasia.
10. Bilateral external iliac chain lymphadenopathy up to 20 mm, possibly
reactive, correlate clinically.

INDICATION:  ___ male with recurrent hepatocellular carcinoma, status
post transarterial chemoembolization of two right hepatic lesions, for
followup.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen ___ and multiple priors.

TECHNIQUE:  Non-contrast multidetector axial CT images of the abdomen were
obtained followed by contrast-enhanced axial CT images of the chest, abdomen
and pelvis with multiplanar reformatted images also submitted for review.

CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST:  No pathologically enlarged axillary, mediastinal or
hilar lymph nodes are observed.  The heart and great vessels are unremarkable
and there is no pericardial or pleural effusion.  Lung windows reveal a tiny
peripheral 2-mm pulmonary nodule at the right lung base (5:39), unchanged
since ___.  No other nodules, consolidations or masses are
identified.  The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally.

CT ABDOMEN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST: The patient is status post left lateral
segmentectomy and hepatic segment ___ resection. In comparison to the
pre-chemoembolization CT from ___, lipiodol chemoembolization material
has collected in the two largest lesions, one at the hepatic dome and one in
the posterior right lobe. Lipiodol has also collected within the smaller
lesion  in the periphery of hepatic segment VI. A tiny amount of lipiodol is
noted within only one of two previously seen adjacent lesions in segment
VII/VIII (2:15), however these lesions are inconspicuous on today's arterial
phase imaging. 

Arterial phase enhancement is noted along the left hepatic resection bed
(3:25), not seen on prior.  No other concerning foci of arterial enhancement
are present. An round 12-mm focus of early washout in segment V (5:62) is 
unchanged. 

 The gallbladder, spleen and adrenal glands appear normal.  The kidneys
enhance symmetrically and excrete contrast normally without hydronephrosis or
hydroureter.  Focal small calcifications in the pancreatic head and uncinate
process are consistent with focal chronic pancreatitis with mild main
pancreatic ductal dilatation again noted. Approximately two adjacent small
cystic uncinate process lesions measuring up to 12 mm are unchanged. 
Intra-abdominal loops of large and small bowel are of normal caliber and there
is no pneumoperitoneum or free fluid.  No pathologically enlarged mesenteric
or retroperitoneal lymph nodes are seen.  The abdominal aorta is of normal
caliber.

CT PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:  The rectum, sigmoid colon, distal ureters and
bladder appear normal.  There are no free pelvic fluid or pathologically
enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes.

Bone windows reveal no worrisome lytic or sclerotic lesions.  Multilevel
thoracolumbar spondylosis and facet arthropathy is observed.  There are
bilateral pars interarticularis defects at L5/S1 with perhaps minimal
associated anterolisthesis at this level.

IMPRESSION:
1. Successful chemoembolization of three right hepatic lesions. Only a tiny
amount of lipiodol is within 1 of 2 prevoiusly identified adjacent small right
hepatic lesions as described, though these are inconspicous on todays imaging.
Attention to these, and a small focus of early right hepatic washout, are
recommended on follow-up. 
2. Enhancement along the hepatectomy bed may represent recurrence as this is
not seen on priors. Attention is also recommedned on follow-up. 
3. Sequela of focal chornic poancreatitis with small cystic uncinate process
lesions, perhaps representing IPMN (intraductal pancreatic mucinous neoplasm).

INDICATION:  New diagnosis of CLL with splenomegaly and diffuse
lymphadenopathy.  Staging.

COMPARISON:  None available.

coronal reformats were generated.

FINDINGS:  For a complete report on the chest findings of this examination,
please refer to the chest CT, reported separately.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN:  There is a 2.4 x 2.3 cm hypodensity in the right hepatic
lobe which measures 11 Hounsfield units and likely represents a simple hepatic
cyst.  The liver otherwise enhances homogeneously with no evidence of
additional focal hepatic lesions.  There is no intra- or extra-hepatic biliary
ductal dilatation.  The gallbladder has been surgically removed.  Surgical
clips are seen in the right upper quadrant.  The adrenal glands are normal. 
The pancreas is atrophic but otherwise normal.  The spleen is enlarged,
measuring up to 22 cm.  The kidneys enhance symmetrically and excrete contrast
without evidence of hydronephrosis or renal masses.

The stomach is collapsed.  There is no evidence of small-bowel obstruction. 
Small and large bowel are grossly unremarkable.  The appendix is visualized
and there is no evidence of acute appendicitis.  The abdominal aorta is of
normal diameter.  The celiac axis, SMA, bilateral renal arteries, and ___ are
patent. There is no free fluid.  

There is significant lymphadenopathy involving the lymph nodes adjacent to the
gastrohepatic ligament and crural region measuring up to 18 mm (6:50, 53).
There are enlarged peripancreatic, portocaval and paracaval lymph nodes the
largest measuring 3.1 x 2.3 cm adjacent to the right hepatic artery (6:58,
59). Smaller paraaortic, retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes are also
noted (6:76, 82).  

Multiple midline fat containing ventral wall hernias are noted.

CT OF THE PELVIS:  The urinary bladder and terminal ureters are within normal
limits.  Coarse calcifications are seen in the prostate.  The rectum is
normal.  There is no evidence of pelvic free fluid.  There is a moderate-sized
fat-containing right inguinal hernia and possibly one on the left.  

There is lymphadenopathy involving the external and interal iliac chains
bilaterally the largest measuring 2.0 x 1.7 cm on the right (6:111). There are
also multiple bilateral inguinal lymph nodes the largest measuring up to 14
mm. 

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES: No suspicious osseous sclerotic or lytic lesions. 
Increased sclerosis at the posterior 9th rib on the left at the level of the
costovertebral articulation is likely secondary to prior trauma or DJD.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Splenomegaly.

2.  Extensive lymphadenopathy involving lymph nodes in the gastrohepatic
ligament, crura, peripancreatic, portocaval, paracaval, and paraortic regions
as well as the internal/external iliac chains and inguinal lymph nodes, as
described above.

There is marked enlargement of the heart.  There is a pericardial effusion

Lack of intravenous contrast limits evaluation of solid intraabdominal organs.

grossly unremarkable in appearance.

There is intrahepatic biliary ductal air.  Evaluation for intrahepatic biliary
ductal dilatation is limited given the presence of air in the noncontrast
technique.  PTC tube is in place.  There is also a pancreatic duct stent
noted.  No pancreatic ductal dilatation is noted.

Kidneys are grossly unremarkable in appearance.  Low attenuating lesion within
the upper pole of the right kidney likely represents a cyst.

There is a gallstone within the gallbladder.  Visualized bowel is grossly
unremarkable in appearance without bowel wall thickening, inflammatory
changes, or obstruction.

There is no ascites, however, there is diffuse stranding within the mesentery
throughout the abdomen.  This is most pronounced in the right flank and
paracolic gutter.  Multiple nonenlarged porta hepatis, gastrohepatic and

There is a left fat-containing inguinal hernia.

biliary ductal dilatation or pancreatic ductal dilatation.

3.  No evidence of a retroperitoneal or intraperitoneal hematoma.

4.  Diffuse mesenteric stranding pronounced in the right flank/right paracolic
gutter.

5.  Bilateral pleural effusions and small pericardial effusion.

6.  Foley balloon within the prostatic urethra.

Multiple foci of blooming in the basal ganglia bilateral most likely
representing chronic micro hemorrhagic changes in the setting of hypertensive
microangiopathy.

changes. Bilateral chronic basal ganglia and centrum semiovale lacunar
infarcts.
5 mm area of blooming artifact in the right pons with no surrounding T2 or

Partially empty sella.  Craniocervical junction is normal.

MRA BRAIN:
Mild atherosclerotic changes of the carotid siphons bilateral.  Short segment
loss of signal in the M1 segment of the left MCA measuring approximately 3 mm.
There is decreased signal in the left MCA branches distal to the stenotic
area.
3 mm loss of signal in the P2 segment of the left PCA suggests severe
stenosis.
Mild narrowing in the P1 segment of the left PCA.  Mild narrowing in the right
P1 segment.
Loss of signal in the distal/V4 segment of the right vertebral artery.

MRA NECK:
Mild atherosclerotic changes at the distal right common carotid and carotid
bulbs bilateral, but no marked ICA stenosis by NASCET criteria.  Dominant left
vertebral artery.  Diminutive right vertebral artery with loss of signal in
the distal right V3 and V4 segments.

More subtle small foci of high DWI signal most likely represent late acute to
subacute infarcts in the parietal areas bilateral.

microangiopathy.

3.  Multiple bilateral basal ganglia and centrum semiovale chronic lacunar
infarcts.

4.  Moderate periventricular and deep white matter T2 and FLAIR hyperintense
changes are most likely sequela of microangiopathy.

5.  5 mm area of blooming artifact in the right pons with no surrounding T2 or

6.  Short-segment loss of signal suggest severe stenosis of the M1 segment of
the left MCA.

7.  Short-segment loss of signal suggest severe stenosis in the P2 segment of
the left PCA.

8.  Multiple other areas of mild narrowing most likely secondary to
atherosclerotic disease.

9.  There is mild atherosclerotic changes in the distal right common carotid
artery and carotid bulbs bilateral, but there is no marked stenosis by NASCET
criteria.

10.  Dominant left vertebral artery.  Diminutive right vertebral artery with
loss of signal in the distal right V3 and V4 segments.

discovery of the findings.

HISTORY:  ___ female with left foot pain and swelling x 3 months. 
Patient with increased sclerosis of the navicular bone on recent radiographs,
concerning for avascular necrosis.

COMPARISON:  Left foot radiographs from ___

TECHNIQUE:  Multiplanar MR images of the left foot were acquired on a 1.5
Tesla magnet.  Sequences include coronal T1, T2, and inversion recovery
sequences, axial T1 and inversion recovery sequences, and sagittal T1 and
inversion recovery sequences.  No gadolinium contrast was administered.

MRI OF THE LEFT FOOT WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST:  

There is diffuse low signal intensity within the navicular bone on both T1 and
inversion recovery sequences.  Additionally, there appear to be hypointense
linear lines throughout the navicular bone coursing in both transverse and AP
directions, and some appear to reach the articular surface.  However, no
increased STIR signal is identified to suggest acute edema.   

Marrow edema, however, is seen within the adjacent bones of the midfoot
including the anterior process of the talus, lateral aspect of the cuboid, and
medial, middle and lateral cuneiforms.  Marrow signal intensity within the
metatarsals and calcaneus appear normal.  Significant edema is seen within the
soft tissues surrounding the midfoot on both the dorsal and plantar surfaces
(8:11 and 8:15).  There is a small effusion within the anterior subtalar joint
and a small synovial cyst arising from the anterior subtalar joint, measuring
5.5 mm (8:15).

Signal intensity within the musculature surrounding the left foot appears
normal.  Flexor and extensor tendons appear normal in signal intensity and
intact.  The partially imaged plantar fascia appears within normal limits.  No
osseous or soft tissue mass lesion is evident.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Low signal intensity within the navicular bone on all sequences with
multiple areas of linear hyperintensity. The absence of edema makes an acute
fracture unlikely.  The most likely etiology is a subacute fracture, possibly
a fracture superimposed on navicular osteonecrosis. 2.  Marrow edema within
other nearby midfoot bones, including the cuboid cuneiforms, and anterior
process of the calcaneus.
3.  Small effusion in the anterior subtalar joint with a small 5 mm subtalar
synovial cyst.
4.  Edema of the soft tissue surrounding the midfoot on dorsal and plantar
surfaces.

200mcg of fentanyl and 4 mg of midazolam throughout the total intra-service
time of ___ MIN during which the patient's hemodynamic parameters were
continuously monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse. 1% lidocaine
was injected in the skin and subcutaneous tissues overlying the access site.

CONTRAST: 0 ml of Optiray contrast.

PROCEDURE  1. Right internal jugular approach chest double lumen Port-a-cath
placement

PROCEDURE DETAILS: Following the explanation of the risks, benefits and
alternatives to the procedure, written informed consent was obtained from the
patient. The patient was then brought to the angiography suite and placed
supine on the exam table. A pre-procedure time-out was performed per ___
protocol. The upper chest was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.
Under continuous ultrasound guidance, the patent right internal jugular vein
was compressible and accessed using a micropuncture needle. Permanent
ultrasound images were obtained before and after intravenous access, which
confirmed vein patency. Subsequently a Nitinol wire was passed easily into the
right atrium using fluoroscopic guidance. The needle was exchanged for a
micropuncture sheath. The Nitinol wire was removed and a short ___ wire was
advanced to make appropriate measurements for catheter length. The ___ wire
was then passed distally into the IVC.
Next, attention was turned towards creation of a subcutaneous pocket over the
upper anterior chest wall. After instilling superficial and deeper local
anesthesia using lidocaine mixed with epinephrine, a 2.5 cm transverse
incision was made and a subcutaneous pocket was created by using blunt
dissection. There was good hemostasis. The double lumen port was then
connected to the catheter. The catheter was tunneled from the subcutaneous
pocket towards the venotomy site from where it was brought out using a
tunneling device. The port was then connected to the catheter and checks were
made for any leakage by accessing the diaphragm using a non-coring ___
needle. No leaks were found.
The port was then placed in the subcutaneous pocket and secured with ___
prolene sutures on either side. The venotomy tract was dilated using the
introducer of the peel-away sheath supplied. Following this, the peel-away
sheath was placed over the ___ wire through which the port was threaded into
the right side of the heart with the tip in the right atrium. The sheath was
then peeled away.
The subcutaneous pocket was irrigated and closed in layers with ___
interrupted and ___ subcuticular continuous Vicryl sutures. Steri-strips were
used to close the venotomy incision site. Steri-Strips were applied over the
sutures. Final spot fluoroscopic image demonstrating good alignment of the
catheter and no kinking. The tip is in the right atrium.
The port was accessed using two non coring ___ needles and could be
aspirated and flushed easily. Sterile dressings were applied. The patient
tolerated the procedure well without immediate complication. The port was left
accessed as requested.

FINDINGS: 

Patent right internal jugular vein.
Final fluoroscopic image showing port with catheter tip terminating in the
right atrium.

IMPRESSION: 

Successful placement of a double lumen chest Port-a-cath via the right
internal jugular venous approach.
The tip of the catheter terminates in the right atrium. The catheter is ready
for use.
The port was left accesses as requested.

INDICATION:  ___ woman with a reported type B aortic dissection.

TECHNIQUE:  CT images of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis were uploaded from an
outside facility for secondary interpretation.  Supplied images include axial,
sagittal, coronal noncontrast and contrast-enhanced CT images.

DOSE:  Dose report from outside facility:

-Total exam DLP 1179.92 mGy-cm.
-CTDIvol 66.04 mGy.

COMPARISON:  None available.

FINDINGS: 

CTA TORSO: The aorta and major thoracic vessels are well opacified. The aorta
demonstrates mild fusiform dilation involving its proximal descending portion
measuring up to 4.0 cm in diameter (see series 5 image 230).  There are 2
focal outpouchings into the wall of the descending thoracic aorta concerning
for penetrating atherosclerotic ulcers.  A wide-mouthed, 17 mm wide such focus
at the posterior aspect of the proximal descending thoracic aorta is noted
(series 5, image 160 as well as series 606 image 81)).  Distal to this, there
is a 7 x 6 mm focal penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer arising from the
posteromedial mid descending thoracic aorta (series 5, image 258, as well as
series 606, image 79).  Surrounding crescentic hyperdensity is concerning for
type B  intramural hematoma along the posterior aspect of the surrounding
segment of descending thoracic aorta (see series 2 images 35 and 37),
measuring up to 1.1 cm in width and 6 mm in length (series 6 and 7, image 72).

Pulmonary artery is well opacified.  There is the linear intraluminal filling
defect within the segmental branches of the pulmonary artery supplying the
posterior and medial basilar segments of the right lower lobe (for example see
series 5, image 275), consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. Additional
similar linear filling defect is seen in a PA branch supplying the medial
basilar segment of the left lower lobe is noted, as well as the right middle
lobe (see series 5 image 266) also likely representing acute pulmonary emboli
(series 5, image 280). The remaining branches of the imaged pulmonary artery
are well opacified to the subsegmental level.  No additional pulmonary
embolism is identified.  No arteriovenous malformation is seen.

The abdominal aorta is heavily calcified and tortuous but normal in caliber. 
Major proximal tributaries are patent, including the celiac axis and major
branches such as the common hepatic artery, left gastric artery, and splenic
artery, the latter which is heavily calcified but patent.  The SMA is patent
throughout its visualized course.  Paired bilateral renal arteries are patent.
The ___ is patent.  The iliac arterial vasculature including the common,
external, and internal iliac arteries are heavily calcified but patent without
high-grade stenosis.  The visualized portions of the femoral arterial
vasculature is patent, however note is made of a very focal dissection of the
right proximal femoral artery (series 5, image 826).

CT CHEST: The thyroid gland is obscured by streak artifact, grossly within
normal limits.  The esophagus is within normal limits.  Scattered multi
station mediastinal lymph nodes are abnormally numerous and prominent (for
example there is a 10 mm node in the subcarinal station (series 5, image 233);
there is a 10 mm node at the low right paratracheal station (5, 169) and a 10
mm high right paratracheal lymph node (5, 135).  No axillary, internal
mammary, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.  The heart is moderately globally
enlarged.  There is a trace pericardial effusion.  Right atrial and
ventricular leads from a left chest cardiac device, within normal limits. 
Major airways are patent bilaterally.  There is moderate apical predominant
centrilobular emphysema.  There is a diffuse subpleural reticular interstitial
abnormality worse at the lung bases.  There is evidence of scarring
atelectasis at the left lung base.  Fissural  thickening is noted involving
the inferior aspect of the left major fissure.  There are distal micronodules
and ___ distribution seen worst in the peripheral right
upper lobe (see series 5, image 23).  No focal lung consolidation elsewhere. 
No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

CT ABDOMEN: Circumscribed hypodensity in segment 8 centrally is likely a
simple hepatic cyst biliary hamartoma, measuring 8 mm.  Otherwise, the liver
demonstrates a normal homogeneous appearance on this arterial phase study. 
Portal vein cannot be assessed due to contrast bolus timing.  There is no
intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation.  There is focal fusiform ectasia of the
proximal extrahepatic common duct measuring up to 15 mm.  The gallbladder
surgically absent.  More distal extrahepatic common duct is unremarkable
within the pancreatic head, normal in caliber.  There is severe, near complete
fatty replacement of the pancreas.  No peripancreatic stranding or ductal
dilation is apparent.  There is a nodular right adrenal gland without a
discrete, measurable nodule.  Left adrenal gland within normal limits.  There
is a 3.1 cm simple cyst in the right kidney; on the left, simple cyst measures
up to 1.2 cm.  Additional bilateral subcentimeter hypodensities elsewhere in
the bilateral kidneys are too small to characterize.  The kidneys otherwise
enhance normally and symmetrically.  No hydronephrosis.  No hydroureter.  The
stomach and duodenum are unremarkable.  Non-dilated small bowel loops are
normal in course and caliber without evidence of wall thickening or
obstruction.  There is diffuse colonic diverticulosis with retained contrast
seen.  Colon is otherwise unremarkable.  Appendix not well seen, however no
secondary signs of appendicitis.

Scattered retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes not individually
enlarged.  No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

CT PELVIS: The bladder is within normal limits.  No focal worrisome uterine or
adnexal abnormality.  No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.  Pelvic floor
descent is noted.  No free pelvic fluid.

MUSCULOSKELETAL: There is a left chest cardiac device, unremarkable.  No
worrisome focal subcutaneous or soft tissue abnormality in the imaged chest or
abdominopelvic wall.  Evidence of prior left posterior rib resections are
noted involving ribs 8 and 9.  There are severe multilevel thoracic and lumbar
spine degenerative changes, including multilevel confluent anterior
osteophytes and multilevel disc height loss worst at.  No evidence of acute
fracture.  No worrisome focal osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:


1. Two (2) foci of acute penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer in the descending
thoracic aorta posteriorly.  The more distal of these in the mid thoracic
aorta demonstrates surrounding type B intramural hematoma measuring up to 6 cm
in length, as above.
2. Bilateral acute segmental pulmonary emboli to the basilar segments of the
right and left lobes, as well as the right middle lobe, as above.
3. Focal short-segment dissection of the proximal right femoral artery.
4. ___ micro nodules in the peripheral right upper lobe are likely
infectious or inflammatory.
5. Fusiform dilation of the descending thoracic aorta measuring 4 cm.
6. Severe, diffuse fatty atrophy of the pancreas.
7. Severe background thoracoabdominal tortuosity and atherosclerotic
calcification extending into the iliac and femoral arterial vessels, as above.
8. Left lower lobe scarring atelectasis, as well as left eighth and ninth rib
defects posteriorly, consistent with prior surgery.
9. Right paratracheal enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes are likely reactive in
nature.
10. Colonic diverticulosis.  Severe thoracolumbar spine degenerative changes. 
Post cholecystectomy fusiform extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation.  Bilateral
simple renal cysts.  Other incidental findings, as above.

NOTIFICATION:  The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by
___, M.D. in person on ___ at 3:49 pm, 1 minutes after
discovery of the findings.

text:
INDICATION:  Motor vehicle crash.

COMPARISONS:  None.

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial MDCT images were obtained through the chest,
abdomen, and pelvis after the administration of IV contrast.  Sagittal and
coronal reformatted images were obtained and reviewed.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:  The thyroid is normal.  There is no axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy.  The heart is normal in size.  There is no pericardial
effusion.  The thoracic aorta is normal in course and caliber without evidence
of injury.

The airways are patent to the subsegmental levels.  There is mild atelectasis
along the right minor fissure (2, 39).  The lungs are otherwise clear.  There
are no focal consolidations to suggest contusion.  There is no pleural
effusion or pneumothorax.

ABDOMEN:  The liver is normal in shape and contour.  There are no focal
hepatic lesions.  There is no intra- or extra-hepatic biliary duct dilation. 
There is no evidence of a liver injury.  The portal vein is patent.  The
gallbladder is not distended and normal in appearance.  The spleen is
unremarkable without evidence of a splenic injury.  The pancreas is normal. 
The bilateral adrenal glands and kidneys are normal without evidence of focal
injuries.  There is no perinephric fluid collection.  There is no
hydronephrosis.  The kidneys enhance and excrete contrast symmetrically.  The
stomach is distended and filled with food.  The remainder of the small bowel
is unremarkable without evidence of obstruction.  There is no free air or free
fluid in the abdomen.  There is a paucity of mesenteric fat, which makes
evaluation for stranding difficult.  The abdominal vasculature is normal in
course and caliber.  There is no abdominal or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS:  The large bowel is normal in course and caliber.  There is a moderate
fecal load.  There is no evidence of injury or obstruction.  The bladder and
prostate are normal.  There is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.  There
is no free fluid in the pelvis.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  There is a non-displaced fracture through the right
clavicle (2, 6).  No other fracture is identified.  Vertebral body height and
alignment is normal.  There is a non-aggressive lucent lesion in the posterior
right second rib, which measures 5.9 x 2.3 cm.  There is soft tissue density
within the lesion.  There is no evidence of a pathologic fracture or acute
fracture through the lesion.

IMPRESSION:
1.  No evidence of an intra-thoracic or intra-abdominal traumatic injury.
1.  Non-displaced right clavicle fracture.
2.  Lucent right rib lesion, which likely is fibrous dysplasia or an
aneurysmal bone cyst.  Could further evaluate with a non-emergent MRI.

BILATERAL DIGITAL DIAGNOSTIC MAMMOGRAM WITH CAD AND RIGHT BREAST ULTRASOUND

CLINICAL INDICATION:  Right breast lump in the lower inner quadrant, family
history of breast cancer.

No comparison studies.  Baseline mammogram.

The breast tissues are fatty with some scattered fibroglandular tissue.  No
dominant mass, area of architectural distortion or cluster of suspicious
microcalcification is seen.  A more focal 3-mm nodular density in the upper
outer right breast appears to have a fatty notch and likely represents an
intramammary lymph node.

This study was interpreted with the aid of the  ICAD computer-aided detection
system.

Ultrasound of the right breast from 3 to 6 o'clock 1 to 8 cm from the nipple
in the area of concern as indicated by the patient was performed.  No solid
suspicious mass or macrocyst is seen.

IMPRESSION:

1.  No focal mammographic or ultrasonographic abnormality identified in the
right breast in the area of concern as indicated by the patient.  Any decision
