INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with ESBL UTI who needs 1 week of ertapenem at
home; requested PICC but team has had difficulty in past and has recommended
___ placement going forward  // placement of PICC

COMPARISON:  None.

TECHNIQUE:  OPERATORS: Dr. ___ radiologist performed the
procedure.
ANESTHESIA: 1% lidocaine was injected in the skin and subcutaneous tissues
overlying the access site.
MEDICATIONS: 1% local lidocaine
CONTRAST: No contrast.
FLUOROSCOPY TIME AND DOSE:  0.3 min, 1 mGy

PROCEDURE:
1. Single lumen PICC placement through the left basilic vein.

PROCEDURE DETAILS: Using sterile technique and local anesthesia, the left
basilic vein was punctured under direct ultrasound guidance using a
micropuncture set. Permanent ultrasound images were obtained before and after
intravenous access, which confirmed vein patency. A peel-away sheath was then
placed over a guidewire. The guidewire was then advanced into the superior
vena cava using fluoroscopic guidance. A single lumen PIC line measuring 45 cm
in length was then placed through the peel-away sheath with its tip positioned
in the distal SVC under fluoroscopic guidance. Position of the catheter was
confirmed by a fluoroscopic spot film of the chest. The peel-away sheath and
guidewire were then removed. The catheter was secured to the skin, flushed,
and a sterile dressing applied.
The patient tolerated the procedure well. There were no immediate
complications.

FINDINGS: 

1.  The accessed vein was patent and compressible.
2.  Basilicvein approach single lumen left PICC with tip in the distal SVC.

IMPRESSION: 

Successful placement of a left 45 cm basilic approach single lumen PowerPICC
with tip in the distal SVC.  The line is ready to use.

CTA OF THE CHEST

HISTORY:  ___ male with follicular lymphoma, AIDS, with new shortness
of breath x2 days.  Assess for PE, pneumonia, CHF.

COMPARISONS:  CT, ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Following the administration of intravenous contrast, MDCT axial
images were acquired from the thoracic inlet to the upper abdomen.  Coronal,
sagittal, and oblique reformatted images were then obtained.

CT OF THE CHEST WITHOUT AND WITH IV CONTRAST:  The heart is moderately
enlarged.  A trace pericardial effusion is evident.  Calcification of the left
anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries is noted.
The pulmonary artery is slightly enlarged in diameter measuring up to 3.2 cm.
A subsegmental filling defect within an arterial branch to the right middle
lobe is evident (4:53, 503B:69).  There are no filling defects within the
central or segmental pulmonary arterial vasculature.  Small subcentimeter
mediastinal lymph nodes are evident, however, none meet criteria for pathology
by CT.  Several clips are evident in the left axillary region.

Large pleural effusions, right greater than left, new compared to the CT of
___.  Associated atelectasis is additionally noted.  Mild diffuse
bronchiectasis is probably unchanged.  Several patchy infiltrates are noted
within the left upper and left lower lobes, also new compared to the ___ study.  Several peripheral areas of nondependent ground-glass change are
noted anteriorly within the right middle lobe and lingula.  Small pleural
calcification is noted near the right lung base (2:41). Several defects within
the ventral abdominal wall are probably stable, but incompletely imaged on
today's evaluation. Otherwise, the imaged portions of the upper abdomen appear
unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Possible right middle lobe single subsegmental pulmonary embolus.
2.  Patchy areas of consolidation throughout the left lung, most notably at
the base, concerning for infection.  Atypical and fungal infections could be
considered.
3.  New large pleural effusions with associated atelectasis suggestive of
volume overload and pulmonary edema.
4.  Evidence of chronic lung disease including bronchiectasis and mild
interstial abnormality.
5.  Enlarged pulmonary artery consistent with pulmonary hypertension.
6.  Likely previous asbestos exposure.

report of CT chest performed on the same day.

stenosis, occlusion, or aneurysmal dilatation of the intracranial arteries. 
The vertebral arteries and internal carotid arteries within the neck are
tortuous bilaterally, but patent throughout their course.

These findings may represent an atypical appearing hemangioma, other
considerations include Paget's disease, or metastasis.

head and neck.

3.  No evidence of cerebral infarction.

4.  Diffuse white matter T2 hyperintensity consistent with chronic small
vessel ischemia.

MEDIASTINUM:  Mediastinal lymph nodes are not enlarged.

HILA:  Hilar lymph nodes are not enlarged.

HEART:  The heart is not enlarged and there is extensive coronary arterial
calcification.  There is no pericardial effusion.

VESSELS:  Vascular configuration is conventional.  Aortic caliber is normal. 
The main, right, and left pulmonary arteries are normal caliber.  A tunneled
left IJ central venous catheter terminates at the cavoatrial junction.

PULMONARY PARENCHYMA:  There is no evidence of infection or malignancy.

CHEST WALL AND BONES: There is extensive heterogeneity of the visualized

level.

At L5-S1, there is a diffuse disc bulge and a central disc protrusion with
mild central stenosis and moderate right foraminal narrowing with nerve root
impingement.  Central stenosis has progressed slightly since the prior
examination.

The pre- and paravertebral soft tissues are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

Apparent progression of lumbar stenosis from L3 through S1.

text:
EXAMINATION:  Ultrasound-guided percutaneous cholecystostomy

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with cholecystitis// placement of perc chole

COMPARISON:  Correlation with CT and ultrasound from ___.

PROCEDURE:  Ultrasound-guided drainage of the gallbladder.

OPERATORS:  Dr. ___, radiology trainee and Dr. ___
radiologist. Dr. ___ supervised the trainee during the key
components of the procedure and reviewed and agrees with the trainee's
findings.

TECHNIQUE:  The risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure were
explained to the patient. After a detailed discussion, informed written
consent was obtained. A pre-procedure timeout using three patient identifiers
was performed per ___ protocol.

The patient was placed in a supine position on the US scan table. Limited
preprocedure ultrasound was performed to localize the gallbladder. Based on
the ultrasound findings an appropriate skin entry site percutaneous
cholecystostomy was chosen. The site was marked. Local anesthesia was
administered with 1% Lidocaine solution.

Using continuous sonographic guidance, ___ Exodus drainage catheter was
advanced via trocar technique into the gallbladder. A sample of fluid was
aspirated, confirming catheter position within the gallbladder. The pigtail
was deployed. The position of the pigtail was confirmed within the gallbladder
via ultrasound.

Approximately 60 cc of bilious fluid was drained with a sample sent for
microbiology evaluation. The catheter was secured by a StatLock. The catheter
was attached to a drainage bag.  Sterile dressing was applied.

The procedure was tolerated well, and there were no immediate post-procedural
complications.

SEDATION:  Moderate sedation was provided by administering divided doses of
0.5 mg Versed and 50 mcg fentanyl throughout the total intra-service time of
20 minutes during which patient's hemodynamic parameters were continuously
monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse.

FINDINGS: 

Following gentle aspiration, the majority of the fluid within the gallbladder
had been removed.  The pigtail drain appeared to be in good positions.

IMPRESSION: 

Successful US-guided placement of ___ pigtail catheter into the
gallbladder. Samples sent for microbiology evaluation.

CT OF THE CHEST

INDICATIONS:  Long-standing diagnosis of COPD with recurrent exacerbations
refractory to treatment.  Evaluation of lung parenchyma requested.

COMPARISONS:  No prior dedicated CT imaging.  Radiographs from two days
earlier are available.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial non-contrast CT images were obtained with interstitial lung
disease protocol including expiratory and thin slice imaging including limited
images with prone positioning.

FINDINGS:  A scout view shows barium throughout the visualized portion of the
colon.  The heart is normal in size.  Coronary artery calcifications are
present.  Lipomatous hypertrophy of the interatrial septum is noted.  There is
no pleural or pericardial effusion.  There are no enlarged lymph nodes.  

Aside from areas of minimal atelectasis or scarring, including a small
ill-defined nodule in the right upper lobe of 3 mm (104:63), the lungs are
essentially clear.  There is no evidence for substantial emphysema or
intrinsic interstitial lung disease.  With expiration, there is an appropriate
increase in the attenuation of the lung parenchyma with no substantial focal
regions of air trapping identified.  There is mild physiological relative
narrowing of the trachea and central airways with expiration.

Limited views of the upper abdomen are unremarkable.

BONE WINDOWS:  There are no suspicious lytic or blastic lesions.  Small
marginal osteophytes are present along interspaces throughout the thoracic
spine.  

IMPRESSION:

1.  Small lung nodule.  In the absence of risk factors for malignancy such as
smoking or a known prior history of malignancy, follow-up chest CT imaging is
probably unnecessary according to the ___ society guidelines. 
Otherwise, follow-up chest CT imaging could be considered in one year.

2.  No definite pulmonary abnormality.

EXAMINATION:  CT INTERVENTIONAL PROCEDURE

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with abdominal/pelvic sidewall abscess for
drainage.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen pelvis of ___.

PROCEDURE:  CT-guided drainage of the left pelvic sidewall collection.

OPERATORS:  Dr. ___ resident and Dr. ___
radiologist. Dr. ___ supervised the trainee during the key
components of the procedure and reviewed and agrees with the trainee's
findings.

TECHNIQUE:  The risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure were
explained to the patient.  After a detailed discussion, informed written
consent was obtained.  A pre-procedure timeout using three patient identifiers
was performed per ___ protocol.

The patient was placed in a supine position on the CT scan table. Limited
preprocedure CT scan was performed to localize the collection.  Based on the
CT findings an appropriate skin entry site for the drain placement was chosen.
The site was marked.  Local anesthesia was administered with 1% Lidocaine
solution.

Using intermittent CT fluoroscopic guidance, an 18-G ___ needle was
inserted into the collection.  A sample of fluid was aspirated, confirming
needle position within the collection.  0.038 ___ wire was placed through
the needle and needle was removed. This was followed by placement of ___
Exodus pigtail catheter into the collection. The plastic stiffener and the
wire were removed.  The pigtail was deployed. The position of the pigtail was
confirmed within the collection via CT fluoroscopy.

Approximately 50 cc of reddish, purulent fluid was aspirated with a sample
sent for microbiology evaluation. The catheter was secured by a Flexitrack.
The catheter was attached to suction bulb. Sterile dressing was applied.

The procedure was tolerated well, and there were no immediate post-procedural
complications.

 Total DLP (Body) = 817 mGy-cm.

SEDATION:  Moderate sedation was provided by administering divided doses of
0.5 mg Versed and 25 mcg fentanyl throughout the total intra-service time of
15 minutes during which patient's hemodynamic parameters were continuously
monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse.

FINDINGS: 

1.  Re-demonstration of the known left pelvic sidewall collection.  Notably,
the collection now contains contrast, either oral or intravenous from the CT
abdomen pelvis from the prior day.  Findings are concerning for underlying
ureteral or bowel injury, causing communication with the collection.

2.  Fluid in the right lower quadrant is free, and was not targeted for
drainage.

Successful CT-guided placement of an ___ pigtail catheter into the left
pelvic sidewall collection.  50 cc of reddish, purulent fluid were aspirated. 
Samples were sent for microbiology and laboratory evaluation, per the ordering
clinicians' request.

PROCEDURES:
1.  Percutaneous pullback cholangiogram over a guidewire through existing
percutaneous access to the right anterior and right posterior biliary ducts.
2.  Placement of metallic stents into the common bile duct (10 x 94 mm), right
anterior intrahepatic duct (6 x 60 mm) and right posterior intrahepatic duct
(6 x 40 mm).
3.  Balloon dilatation post-stenting up to 5 mm.

CLINICAL INDICATION:  ___ woman with high-grade malignant biliary
obstruction at the junction of the right posterior and right anterior biliary
ductal system.  The patient currently presents with two percutaneous biliary
drainage catheters in place, both through the right biliary ducts.  Recent
brushings yielded adenocarcinoma specimens.

Informed consent for the procedure was obtained after risks, benefits, and
potential complications had been discussed.  The patient was placed on the
angiographic table in supine position and skin of the abdominal wall around
insertion site of existing percutaneous biliary drainage catheters was prepped
and draped in a sterile fashion.  Timeout protocol was carried out prior to
the procedure according to the ___ policy.

BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIMICROBIAL PROPHYLAXIS:  3 g Unasyn IV.

ANESTHESIA:  General endotracheal.

Existing percutaneous biliary drainage catheters were divided several
centimeters proximal to the skin entry.  Under fluoroscopic visualization,
0.035 ___ guidewires were inserted into each anchor percutaneous biliary
drainage catheter.  Existing catheters were removed.  7 ___ ___ 25-cm
long sheaths were advanced over the ___ guidewires into the common bile
duct and past the major duodenal papilla.  Pullback cholangiograms were
obtained over the two guidewires.  A 10 x 94 mm metallic Wallstent was
initially inserted across the entire length of the common bile duct and into
the distal segment of the right anterior intrahepatic biliary duct.  The
second metallic stent, 6 x 60 mm, was deployed across the right posterior
intrahepatic duct and into the proximal segment of the common bile duct.  The
third 6 x 40 mm biliary stent was deployed in a telescoping fashion into the
proximal right anterior intrahepatic bile duct.  Pullback cholangiogram
through the 6 ___ sheath demonstrated brisk flow of secretions and contrast
material across all three stents.  Additional balloon dilatation by 5 x 40 mm
balloon was performed through the 6-mm profile stents to consolidate and
stabilize stent placement.  At the conclusion of the procedure, a ___ 6
___ ___ catheter was placed across over the wire through the anterior
right-sided biliary ductal access and into the duodenum.  The purpose of the
catheter placement is to maintain access for percutaneous cholangiogram the
following day.  After patency of the metallic stents is confirmed, 6 ___
___ catheter can be removed.

Dr. ___, the attending physician, supervised this interventional
procedure.

CONCLUSIONS:
1.  Moderate stricture at the confluence of the right anterior and right
posterior biliary ducts was corroborated on percutaneous pullback
cholangiogram through existing anterior and posterior biliary ductal access in
the right hepatic lobe.

2.  Uncomplicated placement of three metallic Wallstents traversing the entire
length of the common bile duct and distal right anterior and right posterior
intrahepatic ducts, respectively.  Balloon dilatation of the smaller profile
stents up to 5 mm consolidated placements of the central stents.

3.  Temporary placement of 6 ___ ___ access catheter through
the anterior ductal system, the sole purpose of which is access for
percutaneous cholangiogram to confirm metallic stent patency the day after the
procedure.

INDICATION:  ___ -year-old man with status post liver transplant (___) presenting with fever and leukocytosis.

TECHNIQUE:  Gray scale, color and spectral Doppler evaluation of the abdomen
was performed.

COMPARISON:  Liver ultrasound ___.

FINDINGS: 

Liver:  The patient is status post liver transplant.  In the region of the
porta hepatis, but possibly intraparenchymal, there is a 3.3 x 5.2 x 2.5 cm
hypoechoic structure with a thin septation which does not demonstrate internal
vascularity, new from ___.  There is no ascites.
Bile ducts:  There is no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation.  The common
hepatic duct measures 6 mm.
Gallbladder:  The gallbladder is surgically absent.
Pancreas:  Imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits, with
portions of the pancreatic tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.
Spleen:  The spleen demonstrates normal echotexture, and measures 12.0 cm.
Kidneys:  The right kidney measures 10.2 cm.

There are bilateral pleural effusions.

Doppler evaluation:

The main portal vein is patent, with flow in the appropriate direction.
Main portal vein velocity is 36.5 cm/sec.
Right and left portal veins are patent, with antegrade flow.

The main hepatic artery is patent, with appropriate waveform.
Right, middle and left hepatic veins are patent, with appropriate waveforms.
Splenic vein and superior mesenteric vein are patent, with antegrade flow.

IMPRESSION:


1. Patent hepatic vasculature.
2. 5.2 x 3.3 cm hypoechoic structure with a thin septation in the region of
the porta hepatis but appearing intraparenchymal in the liver, finding may
represent a postoperative collection or vascular structure although no
internal flow is seen.  This can be further evaluated with abdominal CT or
MRI.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Abdominal CT or MRI to evaluate hypoechoic structure

text:
HISTORY:  ___ male with difficulty walking, status post fall.  Patient
has history of tuberous sclerosis, status post fusion of the T-spine. Evaluate
for any injury.

No prior studies are available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images of the thoracic spine were obtained
without IV contrast.  Coronal and sagittal reformations were obtained.

FINDINGS:  There are paired spinal fusion rods spanning from approximately the
level of T4 to approximately L3.  No definite acute hardware-related
complication is identified, though assessment is limited due to extensive
streak artifact. However, the chronicity of this finding is unknown, and
correlation with outside studies is recommended.

There is severe scoliosis, with a rightward curvature of the thoracic spine,
and a leftward curvature of the lumbar spine and distortion of the anatomy.
However, no fracture is identified. The vertebral body heights appear to be
well maintained.  There are scattered sclerotic foci throughout the vertebral
bodies, which are of uncertain etiology, but may reflect diffuse osseous
changes related to tuberous sclerosis.

Assessment of the spinal canal is limited due to extensive streak artifact
from adjacent hardware.

Within the visualized lungs, there is minimal paraseptal blebs within the
right upper lobe.

Within the visualized abdomen, there are numerous hypodense lesions involving
bilateral kidneys, with scattered calcifications seen within the collecting
systems bilaterally, which may reflect stones.  The hypodense lesions are all
incompletely characterized, but given the history of tuberous sclerosis, these
may reflect multiple AMLs (angiomyolipomas), however, this is incompletely
characterized on this study.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Severe scoliosis, status post posterior fusion, with paired rods and
spinal laminar hooks.  No evidence of fracture.  The superior most hook at the
level of approximately T4 on the right appears to be slightly oriented to the
right, which is of uncertain clinical significance, and correlation with any
outside imaging is recommended to assess for chronicity of this finding.

2.  Scattered foci of sclerosis involving the vertebral bodies, may reflect
osseous changes related to tuberous sclerosis.

3.  Multiple rounded hypodense lesions in visualized bilateral kidneys.  Given
history of tuberous sclerosis, this may reflect multiple angiomyolipomas
(AML).

4.  Calcified foci within bilateral renal collecting systems, may reflect
stones.

Findings posted to the ED dashboard at the time of interpretation.

EXAMINATION:  MR angiogram of the pelvis.

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with bicornuate uterus ? AVM versus retained
products of conception  // evaluate for retained products of conception versus

TECHNIQUE:  Multiplanar MRI of the pelvis is obtained at 1.5 Tesla per the
bladder mass protocol. T1 and T2 weighted sequences are acquired both pre and
post administration of 15 mL of MultiHance. 3D postprocessing was performed on
an independent workstation, including the creation of 3D maximum intensity
projection images.

COMPARISON:  Pelvic ultrasound dating ___.

FINDINGS: 

The uterus remains enlarged, reflecting recent post-partum status. Approximate
uterine ___ are 15.4 x 6.6 x 11.4 cm with a bicornuate configuration.
The cervix is thin and the endocervical canal expanded, with the os measuring
3.5 cm in diameter (series 17 image 54). T1 hyperintense fluid fills the
endometrial cavity, endocervical anal, and vagina.  The endometrial canal also
contains highly vascularized soft tissue projections measuring approximately
9.4 x 3.5 x 3.2 cm (17:54), appearing T1 isointense and T2 hyperintense
relative to the myometrium on pre-contrast sequences, and arising primarily
from the anterior left lateral aspect of the fundus (1501:27). The appearance
is concerning for retained products of conception.

While no discrete vascular malformation is detected, there is rapid drainage
into the uterine venous plexus and gonadal veins, more prominent on the left. 
No early internal iliac venous drainage is appreciated.

The urinary bladder is decompressed with Foley catheter in place.  The bladder
and lower uterine wall are not well visualized along the superior aspect of
the Foley balloon, spanning at least 1 cm, and an underlying wall defect
cannot be excluded (17:51).  Additional thinning is seen more superiorly at
the anterior lower uterine segment anterior and superior to the bladder (17:
50).

The ovaries are normal.

There is mild pelvic edema but no significant free pelvic fluid. Nonspecific
inguinal and external iliac lymph nodes are noted, none pathologically
enlarged by size criteria.

Relatively large volume of solid fecal material is seen throughout the distal
colon.

The osseous structures are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Distended endometrial cavity, endocervical canal and vagina with blood
products.  Enhancing soft tissue projections into the endometrial cavity are
suspicious for retained products of conception.
2.  No discrete AVM. Early drainage into uterine venous plexus and into the
gonadal veins are non-specific in the setting of highly vascularized POC;
underlying occult AVM cannot be entirely excluded.
3.  Marked wall thinning along the anterior lower uterine wall and adjacent
superior bladder, against the superior aspect of the Foley balloon; underlying
defect cannot be excluded.

person  on ___ at 10 AM, 60 minutes after initial interpretation.

text:
INDICATION:  Left breast cancer and left papillary lesion.

MAMMOGRAPHIC WIRE LOCALIZATION LEFT BREAST:  The patient was referred for
preoperative wire localization bracketing the calcifications and two
percutaneous clips in the left breast (which represent her cancer) as well as
targeting a separate papillary lesion which is denoted by a clip.  The
procedure, risks and benefits were explained to the patient and written
informed consent was obtained.  A preprocedure timeout was performed using two
patient identifiers. Laterality was confirmed.

Using standard aseptic technique and 1% lidocaine for local anesthesia, three
needles and subsequently three wires were advanced into the patient's breast.
Satisfactory positioning of the wires placed to bracket the cancer were
confirmed on orthogonal views. The wire placed to the papillary lesion was not
appropriately placed.  Therefore, the malplaced wire was removed and the
procedure was repeated.  The percutaneous clip representing the papillary
lesion was then appropriately targeted and this was confirmed on orthogonal
views.

The patient tolerated the procedure with no immediate complications.  The

EXAMINATION:  BILAT HIPS (AP,LAT AND AP PELVIS)

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with Bilateral hip dysplasia.2.  Left hip
chronic recurrent prosthetic joint infection status post resection in proximal
femur.3.  Acute on chronic right hip pain.  // eval bilateral hip

TECHNIQUE:  AP pelvis, two views left hip, two views right hip.

COMPARISON:  Pelvic radiographs ___

FINDINGS: 

The left hip region is markedly abnormal with evidence of prior resection of
the proximal femur, likely for prosthetic placement.  Any prosthesis has
subsequently been removed.  There is remodeling of the acetabulum, with
heterotopic new bone formation presumably also postoperative. A linear lucency
extends through the medial acetabular wall, unchanged compared to the prior
study.  Surgical clips in the left flank region are compatible with prior mesh
hernia repair.  Overall, appearances of the left hip are grossly unchanged.

Right hip displasia is also noted with a very shallow acetabulum, proximal
migration of the femoral head and pseudoarticulation with the iliac wing. 
There has been significant progressive destruction of the right femoral head
when compared to the prior study with fragmentation and collapse.  This
appearance may be due to avascular necrosis but infection cannot be excluded.

Extensive prior surgery noted in the lumbar spine, incompletely evaluated on
this study but grossly unchanged compared to the prior study.

IMPRESSION: 

New collapse and fragmentation of the right femoral head may reflect avascular
necrosis but infection cannot be excluded.

Otherwise unchanged appearances with evidence of bilateral hip dysplasia and
multiple prior surgeries.

___ on ___ at 15:20 into the Department of Radiology
critical communications system for direct communication to the referring
provider.

INDICATION:  Evaluate interval change in a brain abscess in a ___
female patient.

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images were obtained through the brain.  No
contrast was administered.

COMPARISON:  Multiple previous CT and enhanced MR studies of the head, the
most recent CT head, done ___.

FINDINGS:  The patient is status post left frontoparietal craniectomy with
evacuation of left subdural empyema and drainage of left frontoparietal
abscess. There is significant improvement of the subdural collection compared
to study on ___,  and the appearance is stable since the study done on
___, with apparent small residual extra-axial collection at the
frontovertex.

The vasogenic edema in the left centrum semiovale predominatly involving its
posterior portion, and extends up to the vertex has increased compared to the
prior study.  Also there is a more peripheral extension of this hypodensity in
the left posterior frontoparietal cortex, centered on the site of the original
dominant parietal abscess.

There is a new roughly triangular region of hypodensity in the anterior left
frontal white matter, but sparing the overlying cortical ribbon, measuring 2.9
x 2.7 cm, subjacent to the recent left frontal craniectomy site. There is a
slight 2mm rightward shift of midline structures. The ventricles and sulci are
normal in caliber and configuration.

Bilateral ethmoidal air cell opacification, left greater than right, is
redemonstrated.

IMPRESSION:
1.  New appearance of likely vasogenic edema in the anterior left frontal
subcortical white matter, could represent a second focus of
cerebritis/evolving cerebral abscess. Another consideration would be early
contusion relating to manipulation of the brain during recent surgery.

Contrast-enhanced MR study, preferably with MR spectroscopy, is recommended to
further characterize this process.

2.  Apparent slight increase in the white matter edema in the left centrum
semiovale, suggesting interval worsening of the underlying infectious process.
There is only slight, 2-mm rightward shift of midline structures.

3.  Small persistent extra-axial collection at the left frontovertex, s/p
recent drainage procedure.

COMMENT: The findings were discussed with Dr. ___ requesting
neurologist) by Dr. ___, ___, ___.

INDICATION:  ___ year old male with hx pericardectomy, TB on triple
antibiotics, CHF, anemia, respiratory failure who presented with shock and
renal failure.  // please eval for tunneled HD line placement NEEDS
INTERPRETER! ___ interpreter not listed as an option

COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph ___.

TECHNIQUE:  OPERATORS: Dr. ___ and Dr.
___ radiologist performed the procedure.  Dr. ___
Dr.  ___ supervised the trainee during the key components of
the procedure and have reviewed and agree with the trainee's findings.
ANESTHESIA: Moderate sedation was provided by administrating divided doses of
100 mcg of fentanyl and 2 mg of midazolam throughout the total intra-service
time of 45 minutes during which the patient's hemodynamic parameters were
continuously monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse. 1% lidocaine
was injected in the skin and subcutaneous tissues overlying the access site.
MEDICATIONS:  100 mcg Fentanyl, 2 mg Versed
CONTRAST: 0 ml of Optiray contrast.
FLUOROSCOPY TIME AND DOSE:  0.42 min, ___ MGy

PROCEDURE:  PROCEDURE DETAILS: Following the explanation of the risks,
benefits and alternatives to the procedure, written informed consent was
obtained from the patient. The patient was then brought to the angiography
suite and placed supine on the exam table. A pre-procedure time-out was
performed per ___ protocol. The right upper chest was prepped and draped in
the usual sterile fashion.
Under continuous ultrasound guidance, the patent right internal jugular vein
was compressible and accessed using a micropuncture needle. Permanent
ultrasound images were obtained before and after intravenous access, which
confirmed vein patency. Subsequently a Nitinol wire was passed into the right
atrium using fluoroscopic guidance.  The needle was exchanged for a
micropuncture sheath. The Nitinol wire was removed and a short ___ wire was
advanced to make appropriate measurements for catheter length. The ___ wire
was then passed distally into the IVC.
Next, attention was turned towards creation of a tunnel over the upper
anterior chest wall. After instilling superficial and deeper local anesthesia
using lidocaine mixed with epinephrine, a small skin incision was made at the
tunnel entry site. A 23cm tip-to-cuff length catheter was selected. The
catheter was tunneled from the entry site towards the venotomy site from where
it was brought out using a tunneling device.  The venotomy tract was dilated
using the introducer of the peel-away sheath supplied. Following this, the
peel-away sheath was placed over the ___ wire through which the catheter was
threaded into the right side of the heart with the tip in the right atrium.
The sheath was then peeled away. The catheter was sutured in place with 0 silk
sutures. Steri-strips were also used to close the venotomy incision site.
Final spot fluoroscopic image demonstrating good alignment of the catheter and
no kinking. The tip is in the right atrium. The catheter was flushed and both
lumens were capped. Sterile dressings were applied. The patient tolerated the
procedure well.

FINDINGS: 

Patent right internal jugular vein.  Final fluoroscopic image showing the
hemodialysis catheter with tip terminating in the right atrium.

COMPARISON:  Multiple prior CTs, most recently CT abdomen pelvis of ___.

sagittal reformats.  Coronal reconstructions were not provided.  

FINDINGS:
The heart size is normal.  Dense coronary artery calcifications are similar to
prior.  No pericardial effusion.  Small amount of fluid is present in the left
major fissure.  Trace dependent pleural effusions are present with minimal
adjacent atelectasis.  

ABDOMEN:  
Evaluation of the intra-abdominal organs is limited without the administration
of IV contrast and somewhat limited without coronal and complete sagittal
reconstructions.  The liver is diffusely fatty.  The unenhanced appearance of
the gallbladder, intra and extrahepatic bile ducts, pancreas, and adrenal
glands are normal.  Splenomegaly is similar to prior.  Mild bilateral
perinephric stranding is stable.  The unenhanced appearance of the kidneys
appear normal without hydronephrosis or hydroureter.  The ileal conduit and
right-sided urostomy appear stable without adjacent inflammation or stranding.


The stomach appears normal.  The small and large bowel have a normal caliber.
No evidence of diverticulitis. The appendix is normal.  

There is increased stranding diffusely throughout the peritoneum, especially
involving the greater omentum and sigmoid mesocolon, concerning for peritoneal
carcinomatosis.  No discrete mass is appreciated, though lack of IV contrast
limits evaluation.  Nonhemorrhagic peritoneal fluid has also increased,
distributed throughout the mid abdomen and central loops of bowel.  No fluid
is appreciated in the cul-de-sac.

No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  Previously seen
lymphadenopathy along the left renal vein remains resolved.  The unenhanced
appearance of the intra-abdominal systemic vasculature is normal.  No
abdominal wall hernia.

The patient is status post cystoprostatectomy.  Metallic clips in the pelvis
are in stable position.  12 x 11 mm nodular soft tissue density surrounding
the right-sided surgical clips previously measured 15 x 12 mm.  No new or
enlarged soft tissue is present.  Small bilateral inguinal hernias contain
fat. No pelvic side-wall or inguinal lymphadenopathy.  Small amount of free
pelvic fluid is nonhemorrhagic.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES: No focal lytic or sclerotic lesion concerning for
metastases.


IMPRESSION:
1.  Diffuse peritoneal stranding, preferentially involving the greater omentum
and sigmoid mesocolon, raises concern for advancement of disease with
peritoneal carcinomatosis.  Though an infectious process could have this
appearance, there is no other evidence for the presence of such.

2.  Status post cystoprostatectomy with slightly decreased size of nodular
soft tissue surrounding the right pelvic surgical clips.  Retroperitoneal
disease remains resolved.  

3.  Fatty liver.

EXAMINATION:  Chest CTA with CT abdomen/pelvis

INDICATION:  ___ with N/V, abd pain, tachycardia w/ new O2 requirement//
SBO/acute abdominal process, PE?.  Per EMR, multiple episodes of nausea and
vomiting.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast in the arterial
phase.  Then, imaging was obtained through the abdomen and pelvis in the
portal venous phase. Reformatted coronal and sagittal images through the
chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and oblique maximal intensity projection images of
the chest were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 1.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 4.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 2.3
mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 4.2 s, 33.1 cm; CTDIvol = 9.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 327.0
mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 4.4 s, 47.9 cm; CTDIvol = 12.4 mGy (Body) DLP = 595.8
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 925 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:   Patient has no prior imaging examinations for comparison at this
institution.  .

FINDINGS: 

HEART AND VASCULATURE: No pericardial effusion.  The thoracic aorta is normal
in caliber.  Aortic valve calcifications are moderate.  Coronary artery
calcifications are mild.  Aortic arch and great vessel origin calcifications
are moderate with mild stenosis of the left subclavian artery origin.  No
evidence of thoracic aortic dissection.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: There is mild biapical scarring and moderate upper lobe
predominant centrilobular emphysema.  There is a 5 mm left upper lobe
pulmonary nodule (series 3, image 100) and a 4 mm right upper lobe pulmonary
nodule (series 3, image 71).  There are a few other scattered pulmonary micro
nodules.  A 1.3 cm predominantly fatty pulmonary nodule in the left lower lobe
posterior basal segment is consistent with a pulmonary hamartoma.  There is
mild basilar interlobular septal thickening.  There is mild diffuse bronchial
wall thickening and subsegmental mucous impaction.  There are calcified
punctate biapical pulmonary granulomas.

ABDOMEN: The liver demonstrates multiple hypodense lesions too small to
characterize and in a right hepatic hemangioma with an adjacent perfusion
defect.  The spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands and right kidney are
unremarkable except for a hypodense right renal and splenic lesion too small
to characterize.

There is a 1.2 x 1.2 cm heterogeneous lesion in the interpolar left kidney
(series 2, image 145; series 607, image 35).  A hypoattenuating lesion in the
right kidney is too small to completely characterize.

No infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm.

GASTROINTESTINAL: There is no small intestinal obstruction or ascites, however
the stomach is at least moderately distended with fluid. Incidental small
periampullary duodenal diverticulum.

LYMPH NODES: No enlarged abdominal or pelvic lymph nodes.

PELVIS: There is no pelvic free fluid.  Hysterectomy changes are present. 
There is a 2.5 cm left ovarian cystic lesion.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES: No aggressive osseous lesions are seen. 
Sequela of prior surgery seen in the anterior abdominal wall.  There are
small, fat containing bilateral inguinal hernias.  A calcified nodule
overlying the left gluteus maximus musculature probably reflects an injection
site granuloma.  Lateral right fourth and fifth rib fractures appear chronic.

IMPRESSION:


1. Moderate centrilobular emphysema. Mild basilar interstitial pulmonary
edema. No pulmonary embolus.
2. Moderate distention of the stomach which is filled with fluid, raising
concern for gastric outlet obstruction, particularly in the setting of
multiple episodes of nausea and vomiting.  Consider endoscopy if one has not
been performed recently.
3. Additional incidental findings as follows:  1.3 cm heterogeneous left renal
lesion raises possibility of a solid renal mass. Nonemergent MRI is
recommended.  Pulmonary nodules and micro nodules measure up to 5 mm. For
incidentally detected multiple solid pulmonary nodules smaller than 6mm, no CT
follow-up is recommended in a low-risk patient, and an optional CT follow-up
in 12 months is recommended in a high-risk patient. 1.3 cm left ovarian cystic
lesion.  At this age, consider ultrasound in ___ year for follow-up. 
Alternatively, all these findings could be followed with a CT to reassess for
interval change.

RECOMMENDATION(S):
1. A 1.3 cm heterogeneously hypoenhancing lesion in the interpolar left kidney
is most concerning for papillary renal cell carcinoma with other differential
considerations including an oncocytoma or fat poor angiomyolipoma. Recommend
nonemergent renal MRI for further evaluation.
2. Pulmonary nodules and micro nodules measure up to 5 mm. For incidentally
detected multiple solid pulmonary nodules smaller than 6mm, no CT follow-up is
recommended in a low-risk patient, and an optional CT follow-up in 12 months
is recommended in a high-risk patient.
3. There is a 1.3 cm left ovarian cyst. Recommend follow-up pelvic ultrasound
in one year.
4. The stomach is distended with fluid.  Recommend nonemergent upper
endoscopy.

___, M.D. by ___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 8:52
am.

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman h/o cholelithiasis s/p CCY but with RUQ pain,

choledocholithiasis.

TECHNIQUE:  T1- and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen were
acquired in a 1.5 T magnet.
Intravenous contrast: 8 mL Gadavist.
Oral contrast:  1 cc of Gadavist mixed with 50 cc of water was administered
for oral contrast.

COMPARISON:  Gallbladder drainage ___.
Abdominal ultrasound ___.
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast ___.
Outside CT torso ___.

FINDINGS: 

Lower Thorax: Limited evaluation of the lung bases is notable for small
bilateral pleural effusions and compressive atelectasis, increased since ___.

Liver: Liver is homogeneous in signal intensity.  No suspicious mass.  There
is mild signal drop on out of phase imaging consistent with mild hepatic
steatosis (fat fraction 5%).  Trace perihepatic free fluid noted.

Patient is status post placement of a percutaneous drainage catheter coursing
through segment 5 into the rim enhancing fluid collection located within the
gallbladder fossa which has decreased in size now measuring 3.9 x 1.6 cm
(previously 5.2 x 3.4 cm).  (1402:86).

There is an elongated nonenhancing 4.8 x 1.5 cm fluid collection along the
undersurface of segment 5 of the liver which has slightly increased since ___ (04:32).  Medial to this lesion there is a small fluid-filled tail
that tracks to the duodenal bulb without definite fistulous connection.  Mild
fat stranding around the duodenum is noted. (1402:100).

Additional 2.1 x 1 cm and 2.2 x 1.2 cm rim enhancing fluid collections are
noted along the inferior right hepatic tip just adjacent to the peritoneum
(1401:135).  Mild peritoneal enhancement with associated fat stranding is
noted adjacent to the anterior located collection.  There is mild fat
stranding and phlegmonous changes tracking from the anterior collection
towards the anti mesenteric side of the ascending colon/proximal transverse
colon at the hepatic flexure with associated mild wall thickening and without
definite luminal fistulous connection noted.  (1402:114).

Biliary: There is mild central intrahepatic biliary prominence with a saccular
0.3 cm focal dilatation of the central most aspect of the anterior right
biliary duct (9:2).  Mild extrahepatic biliary duct dilatation is expected
post cholecystectomy.  The cystic duct stump is identified and points towards
the gallbladder fossa, similar to prior CT.  Mild peribiliary enhancement of
the common hepatic duct, and central intrahepatic biliary ducts noted
(1402:63).  The common bile duct smoothly tapers to the level of the ampulla
without obstructing stone or large periampullary mass. Trifurcation biliary
anatomy noted.

Pancreas: Pancreas is homogeneous in signal intensity without suspicious mass.
No pancreatic duct dilatation or peripancreatic fat stranding.

Spleen: Spleen is normal in size without suspicious mass.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are symmetric in size without
discrete nodule.

Kidneys: 0.2 cm simple cyst is seen in the interpolar region of the left
kidney.  Kidneys are otherwise homogeneous in signal intensity without
suspicious mass.  No hydronephrosis or perinephric fat stranding.

Gastrointestinal Tract:  Distal esophagus, stomach, and visualized small bowel
are unremarkable.

Lymph Nodes: Subcentimeter porta hepatis and periportal lymph nodes are again
noted, likely reactive with largest measuring 1.1 cm within the portacaval
region, unchanged since prior (06:33).  Mesenteric lymph nodes are
nonenlarged.

Vasculature: No abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Celiac axis, SMA, bilateral renal
arteries, and accessory right renal artery are patent.  Severe stenosis of the
distal abdominal aorta is again noted, similar to ___.

Osseous and Soft Tissue Structures: No aggressive osseous lesions.  Soft
tissues are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Mild increase in 4.8 x 1.5 cm fluid collection along hepatic undersurface.
The medial most extent of this collection demonstrates a fluid-filled tail
that tracks towards the duodenal bulb with associated duodenitis, likely
reactive.  Differential includes abscess versus biloma.
2.  2.1 and 2.2 cm fluid collections along the inferior right hepatic tip with
associated focal adjacent peritonitis.  Associated phlegmonous and
inflammatory changes tract toward the colon at the hepatic flexure with
reactive inflammatory changes.  No definite fistulous connection.
3.  Status post percutaneous catheter drainage of a collection within the
gallbladder fossa which has decreased in size.
4.  Cholangitis involving central intrahepatic and common hepatic duct.
5.  Increase in small bilateral pleural effusions with compressive
atelectasis.
6.  Mild hepatic steatosis.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Largest of these lesions is on the lower limits of a
drainable collection and if drainage is warranted consultation with the
radiology procedure service is recommended.

NOTIFICATION:   The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 5:50 pm, 5 minutes after
discovery of the findings.

EXAMINATION:  MRI of the Abdomen

INDICATION:  Abdominal mass, Left lower quadrant  // Pt with LLQ painful mass
not seen on abdominal ct recommended abdominal MRI.  AUTH# ___ ___ to
___

TECHNIQUE:  T1- and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen were
acquired in a 1.5 T magnet.
Intravenous contrast: 10 mL Gadavist.

COMPARISON:  CT ___.

FINDINGS: 

A 5.6 x 5.5 x 5.5 cm, heterogeneously enhancing well-circumscribed lesion
immediately adjacent to or centered in the transversalis muscle of the left
anterior abdominal wall is predominantly T1 isointense to muscle, though the
possibility of some subtle high T1 signal within it cannot be excluded due to
motion artifact (03:11 ).  In addition, the sagittal localizer images show
punctate area of high T1 signal (2:9).  The lesion is also predominantly low
signal/isointense to muscle on the STIR sequence.  However, small curvilinear
areas of high STIR signal are seen within the mass, possibly corresponding to
the areas of low signal intensity seen on the CT scan.  The ___
CT shows no calcification or ossification associated with this lesion. 
Following contrast administration, there is heterogeneous enhancement within
this lesion, both peripheral and internal.

There is no significant lymphadenopathy. A 1.5 cm simple cyst is noted in the
interpolar region of the left kidney. The remainder of the visualized
intra-abdominal and pelvic structures appear within normal limits.  There is
very mild edema within the soft tissue surrounding the lesion.

Subchondral degenerative changes are noted at the acetabulae, bilaterally.

IMPRESSION: 

Solid, vascular lesion adjacent to or centered in the transversalis muscle of
the left anterior abdominal wall, corresponding to the finding on the ___ CT scan.  Although there may be some associated high T1 signal,
the appearance is not highly suggestive of a hematoma.  The MR appearance is
non-specific. However, low T2 signal suggests a fibrous or cellular soft
tissue mass, possibly a desmoid or leiomyoma/leiomyosarcoma.

Tissue sampling should be considered.  If tissue sampling is not pursued, then
short-term follow-up (e.g.  MRI in ___ weeks) would be recommended.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Solid, vascular lesion adjacent to or centered in the
transversalis muscle of the left anterior abdominal wall, corresponding to the
finding on the ___ CT scan.

Tissue sampling should be considered. If tissue sampling is not pursued, then
short-term follow-up (e.g.  MRI in ___ weeks) would be recommended.

___ on ___ at 16:34 into the Department of Radiology critical
communications system for direct communication to the referring provider.

EXAMINATION:  DX TIB/FIB AND ANKLE

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with right leg pain// right leg pain

TECHNIQUE:  Two views right tibia and fibula, mortise view of the right ankle

COMPARISON:  Right tibia and fibula radiographs ___

FINDINGS: 

There are comminuted fractures of the mid fibular diaphysis and the distal
fibular diaphysis with numerous small bony fragments displaced posteromedially
and a large butterfly fragment along the more proximal comminuted fracture
measuring almost 10 cm craniocaudal.  Metallic densities in the soft tissues
along the posterior aspect of the left ankle likely reflect bullet fragments.

callus formation around the comminuted fractures.

distal fibula.  Punctate metallic densities along the posterior aspect of the
ankle consistent with tiny residual below fragments.

EXAMINATION:  MR ___

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with Crohn's, lower left quadrant pain,
response to RX  // ___ year old woman with Crohn's, lower left quadrant pain,
response to RX

TECHNIQUE:  T1 and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen and pelvis
were acquired within a 1.5 T magnet, including 3D dynamic sequences performed
prior to, during, and following the administration of 0.1 mmol/kg of Gadavist
intravenous contrast (7 cc). Oral contrast consisted of 900 mL of VoLumen. 1.0
mg of Glucagon was administered IM to reduce bowel peristalsis.

COMPARISON:  Compared to a prior MRI performed ___.

FINDINGS: 

MR ENTEROGRAPHY:
There is satisfactory distention of the small bowel loops with oral contrast. 
Normal small bowel peristalsis.  Unfortunately most of the distal colon is
collapsed, however there is submucosal fat infiltration extending from the
distal transverse colon to the rectum, consistent with chronic inflammatory
disease.  There is minimal mucosal hyper enhancement on the arterial phase
that could indicate very mild active inflammatory disease, however could also
be seen with chronic inflammation.  There is no transmural disease or
intramural edema.  There is no stricturing or proximal bowel dilatation.  No
abscess or fistulas.  The remainder colon and small bowel are normal without
focal thickening or intramural edema.

MRI OF THE ABDOMEN WITH AND WITHOUT IV CONTRAST:
The liver demonstrates normal signal intensity without suspicious hepatic mass
lesion.  There are few gallstones noted.  There is no gallbladder wall
thickening or pericholecystic fluid.  No intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary
duct dilatation.  No segmental strictures, dilatation or mural irregularities
to suggest primary sclerosing cholangitis.  The pancreas demonstrates normal
signal intensity and enhances homogeneously.  No dilatation of the main
pancreatic duct.

The spleen is severely atrophic, in keeping with prior infarction.  Bilateral
adrenal glands are normal.

This kidneys are symmetric in size and show good corticomedullary
differentiation.  No suspicious renal mass or hydronephrosis.

There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.  The visualized
portions of the aorta and its major branches are patent.

No suspicious osseous or soft tissue lesions.

MRI OF THE PELVIS WITH AND WITHOUT IV CONTRAST:

Uterus is normal in size and signal.  No adnexal masses noted.

The bladder and distal ureters are normal without focal mass or wall
thickening.

No pelvic lymphadenopathy.  No free fluid pelvis.

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Colonic submucosal fat  from the distal transverse to the rectum,
consistent with chronic inflammatory disease.  Currently no colonic mural
edema with decreased pericolonic vascularity and serosal abnormality
indicating improvement compared to prior; there is thin mucosal hyper
enhancement that could indicate very mild active inflammatory disease, however
could also be seen with chronic inflammation.  There is no transmural active
disease.  No abscess or fistulas.  The remainder of the colon and small bowel
are normal.

2.  Severely atrophic spleen, in keeping with prior infarction.

3.  Gallstones without signs of cholecystitis.

text:
EXAMINATION:  CT CHEST W/CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with metastatic pancreatic cancer, on palliative
chemotherapy  // interval assessment of disease burden

TECHNIQUE:  Multi detector helical scanning of the chest was coordinated with
intravenous infusion of nonionic iodinated contrast agent and reconstructed as
5 and 1.25 mm thick axial, 2.5 mm thick coronal and parasagittal, and 8 mm MIP
axial images. All images were reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 5.7 s, 74.9 cm; CTDIvol = 21.2 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,586.6 mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 0.6 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 3.4 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.7
mGy-cm.
   3) Stationary Acquisition 0.0 s, 0.0 cm; CTDIvol = 134.0 mGy (Body) DLP =
67.0 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,655 mGy-cm.
** Note: This radiation dose report was copied from CLIP ___ (CT ABD AND
PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

COMPARISON:  CT chest ___

FINDINGS: 

NECK, THORACIC INLET, AXILLAE AND CHEST WALL:
No imaging findings in the thyroid require further imaging follow-up.  There
is no axillary or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.  There is minimal
atherosclerotic calcification of the great vessels of the neck.  The soft
tissues of the chest wall are unremarkable.  Right chest wall port with
catheter tip terminating in the cavoatrial junction is unchanged.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA:
There is no mediastinal lymphadenopathy.  Noncontrast imaging limits
evaluation for hilar adenopathy but the hilar contour appears within normal
limits.  There is a small to moderate hiatal hernia.  The esophagus is
moderately dilated and there is distal esophageal wall thickening.

HEART, PERICARDIUM AND VASCULATURE:
There are mild atherosclerotic calcifications of the aortic arch.  No coronary
artery calcifications.  The heart is normal in configuration.  The pulmonary
artery and thoracic aorta are normal caliber.  No pericardial effusion.

LUNGS, AIRWAYS, AND PLEURA:
There are no new or enlarging pulmonary nodules.  The punctate pulmonary
nodules in the right upper lobe all measuring under 2 mm are unchanged (see
302:39, 45, 52, 61).  There is no pleural abnormality.  No new masses or
consolidations are present.  The airways are patent to the level of the
subsegmental bronchi.

CHEST CAGE:
Although there are no bone lesions in the imaged field of view suspicious for
malignancy or infection, it should be noted that radionuclide bone scan and
FDG PET are more sensitive than CT in detecting early osseus pathology.

UPPER ABDOMEN:
Please refer to separate report of CT abdomen and pelvis performed on the same
day for description of the subdiaphragmatic findings.

IMPRESSION:


1. Stable appearance of the chest with no evidence of intrathoracic metastatic
disease.
2. Unchanged small to moderate hiatal hernia. The esophagus is moderately
dilated and there is distal esophageal wall thickening.  Consider nonurgent
upper endoscopy if clinically indicated.

text:
PICC LINE EXCHANGE

INDICATION: Indwelling PICC line not working.

The procedure was explained to the patient.  A timeout was performed.

RADIOLOGIST:  Dr. ___ and ___ performed the procedure.

TECHNIQUE:  Using sterile technique and local anesthesia, a guidewire was
advanced through the indwelling left arm PICC line, and subsequently into the
SVC under fluoroscopic guidance.  The old PICC line was then removed and a
peel-away sheath was then placed over the guidewire.  A new double-lumen PICC
line measuring 40 cm in length was then placed through the peel-away sheath
with its tip positioned in the SVC under fluoroscopic guidance.  Position of
the catheter was confirmed by a fluoroscopic spot film of the chest.

The peel-away sheath and guidewire were then removed.  The catheter was
secured to the skin, flushed, and a sterile dressing applied.

The patient tolerated the procedure well.  There were no immediate
complications.

IMPRESSION:  Uncomplicated fluoroscopically guided PICC line exchange for a
new double-lumen PICC line.  Final internal length is 40 cm, with the tip
positioned in the SVC.  The line is ready to use.

and an 8 mm left frontal lesion (2:9).  An area of vasogenic edema with 11 mm
hyperdensity in the right frontal lobe (601b:20) is also concerning for a
hemorrhagic metastasis. A 12 mm lesion along the septum pellucidum (2:15), an
8mm lesion in the midline of the cerebellum (601b:40), a 15mm hypodense lesion
with peripheral nodular increased density within the left cerebellum
(601b:54), and a 5mm lesion in the right cerebellum (602b:79) are also noted.

structures or major vascular territorial infarction.  The ventricles and sulci
are normal in size and configuration with patent basal cisterns.  There is no
fracture.  The imaged paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are well
aerated.

IMPRESSION: At least 7 of the patient's numerous metastatic lesions
demonstrate intralesional hyperdensity concerning for blood.

HISTORY:  Status post open reduction and internal fixation of femoral fracture
13 days ago with persistently elevated white blood cell count.  Evaluate for
fluid collection.

COMPARISON:  Preoperative radiographs ___ and postoperative
radiographs from ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial non-contrast CT of the right femur and knee was performed.
Sagittal and coronal reconstructions were performed.  No intravenous contrast
was given due to recently resolved renal failure.

FINDINGS:

Again seen is a long-stem total knee arthroplasty.  The tibial and femoral
components are well seated.  Lateral fixation plate at the mid-to-distal
femoral shaft with numerous transverse fixation screws and cerclage wires is
again noted fixating the oblique fracture through the distal femoral shaft at
the superior aspect of the femoral component. This fracture traverses the
cement surrounding the tip of the femoral component.

Beneath the skin staples along the lateral distal thigh is a 4.4 x 3.3 x 6.5
(AP x TV x CC) cm fluid collection in the superficial subcutaneous tissues.
Evaluation of this area is limited due to streak artifact, however there is no
fluid traversing deep to the muscular fascia and there is no suncutaneous air.
No intermuscular or intramuscular fluid collections are seen.

There is no osseous cortical destruction or new periosteal bone to suggest
osteomyelitis.

IMPRESSION:

1.  Near anatomic alignment of distal femoral shaft periprosthetic fracture
fixated with lateral plate, cerclage wires, and screws.

2.  Subcutaneous fluid collection deep to the lateral skin staples is most
likely a postoperative seroma.  Superimposed infection cannot be entirely
excluded with imaging alone.

volume loss as expected.  There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. 
Right-sided port terminates as well into the right atrium as on the prior. 
Trachea and central airways are patent to the segmental level.  There is
diffuse patchy opacification of both lungs concerning for an infectious
process.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH CONTRAST:  The liver is normal without focal lesion. 
The gallbladder as on the prior demonstrates large gallstone and trace amount
of pericholecystic fluid without fat stranding or wall thickening to suggest
acute cholecystitis.  The pancreas is quite atrophic as on the prior without
focal lesions.  The spleen and adrenal glands are unremarkable.  The right
kidney enhances and excretes contrast symmetrically.  Left kidney has been
removed with surgical clips causing marked streak artifact limiting assessment
of the resection bed.  The stomach, small and large bowel are unremarkable. 
There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal adenopathy.  There is no free air or
free fluid in the abdomen.  The aorta and major branches appear patent.  The
bladder, uterus, and rectum are unremarkable.  There is no free pelvic fluid. 
There is no pelvic or inguinal adenopathy.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  Lucent lesion in the head of the left femur is again
demonstrated, not particularly changed without evidence for fracture. 
Multiple vertebral lesions are again seen causing marked destruction of the
L3, T11 and 12 vertebral bodies.  These do not appear changed from the prior
studies.  Left rib metastasis is also again demonstrated.  Left pectoral
metastasis is slightly fuller in appearance, measuring 1.4 cm.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Perhaps slight interval increase in the size of the representative
pulmonary nodules with otherwise stability in the number and distribution.
2.  Bilateral patchy pulmonary opacities could reflect infectious or
inflammatory process. Given recent hospitalization and treatment for
pneumonia, follow up chest radiograph approximately 4 weeks after treatment is
advised to document clearance. 
3.  Unchanged bony metastases as described above.
4.  Slight increase in left pectoral metastasis. 
5.  Cholelithiasis and divterticulosis without acute inflammation.

This information was entered into the radiology department's online record for
notification of critical results.

text:
EXAMINATION:  CTA chest

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with CIDP off heparin and now in shock, likely
septic. r/o PE.// PE?

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the chest
abdomen and pelvis after the uneventful administration of intravenous
contrast.
Reformatted coronal, sagittal, thin slice axial images, and oblique maximal
intensity projection images were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 1.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 9.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 4.6
mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 3.5 s, 27.6 cm; CTDIvol = 14.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 401.9
mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 5.1 s, 56.4 cm; CTDIvol = 14.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 794.1
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,200 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT of the chest ___

FINDINGS: 

Left pacemaker with tips terminating in the right atrium, right ventricle and
through the coronary sinus in a left coronary vein.
Heart is mildly enlarged, unchanged.
Minimal pericardial effusion is new in comparison to prior.

The study is not gated.
Moderate atherosclerotic calcifications of the coronaries and aortic annulus,
are noted also along the thoracic aorta and head and neck vessels.
The aorta and its major branch vessels are patent, with no evidence of
stenosis, occlusion, dissection, or aneurysmal formation.
There is no evidence of penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer.

The pulmonary arteries are well opacified to the segmental level, with no
evidence of filling defect within the main, right, left, lobar, segmental
pulmonary arteries.
The main and right pulmonary arteries are normal in caliber, and there is no
evidence of right heart strain.

There is no supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy.
Small hypodense nodules with coarse calcification in the left lobe of the
thyroid.

Endotracheal tube in good position, terminating approximately 2.5 cm from the
carina.
The study was performed in partial inspiration, bibasilar dependent
atelectasis associated with subsegmental platelike atelectasis of the lingula.
There is no evidence of pneumonia.
Bilateral smooth septal thickening and minimal bilateral pleural effusions are
new in comparison to prior suggesting moderate pulmonary edema.

ABDOMEN:
Nasogastric tube terminates in the stomach.
Liver is homogeneous, unremarkable with no intra or extrahepatic bile duct
dilatation.
Gallbladder within normal limits.

Pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands are unremarkable.

Tiny cortical cysts in the lower pole of the right kidney.  No signs of
hydronephrosis and there is no evidence of calculi.
Minimal perirenal fat stranding and left trace of free fluid is nonspecific. 
There is no free fluid in pelvis.
No intra-abdominal free air.
Ureters are collapsed.
The urinary bladder is empty with Foley catheter

Mild diverticulosis along the sigmoid and left colon with no signs of
diverticulitis.
Small bowel is unremarkable.
Appendix within normal limits.

Extensive atherosclerotic calcifications and soft plaques along abdominal
aorta and iliacs, no evidence of abdominal aneurysm.

Minimal subcutaneous edema along the walls of the abdomen and pelvis.

No lytic or blastic osseous lesion suspicious for malignancy is identified.
Multilevel degenerative changes of thoracic and lumbar spine.

IMPRESSION:


-No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.
-Bibasilar dependent atelectasis, no evidence of pneumonia.
-Moderate pulmonary edema associated with minimal bilateral pleural and
pericardial effusion, new in comparison to prior suggesting volume overload.
-No acute abdominal pelvic findings.

MRI OF RIGHT KNEE

INDICATION:  Assess for meniscal tear.

TECHNIQUE:  Multiplanar proton density and T2-weighted sequences were acquired
on 1.5 Tesla magnet according to routine knee protocol.

COMPARISON:  Radiograph of right knee ___, and MRI of right knee
___.

FINDINGS:

The medial and lateral menisci are normal.  The ACL and PCL are intact.  The
extensor mechanism is intact.  The medial collateral ligament is normal.  The
lateral collateral ligament complex is normal.

In the region of previous microfracture and focal cartilage defect, there is
focal bony prominence compatible with an articular surface osteophyte at the
weightbearing portion of the medial femoral condyle (series 4, image 20). This
measures approximately 7 mm in anteroposterior dimension, 3 mm in craniocaudal
length, and approximately 6-7 mm transversely. There is corresoinding thinning
of the overlyling cartilage. There is subchondral marrow edema at this portion
of the medial femoral condyle (series 5, image 20).

The lateral compartment cartilage is within normal limits.  The patellofemoral
compartment cartilage is normal.

There is a loose body in the intercondylar eminence.  This is located
posterior to the PCL.  It measures 15 mm in craniocaudal length x 7 mm
transversely x 9 mm in anteroposterior dimension.  It is immediately lateral
to the medial femoral condyle.

There is a tiny medial popliteal cyst.  There is a physiological amount of
joint fluid.

The extensor mechanism is intact.

IMPRESSION:
1.  No  meniscal tear detected.
2.  Focal bony prominence in the region of previous microfracture compatible
with articular surface osteophyte at the medial femoral condyle weightbearing
surface with overlying cartilage thinning and subchondral marrow edema.
2.  Loose body in the intercondylar notch.

text:
INDICATION:  ___ female with history of spinal stenosis, presents with
neck pain and numbness and tingling in the left arm.  Rule out nerve root
compression.

TECHNIQUE:  Sagittal T2W and T1W FLAIR FSE, and axial T2W gradient echo
sequences were obtained.  No contrast was administered.

COMPARISON:  CT of ___.

FINDINGS: Alignment and lordosis of the cervical spine is maintained.  There
are no signal intensity or intradural abnormalities noted.  There is no
evidence of nerve root compression.  There are multilevel degenerative changes
as described below:

C4-5: Moderate broad-based disc protrusion and ligamenta flava thickening
result in ventral canal narrowing, with a confined appearance of the spinal
cord, but no evidence of neural foraminal narrowing or nerve root compression.

C5-6: Broad-based disc bulge and endplate spondylotic complex with ligamenta
flava thickening, causing narrowing of the entire canal, circumferential
effacement of the CSF and deformity of the spinal cord.  Moderate right and
moderately severe left neural foramen encroachment secondary to uncovertebral
and facet arthrosis, but no definite nerve root impingement.

C6-7: Broad-based disc protrusion and endplate spondylotic ridge which very
slightly indents the ventral cord.  No neural foraminal narrowing or nerve
root compression noted.

Also noted is fatty bridging of several upper thoracic vertebrae, with
loss of the anterior portion of those disc spaces, confluent with a thickened
anterior longitudinal ligment, consistent with diffuse idiopathic skeletal
hyperostosis (DISH).

IMPRESSION:
1.  No evidence of cervical spinal malalignment or fracture, or signal
abnormality in the cord.
2.  Degenerative changes of the cervical spine, most significant at C5-6 with
deformity of the spinal cord, but no evidence of nerve root compression.
3.  Probable diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH).

text:
EXAMINATION:  MRCP

INDICATION:  ___ woman with history of pancreatitis, abnormalities on
prior MR, with persistent right/mid-epigastric pain; evaluate for interval
change.

TECHNIQUE:  T1- and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen were
acquired in a 1.5 T magnet.
Intravenous contrast: 10 mL Gadavist.
Oral contrast:  1 cc of Gadavist mixed with 50 cc of water was administered
for oral contrast.

COMPARISON:  MRCP dated ___.

FINDINGS: 

Lower Thorax: This study is not dedicated for evaluation of the lungs.  Within
this limitation, no obvious pulmonary mass is identified on limited views of
the lungs.  No evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.  Bibasilar
atelectasis is mild.

Liver: The liver is normal in size and surface contour.  A 7-mm segment VIa
possible sclerosed hepatic hemangioma is overall unchanged although less
conspicuous.  Hepatosteatosis persists.  No new focal hepatic lesion.  No
ascites.

Biliary: The gallbladder is not visualized with susceptibility artifact likely
from clips related to prior surgical removal.  This artifact limits detailed
evaluation of surrounding structures.  Mild ectasia of right intrahepatic bile
ducts is unchanged (series 3, image 21).  No extrahepatic biliary ductal
dilatation.

Pancreas:  There is pancreatic divisum.  Again seen is a focal, short, 2-cm
segment of main pancreatic duct narrowing in the body with a short 1.5-cm
segment of post-stenotic mild dilatation at the body-tail junction that
measures up to 5 mm (series 3, image 21, 22), overall unchanged.  The pancreas
is diffusely atrophic but more significantly in the tail distal to the abrupt
cut off in duct caliber. No peripancreatic fluid collection or fat stranding.
No evidence of a focal pancreatic mass.  Pancreatic calcifications are not
well-appreciated on this exam.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and signal intensity.  No focal splenic
mass.  A 1.6 x 1.2-cm, round lesion near the hilum of the spleen and
pancreatic tail is consistent with an accessory spleen (series 18, image 54),
unchanged.

Adrenal Glands: The adrenal glands are normal in size and configuration.  No
evidence of a focal adrenal mass.

Kidneys: The kidneys are normal in size and symmetric with normal nephrograms.
Multiple bilateral simple renal cysts are overall similar the prior exam.  No
concerning focal renal mass.  No hydronephrosis or perinephric abnormality.

Gastrointestinal Tract:  The bowel is normal in caliber.  No bowel
obstruction.

Lymph Nodes: Multiple retroperitoneal and peripancreatic lymph nodes are again
visualized, measuring up to 8 mm (e.g., series 18, image 51, 62, 63, 86, 87,
88, 98).

Vasculature: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber.  The bilateral renal
arteries are widely patent.

Osseous and Soft Tissue Structures: No suspicious osseous lesion for
malignancy or infection.  A 1.2-cm, T2-hyperintense hemangioma in a lower
thoracic vertebral body is overall unchanged (series 2, image 11).  Multilevel
degenerative changes of the visualized spine are mild-to-moderate and most
pronounced at L5-S1.  A fat-containing left lower quadrant ventral hernia is
small (series 2, image 42).

IMPRESSION: 

1.  Persistent short-segment narrowing in the main pancreatic duct in the body
with mild focal short-segment of dilatation distally in the pancreatic
body-tail and parenchymal atrophy, overall unchanged and without evidence of a
focal mass.  Calcifications are not well-assessed on this exam.

2.  Pancreatic divisum.  No MR evidence of active pancreatitis.

3.  Indeterminate 7-mm hepatic Segment ___ lesion is unchanged.  No new
hepatic lesion.

4.  Small left lower quadrant fat-containing ventral hernia.

text:
EXAMINATION:  CT-guided core needle biopsy of a right lung mass

INDICATION:  ___ year old man with locally advanced bladder cancer with lung
lesion found on imaging concerning for primary lung cancer vs. metastatic
bladder cancer please obtain biopsy for diagnosis. Please place fiducial in
lung lesion for radiation// biopsy lung lesion, fiducial placement

COMPARISON:  ___ CT chest CT

PROCEDURE:  CT-guided core needle biopsy of a right lung mass biopsy.

OPERATORS:  Dr. ___, radiology trainee and Dr. ___,
attending radiologist. Dr. ___ personally supervised the trainee
during the key components of the procedure and reviewed and agrees with the
trainee's findings.

TECHNIQUE:  The risks, benefits, and alternatives of the procedure were
explained to the patient.  After a detailed discussion, informed written
consent was obtained.  A pre-procedure timeout using three patient identifiers
was performed per ___ protocol.

The patient was placed in a supine position on the CT scan table.  Limited
preprocedure CTscan of the intended biopsy area was performed.  Based on the
CT findings an appropriate position for the biopsy was chosen.  The site was
marked.

The site was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.  1% lidocaine
were administered to the subcutaneous and deep tissues for local anesthetic
effect.  Under CT guidance, a 17 gauge coaxial needle was introduced into the
lesion.  An 18 gauge core biopsy device with a 22 mm throw was used to obtain
five core biopsy specimens, which were sent for pathology.  Then a gold
fiducial seed was placed in the ___ the mass through the coaxial needle.

The specimen was evaluated by onsite cytologist, deemed adequate.

The procedure was tolerated well and there were no immediate post-procedural
complications.

DOSE:  Total DLP (Body) = 194 mGy-cm.

SEDATION:  Moderate sedation was provided by administering divided doses of
0.5 mg Versed and 25 mcg fentanyl throughout the total intra-service time of
25 minutes during which patient's hemodynamic parameters were continuously
monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse.

FINDINGS: 

Re-demonstrated is the paraspinal right lower lobe mass.

Post biopsy a fiducial is seen in the central portion of the mass with a few
surrounding bubbles of gas.  No pneumothorax..

IMPRESSION: 

Technically successful CT-guided core needle biopsy of a right lung mass with
single fiducial placement.

text:
EXAMINATION:  MR ___ SPINE W/O CONTRAST ___ MR SPINE

INDICATION:  ___ female with a known L3 compression fracture with
worsening lower back pain found to have worsened fracture on CT now with new
bilateral lower extremity numbness.  Evaluate extent of fracture and for
spinal cord or nerve root compression.

TECHNIQUE:  Sagittal imaging was performed with T2, T1, and STIR technique,
followed by axial T2 imaging.

COMPARISON:  ___ contrast abdomen and pelvis CT.  ___
lumbosacral spine radiograph, ___ contrast torso CT.  ___
contrast abdomen and pelvis CT.

FINDINGS: 

Study is mildly degraded by motion, especially on axial T2 images.

 For the purposes of numbering, the lowest rib bearing vertebral body was
designated the T12 level.

Again, there is L3 vertebral body compression deformity with approximately 80%
height loss, no associated edema, with 6 mm bony retropulsion resulting in
moderate spinal canal stenosis.  Allowing for differences in technique, L3
loss of height is grossly unchanged compared to the same-day CT examination,
has progressed since the ___ radiograph, and is new since the prior
CT from ___.  Within limits of the study, anterior and posterior
longitudinal ligaments are grossly preserved without definite destruction
identified.  The posterior ligamentous complex is intact.

No other fracture is seen.  7 mm fat containing lesion in the T12 vertebral
body, 10 mm fat containing lesion at the anterior inferior endplate of the L5
vertebral body and another adjacent 15 mm fat containing lesion as well as
another 9 mm fat containing lesion in the L4 vertebral body abutting the
pedicle are compatible with vertebral body hemangiomas.  There is no epidural
collection.

The terminal spinal cord is normal in signal and morphology.  The conus
medullaris terminates of the L1 level.

There is loss of intervertebral disc height and signal at all levels of the
lumbar spine.

T11-T12:  There is moderate intervertebral disc space narrowing with posterior
disc bulge and small nonspecific intervertebral disc fluid.  There are tiny
bilateral Tarlov cysts.  Mainly facet hypertrophy causes minimal bilateral
neural foraminal narrowing.  Small anterior osteophyte is noted.

T12-L1:  There is mild narrowing of the intervertebral disc space with mild
posterior disc bulge indenting the ventral thecal sac.  This in combination
with facet hypertrophy causes mild bilateral neural foraminal narrowing. 
Anterior osteophytes are noted.

L1-L2:  There is a mild posterior disc bulge in conjunction with facet
hypertrophy causing mild bilateral neural foraminal narrowing.

L2-L3:  A combination of facet hypertrophy and changes from the L3 compression
fracture cause moderate bilateral neural foraminal narrowing.

L3-L4:  Bony retropulsion from the L3 vertebral body fracture focally contacts
the traversing nerve roots.  Deformation from the fracture along with mild
posterior disc bulge with focal foraminal protrusion on the right causes
moderate to severe right greater than left neural foraminal narrowing.

L4-L5:  There is grade 1 anterolisthesis of L4 on L5 with a small posterior
disc bulge which in conjunction with facet hypertrophy causes moderate left
and mild right neural foraminal narrowing.  With ligamentum flavum hypertrophy
of this level, there is mild canal stenosis.

L5-S1:  Vertebral disc space is relatively well preserved.  Facet hypertrophy
causes minimal bilateral neural foraminal narrowing.  There is no spinal canal
stenosis.

 Within the limits of this noncontrast study there is no paravertebral or
paraspinal mass identified.  Nonspecific marrow heterogeneity of the right
greater than left sacroiliac joints are noted suggestive of degenerative
changes (see 05:41).

IMPRESSION:


1. Study is mildly degraded by motion.
2. Chronic 80% L3 vertebral body compression deformity with 9 mm bony
retropulsion resulting in moderate vertebral canal stenosis and contacting
traversing L4 nerve roots, new since ___, progressed since ___.
3. Multilevel degenerative changes, as described above.

nature on CT and air within the endometrial cavity on todays examination are

Loops of peristalsing normal small bowel are seen deep to the anterior

Findings were conveyed to the referring clinician, Dr. ___ our
clinical suspicion of an improving right tubo-ovarian abscess.

STUDY:  MRI of the head with and without contrast.

CLINICAL INDICATION:  ___ woman with history of cervical cancer with
metastatic disease, now with DLBCL, status treatment and remission, who has
fall with subdural hematoma, now with confusion, nausea, headache, neck
stiffness, evaluate for tumor recurrence/metastases or acute intracranial
process.

COMPARISON:  Prior CT head examinations, the most recent ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Pre-contrast axial and sagittal T1-weighted sequences were
obtained, axial FLAIR, axial T2, axial magnetic susceptibility, and axial
diffusion-weighted sequences.  The T1-weighted images were repeated after
administration of gadolinium contrast in axial T1, sagittal MP-RAGE, and
multiplanar reconstructions were provided.

FINDINGS:  The low attenuation areas demonstrated on the prior head CT,
represent vasogenic edema, extending along the periventricular region, corpus
callosum, basal ganglia, and involving the midbrain and pons as demonstrated
on the axial FLAIR sequences.  Additionally, multiple areas with abnormal
enhancement are identified involving the right temporal lobe, right cerebral
peduncle, bilateral basal ganglia including the caudate nucleus.  On the
diffusion-weighted sequence, these areas demonstrate a combination of
restricted diffusion and T2 shining-through effect.  There is no evidence of
acute hydrocephalus and the sulci are maintained as well as the
perimesencephalic cisterns.  The major vascular flow voids are patent, the
